
## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Device Approval Workflow (FDA 510(k))

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Man as Manufacturer
    participant NB as Notified Body or FDA
    participant Lab as Test Laboratory
    participant Reg as Regulator Decision
    Man->>Man: Step 1 Design Controls per 21 CFR 820
    Man->>Lab: Step 2 Send Device for Testing
    Lab->>Man: Step 3 Return Test Reports
    Man->>Man: Step 4 Prepare 510k Submission
    Man->>NB: Step 5 Submit via eSTAR Portal
    NB->>Man: Step 6 Acknowledge Receipt
    NB->>NB: Step 7 Substantial Equivalence Review
    alt Substantially Equivalent
        NB->>Reg: Step 8A Issue Clearance Letter
        Reg->>Man: Step 9A Marketing Authorization Granted
    else Not Substantially Equivalent
        NB->>Man: Step 8B Issue Not Substantially Equivalent Letter
        NB->>Man: Step 9B Request Additional Information or De Novo
        Man->>NB: Step 10B Resubmit with Additional Data
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Medical Device Regulation. List the three primary reasons why medical device regulation is necessary. [3 Marks]
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Medical device regulation refers to the legal and administrative framework established by governmental authorities (FDA, EU, CDSCO) to control the design, manufacture, distribution, and post-market monitoring of medical devices, ensuring they are **safe, effective, and of appropriate quality** for their intended use.

**Three Primary Reasons (2 marks):**

1. **Patient Safety Protection** — Medical devices directly interact with the human body. Failure can cause serious injury or death. Regulation enforces safety through design controls, testing, and clinical evaluation.
2. **Quality and Performance Assurance** — Regulation mandates Quality Management Systems (ISO 13485) and process validation, ensuring consistent device performance across production batches.
3. **Market Integrity and Information Asymmetry Correction** — Patients and clinicians cannot independently verify a device's safety. Regulation bridges this information gap through independent assessment by regulators and notified bodies.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Regulation and Compliance in the context of medical devices. [3 Marks]
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Sample QP 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Regulation | Compliance |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | The legal framework, rules, and standards established by authorities (e.g., FDA, EU MDR, CDSCO) governing medical devices. | The act of meeting, adhering to, and demonstrating conformity with those regulations throughout the product lifecycle. |
| **Nature (1 mark)** | Static — set by regulators; applies uniformly to all manufacturers. | Dynamic — varies based on each manufacturer's actions, processes, and documentation. |
| **Example (1 mark)** | EU MDR 2017/745, Article 10 mandates manufacturers to have a QMS in place. | A manufacturer implementing ISO 13485-certified QMS, conducting internal audits, and maintaining a Technical File to satisfy Article 10. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **risk-based classification system** for medical devices as per EU MDR 2017/745. Discuss the classification criteria and the conformity assessment routes for each class. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step process of addressing regulatory compliance** for a Class III implantable cardiac pacemaker intended for the European market. Include reference to relevant standards. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) Solution — Risk-Based Classification System [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The EU MDR 2017/745, Annex VIII, classifies medical devices into **four risk classes — I, IIa, IIb, and III** — based on the device's intended purpose, duration of use, invasiveness, and impact on the central circulatory/nervous system.

**Classification Rules (3 marks):**

| Rule Category | Examples | Resulting Class |
|---------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Non-invasive devices** (Rule 1–4) | Wheelchairs, stethoscopes, examination gloves | Class I |
| **Invasive devices** (Rule 5–8) | Surgical gloves, contact lenses, hypodermic needles | Class IIa or IIb |
| **Active devices** (Rule 9–13) | Diagnostic ultrasound, MRI, infusion pumps | Class IIa or IIb |
| **Special category devices** (Rule 14–22) | Devices in contact with CNS, heart, blood | **Class III** |
| **Duration modifiers** | Transient (≤60 min), Short-term (≤30 days), Long-term (>30 days) | Higher class for longer duration |

**Conformity Assessment Routes (2 marks):**

| Class | Conformity Assessment Route |
|-------|-----------------------------|
| Class I (non-sterile, non-measuring) | Self-declaration by manufacturer (Annex VIII) |
| Class I (sterile, measuring, surgical) | Self-declaration + Notified Body for relevant aspects |
| Class IIa | Annex IX (QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment) or Annex X + XI |
| Class IIb | Annex IX (QMS + Type Examination) or Annex X + XI |
| **Class III** | **Annex IX (QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment) OR Annex X (Type Examination) + Annex XI (Production QMS)** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Higher risk classes require progressively more rigorous conformity assessment, including independent third-party (Notified Body) involvement, comprehensive clinical evaluation, and post-market surveillance.

---

##### Part (b) Solution — Class III Pacemaker Regulatory Pathway [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Risk Classification (1 mark):**
A pacemaker is an **active implantable medical device (AIMD)** in contact with the **central circulatory system** and **life-supporting**. Per Rule 8 and Rule 22 of MDR Annex VIII, it is classified as **Class III** — the highest risk category.

**Step 2: Quality Management System Implementation (1 mark):**
Implement **ISO 13485:2016**-certified QMS covering design controls, document control, CAPA, management review, and supplier management.

**Step 3: Risk Management File (1 mark):**
Develop a complete **Risk Management File per ISO 14971:2019** including:
- Risk Management Plan
- Risk Analysis (FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
- Risk Evaluation Matrix
- Risk Control Measures
- Residual Risk Evaluation
- Risk Management Report

**Step 4: Clinical Evaluation (1 mark):**
Prepare a **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** per **MEDDEV 2.7/1 Rev 4** and MDR Article 61, including:
- Literature review
- Clinical investigation data (mandatory for Class III implantables — MDR Annex XV)
- Post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF) plan

**Step 5: Biocompatibility and Pre-clinical Testing (1 mark):**
Conduct tests per **ISO 10993** series:
- Cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5)
- Sensitization (ISO 10993-10)
- Irritation (ISO 10993-23)
- Systemic toxicity
- The device must also pass **bench testing, electrical safety (IEC 60601-1)**, and **EMC testing (IEC 60601-1-2)**.

**Step 6: Technical Documentation & Notified Body Submission (1 mark):**
Compile the **Technical File** (Annex II) and **Declaration of Conformity** (Annex IV). Submit to a **Notified Body** (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI) for conformity assessment under **Annex IX** or **Annex X + XI**.

**Step 7: CE Marking, UDI, EUDAMED Registration & Post-Market Surveillance (1 mark):**
- Affix **CE Mark** on the device.
- Assign **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** per **MDR Article 27**.
- Register the device in **EUDAMED** database.
- Implement **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** plan, **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)**, and **Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the **global regulatory landscape** for medical devices. Compare the regulatory approaches of the **US FDA, EU MDR, and India (CDSCO)**, highlighting similarities and differences. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **"Addressing the Compliance Need"** in the medical device industry. With a suitable example, illustrate how a non-compliance event can lead to severe financial, legal, and reputational consequences. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) Solution — Global Regulatory Landscape [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Medical device regulation has become a global discipline, with major jurisdictions including the **United States (FDA)**, **European Union (MDR/IVDR)**, and **India (CDSCO under Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)**.

**United States — FDA (2 marks):**
- Governed by **21 CFR Parts 800–1299** and the **Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act**.
- Classification: **Class I, II, III** based on risk.
- Pathways: **510(k) Premarket Notification** (Class II), **Premarket Approval (PMA)** (Class III), **De Novo** (novel low-to-moderate risk), **Emergency Use Authorization (EUA)**.
- Strong emphasis on **post-market surveillance** (MAUDE database, MDR — Medical Device Reporting).

**European Union — EU MDR 2017/745 (2 marks):**
- Replaced MDD (93/42/EEC) in May 2021.
- Classification: **I, IIa, IIb, III** with subdivisions (Is, Im, Ir).
- Mandatory involvement of **Notified Bodies** for Class IIa, IIb, III.
- Requires **Clinical Evaluation**, **PMCF**, **SSCP**, and **EUDAMED** registration.
- UDI system fully integrated.

**India — CDSCO (1.5 marks):**
- Governed by the **Medical Device Rules, 2017** (under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940).
- Classification: **Class A (low), B (low-moderate), C (moderate-high), D (high)** — aligned with GHTF.
- Central licensing authority (CDSCO) for Class C and D; state licensing for Class A and B.
- Requires **Plant Master File, Device Master File, ISO 13485** certification.

**Comparison Table (0.5 mark):**

| Parameter | US FDA | EU MDR | India CDSCO |
|-----------|--------|--------|-------------|
| Number of Classes | 3 (I, II, III) | 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III) | 4 (A, B, C, D) |
| Submission Portal | eSTAR | EUDAMED | SUGAM |
| Clinical Trial | Required for high-risk | Required for Class III | Required for high-risk |
| QMS Standard | 21 CFR 820 / ISO 13485 | ISO 13485 | ISO 13485 (mandatory) |
| Time to Approval (Class III) | 12–18 months | 6–12 months | 6–9 months |

---

##### Part (b) Solution — Compliance Need with Example [7 Marks]

**Concept of Compliance Need (2 marks):**
"Addressing the compliance need" means identifying applicable regulatory requirements and implementing systems, processes, and documentation to ensure ongoing conformity. Compliance is **proactive, continuous, and lifecycle-spanning** — it is not merely obtaining a license but maintaining conformity through design changes, production, and post-market activities.

**The Pillars of Compliance Need (1 mark):**
1. **Patient Safety** — primary ethical obligation
2. **Legal Obligation** — non-compliance attracts fines, recalls, imprisonment
3. **Market Access** — only compliant devices can be sold
4. **Reputation** — trust is the foundation of the medical device industry
5. **Financial Sustainability** — proactive compliance is cheaper than reactive remediation

**Real-World Case Study: DePuy ASR Hip Implant Recall (2010) (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **Device** | DePuy ASR XL Acetabular Cup System (metal-on-metal hip implant) |
| **Compliance Failure** | Inadequate post-market surveillance; failure to act on early adverse event reports; design and biocompatibility issues with metal debris |
| **Regulatory Action** | Global recall by parent company Johnson & Johnson |
| **Patient Impact** | Over 93,000 implants recalled; thousands required revision surgery |
| **Financial Cost** | Estimated **$2.5+ billion** in legal settlements, recall costs, and verdicts |
| **Reputational Impact** | Long-term damage to J&J brand; loss of clinician and patient trust; numerous lawsuits and class-action suits |

**Key Lesson (1 mark):**
Had DePuy proactively implemented robust post-market surveillance, clinical follow-up, and timely CAPA, the catastrophic consequences could have been avoided. **Compliance is not a cost — it is an investment in sustainability.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Regulation" with "Compliance"** — Regulation is the *rulebook*; compliance is the *action of following it*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Listing standards without explaining their purpose** — Writing "ISO 13485" without stating it is the **Quality Management System standard for medical devices** loses marks. Always state **what** the standard is and **why** it applies.
> 3. **Missing the lifecycle concept** — Compliance is **not a one-time event**. Students who write only about pre-market approval and ignore post-market surveillance will lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Incorrect class examples** — Saying "syringe is Class III" is wrong (it's Class IIa in EU MDR). Always verify class examples.
> 5. **Skipping the Notified Body role** — For EU MDR questions, the **role of the Notified Body** is mandatory to mention.
> 6. **No mention of patient safety** — Every regulatory question must ultimately tie back to **patient safety** as the rationale.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Regulation** = rules set by authorities; **Compliance** = manufacturer's adherence to those rules.
- ✅ Medical devices are regulated based on **risk to the patient**, not complexity.
- ✅ The three main regulatory philosophies: **Pre-market approval (FDA PMA)**, **Conformity assessment (EU MDR)**, **Licensing (CDSCO)**.
- ✅ Risk classification determines **scrutiny level**: I → IIa → IIb → III (increasing).
- ✅ Key standards: **ISO 13485 (QMS)**, **ISO 14971 (Risk Management)**, **ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility)**, **IEC 60601 (Electrical Safety)**.
- ✅ Compliance is a **lifecycle activity** — from design → market → post-market.
- ✅ **Notified Body** involvement is mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices in the EU.
- ✅ **Clinical Evaluation** is mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, III devices (MDR Article 61).
- ✅ **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**, **PSUR**, **PMCF**, and **SSCP** are mandatory for Class III.
- ✅ **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** is mandatory globally — assigned to every device.
- ✅ **EUDAMED** is the central EU database for medical device registration.
- ✅ The benefit-risk balance: **Device benefit must exceed its residual risk** (B/R ≥ 1).
- ✅ **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Actions)** is the backbone of post-market compliance.
- ✅ **Cost of non-compliance** can be **100x to 500x** the cost of proactive compliance.
- ✅ **Asymmetric information problem** is the fundamental economic reason for regulation.
- ✅ Regulation ultimately serves **patient safety**, which is the **non-negotiable** foundation of the medical device industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> For any "addressing regulation and compliance" question, structure your answer in this order: **(1) Why needed → (2) What are the rules → (3) How to comply → (4) Real-world implication.** Examiners reward structured, logical answers with clear headings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical device design control process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medical device design control process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (FDA 21 CFR 820.30 & ISO 13485:2016)

**Design Control** is a structured, documented set of interrelated procedures and practices incorporated into the design and development process that systematically ensures that:

1. User needs and intended uses are translated into明确的 (clear) design requirements.
2. The design is verified against those requirements.
3. The final device is validated against user needs and intended use.
4. All design activities are planned, reviewed, documented, and traceable throughout the product lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Anchor Definition (FDA 21 CFR 820.30):**
> *"Each manufacturer of any class III or class II device, and the class I devices listed in paragraph (a)(2) of this section, shall establish and maintain procedures to control the design of the device in order to ensure that specified design requirements are met."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope of Applicability:**
> Design controls are **mandatory** for all **Class II** and **Class III** medical devices (and certain Class I devices such as automated blood cell separators and surgical gloves). For Class I devices not on the mandatory list, design controls remain a strongly recommended best practice and are required for the design of any **Class II** accessories or components of higher-class devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "Building a Custom House"

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to design a custom house for your family. You would **never** accept a house where the architect simply started laying bricks without a plan. Instead, you would insist on the following steps:

| House-Building Step | Equivalent Design Control Element |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| You tell the architect: "We need 3 bedrooms, a south-facing kitchen, and wheelchair accessibility." | **Design Input** (user needs translated into design requirements) |
| The architect produces blueprints, structural calculations, and a Bill of Quantities (BoQ). | **Design Output** (drawings, specs, software code) |
| An independent engineer checks: "Are the pillars strong enough for the given load?" | **Design Verification** ("Did we build the product **right**?") |
| You walk through the finished house and say: "Yes, this matches what my family needed." | **Design Validation** ("Did we build the **right** product?") |
| The builder trains the plumbers and electricians to construct the house from the plans. | **Design Transfer** (moving design into production) |
| The architect keeps a complete project file with all approvals, drawings, and correspondence. | **Design History File (DHF)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Design controls are *not* a one-time event — they are a **continuous, iterative, and closed-loop quality system** that runs from the first sketch of a device until the day it is discontinued from the market.

## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology Glossary

The following terms are drawn directly from **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3** and **21 CFR 820.30** — memorize these for KTU Part A questions.

- **Design Input (DI):** The physical and performance requirements of a device that are used as a basis for device design (raw user needs are converted into objective, testable engineering requirements).
- **Design Output (DO):** The results of a design effort at each design phase and at the end of the total design effort (e.g., drawings, specifications, software source code).
- **Design Review:** A documented, comprehensive, systematic examination of a design to evaluate the adequacy of the design requirements, to evaluate the capability of the design to meet these requirements, and to identify problems.
- **Design Verification (V&V - V):** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that design output meets design input requirements. ("Are we building the product **right**?")
- **Design Validation (V&V - V):** Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the device conforms to user needs and intended use. ("Are we building the **right** product?")
- **Design Transfer:** The process of transferring the device design into production specifications for manufacturing.
- **Design Change:** Any change to design inputs, outputs, or controls after the original design has been approved.
- **Design History File (DHF):** A compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device, serving as the regulatory "proof" of proper design control execution.
- **Risk Management File (RMF):** Per **ISO 14971**, the set of all documents produced during risk management activities. It is intrinsically linked to the DHF.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently confuse *Verification* with *Validation*. The mnemonic is:
> - **Verification** = "**V**erify the **S**pecs" (Did the drawing match the requirement document?)
> - **Validation** = "**V**alidate the **U**se" (Did the finished device actually work for the surgeon/nurse/patient?)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight (8) Pillars of Design Control (21 CFR 820.30)

The FDA's Quality System Regulation enumerates the following **eight inter-locking elements** of a compliant design control system. Examiners frequently test whether the student can list and describe each element with a real example.

### 2.1.1 Design and Development Planning (820.30(a))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain a **Design and Development Plan** that describes the design and development activities and defines responsibility for their implementation.
- The plan must be reviewed, updated, and approved as design and development evolves.
- **Why it matters:** Provides a **roadmap** and assigns accountability. A typical plan includes: activities, deliverables, responsibilities, timelines, resources, and review/approval criteria.

### 2.1.2 Design Input (820.30(c))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain a procedure to ensure that the design requirements relating to a device are appropriate and address the intended use of the device.
- Design inputs must be **objective, measurable, and unambiguous**. They must be documented and reviewed/approved before release.
- **Why it matters:** Ambiguous inputs ("the device should be fast") lead to non-conforming products. Good inputs are testable ("the device shall achieve 30 frames per second at 1280×720 resolution").

### 2.1.3 Design Output (820.30(d))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures for defining and documenting design output in terms that allow an adequate evaluation of conformance to design input requirements.
- Design output must be expressed in terms that can be **verified** and **validated** against design input requirements.
- **Examples:** Engineering drawings, component specifications, software code, manufacturing instructions, labeling, packaging specifications, sterilization instructions.

### 2.1.4 Design Review (820.30(e))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures to ensure that formal documented reviews of the design results are planned and conducted at appropriate stages of the device's design and development.
- Participants should include **independent reviewers** not directly responsible for the design stage being reviewed.
- **Frequency:** Typically conducted at the end of each design phase (Concept, Feasibility, Detailed Design, Process Validation).

### 2.1.5 Design Verification (820.30(f))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures for verifying the device design.
- Verification confirms that **design output meets design input** requirements.
- **Methods:** Inspections, tests, analyses, demonstrations, and comparison with similar devices.
- **Example:** For a syringe specified to deliver 1 mL ± 0.05 mL, perform a gravimetric test on 30 units to confirm accuracy.

### 2.1.6 Design Validation (820.30(g))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures for validating the device design.
- Validation must be performed on **production-equivalent units** under **simulated or actual use conditions**.
- It must ensure that devices conform to defined **user needs and intended use**.
- **Example:** Usability study with 15 surgeons using a new laparoscopic tool in a simulated operating room to confirm ease of use, ergonomics, and effectiveness.

### 2.1.7 Design Transfer (820.30(h))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures to ensure that the device design is correctly translated into production specifications.
- The procedure must ensure that production capability can meet design specifications before transferring the design.
- **Why it matters:** Many "lab-only" prototypes cannot be reliably manufactured at scale. Design transfer ensures **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)**.

### 2.1.8 Design Changes (820.30(i))
- Each manufacturer must establish and maintain procedures for the identification, documentation, validation (where appropriate), review, and approval of design changes before their implementation.
- All design changes must be **re-verified and re-validated** to the extent necessary to ensure compliance with the original design input requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> The Design History File (DHF) is **not** a separate pillar but rather the **compilation record** that captures the evidence from all 8 elements above. It is mandated under **21 CFR 820.30(j)**.

## 2.2 The "V-Model" of Design Control

The industry-standard visualization of design control is the **V-Model** (also called the V&V Model). The left arm represents **decomposition** (User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs), and the right arm represents **integration/evaluation** (Unit Verification → Integration Verification → System Validation → User Validation).

The bottom of the V represents **Design Transfer** and the apex represents the **Design Input/User Need**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important revision artifact** for this topic. Memorize it for Part A questions.

| # | Element | Key Question Answered | Document Type | Responsible Party |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Design Plan** | "What, when, who?" | Project Plan, Gantt Chart | Project Manager / RA |
| 2 | **Design Input** | "What must the device do?" | Requirements Specification | Engineering + Marketing + Clinician |
| 3 | **Design Output** | "How is it built?" | Drawings, Code, BOM, Labeling | Engineering |
| 4 | **Design Review** | "Is the design sound so far?" | Review Report, Meeting Minutes | Cross-functional team |
| 5 | **Verification** | "Did we build the product right?" | Test Reports, Inspection Records | Engineering + QA |
| 6 | **Validation** | "Did we build the right product?" | Usability Studies, Clinical Evaluation | Engineering + Clinical + QA |
| 7 | **Design Transfer** | "Can we build it consistently?" | DMR, Production Procedures | Manufacturing + Engineering + QA |
| 8 | **Design Changes** | "What if we need to modify?" | ECO, Impact Analysis | Engineering + QA + RA |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Design controls are not academic bureaucracy — they provide:
1. **Traceability:** A defect found post-market can be traced back to a specific design input, output, test, and review.
2. **Regulatory Defensibility:** A complete DHF is the manufacturer's best defense in an FDA audit, EU Notified Body audit, or product liability lawsuit.
3. **Knowledge Preservation:** Engineer turnover does not destroy institutional knowledge if the DHF is properly maintained.
4. **Efficiency:** Catching design flaws at the "input" stage is **100x cheaper** than catching them at the post-market stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Design Control Traceability Matrix (DCTM)

The **Design Control Traceability Matrix** is the analytical tool that links every User Need → Design Input → Design Output → Verification → Validation activity. It is the single most valuable "derivation" in design control because it forces a **closed-loop link** between every element.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Construction of a Traceability Matrix

**Step 1: List the User Needs (UNs).**
These come from Voice of Customer (VoC), clinical literature, regulatory requirements, and the marketing Input Requirements Document (IRD).
*Example:* UN-001: The surgeon needs a laparoscopic grasper with a 360° rotational wrist to access difficult anatomical angles.

**Step 2: Translate UNs into Design Inputs (DIs).**
DIs must be objective and measurable. Use the SMART framework (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
*Example:* DI-001: The device distal tip shall achieve continuous rotation of 360° ± 5° under a maximum applied torque of 2.5 N·m.

**Step 3: Define Design Outputs (DOs) that satisfy each DI.**
DOs are the physical/functional artifacts.
*Example:* DO-001: Engineering drawing DWG-LG-101 Rev C, specifying the rotation mechanism tolerances.

**Step 4: Define Verification (V) activities for each DO.**
V proves the output meets the input.
*Example:* V-001: Bench test protocol TP-001, measuring rotation accuracy with a calibrated protractor and torque gauge on 10 units.

**Step 5: Define Validation (Va) activities that confirm the UN is met.**
Va proves the user's need is satisfied in a realistic environment.
*Example:* Va-001: Usability study with 15 surgeons performing simulated laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a porcine lab.

**Step 6: Build the matrix linking UN → DI → DO → V → Va.**

### 3.1.2 Sample Traceability Matrix (Excerpt)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Linkage} &: \text{UN} \rightarrow \text{DI} \rightarrow \text{DO} \rightarrow \text{V} \rightarrow \text{Va} \\
\text{Logic} &: \text{Need} \Rightarrow \text{Spec} \Rightarrow \text{Artifact} \Rightarrow \text{Spec-Met} \Rightarrow \text{Need-Met} \\
\text{Equation of Traceability} &: \text{Traceability} = \frac{\text{Number of Linked Items}}{\text{Total Number of Items}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For an audit-perfect DHF, the Traceability score should equal 1.0 (100%)** for all critical user needs. Any "orphaned" DI (one without a V or Va) or any "untraceable" V (one without a linked DI) is an **audit finding** (Form 483 observation in FDA terminology).

| UN-ID | UN Description | DI-ID | DI Specification | DO-ID | DO Document | V-ID | V Test | Va-ID | Va Test |
|-------|---------------|-------|------------------|-------|-------------|------|--------|-------|---------|
| UN-001 | 360° wrist rotation | DI-001 | 360° ± 5° rotation, 2.5 N·m torque | DO-001 | DWG-LG-101 Rev C | V-001 | TP-001 Bench Test | Va-001 | Surgeon Usability Study |
| UN-002 | Ergonomic single-hand grip | DI-002 | Grip diameter 18 mm ± 1 mm, weight < 350 g | DO-002 | DWG-LG-102 Rev B | V-002 | TP-002 Ergonomic Test | Va-002 | Surgeon Survey (NASA-TLX) |
| UN-003 | Sterilizable via autoclave | DI-003 | Withstand 134 °C / 18 min for 50 cycles | DO-003 | Material Spec MS-014 | V-003 | TP-003 Cycle Test | Va-003 | Hospital Sterilization Protocol Validation |

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Derivation of the "Cost of Design Defect"

Design control is justified economically by the **Rule of Ten**, a well-known concept in quality engineering. Each phase of detection is **10x more expensive** than the previous one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{Concept}} &= C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Design Input}} &= 10 \cdot C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Design Output}} &= 10^2 \cdot C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Verification}} &= 10^3 \cdot C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Validation}} &= 10^4 \cdot C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Production}} &= 10^5 \cdot C_0 \\
\text{Cost}_{\text{Post-Market}} &= 10^6 \cdot C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A defect caught at the user-need stage may cost **₹10,000** to fix; the same defect caught after the device is in the market (recall, litigation, patient harm) may cost **₹100 Crore or more**. This is why design control is not optional — it is **financial survival engineering**.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: V vs. Va

The following matrix distinguishes Verification from Validation along **five orthogonal axes**. KTU examiners love to ask "Differentiate between V and Va" — use this matrix in your answer.

| Axis | Verification (V) | Validation (Va) |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Question** | "Are we building the product right?" | "Are we building the right product?" |
| **Reference Standard** | Design Inputs (Specifications) | User Needs + Intended Use |
| **Stage** | Typically earlier (sub-system, system) | Typically later (final product, clinical) |
| **Environment** | Controlled lab / test bench | Simulated or actual use environment |
| **Test Article** | Engineering prototypes, pre-production units | Production-equivalent units |
| **Activity Type** | Inspections, tests, analyses | Clinical evaluation, usability studies, animal studies |
| **Documentation** | Test reports with measured vs. spec values | Validation reports with user satisfaction and clinical outcomes |
| **Frequency in Lifecycle** | Many (one per DI) | Fewer (one per major user need) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Design Control V-Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["User Needs and Intended Use"]
    B["Design Inputs - Requirements Specification"]
    C["Design Outputs - Drawings, Code, Specs"]
    D["Design Review at Each Phase"]
    E["Design Transfer into Production"]
    F["Production and Post Market"]

    G["Design Verification - Did we build the product RIGHT"]
    H["Design Validation - Did we build the RIGHT product"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F

    B -.-> G
    G -.-> C

    C -.-> H
    H -.-> A

    classDef phase fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef verify fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    classDef transfer fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300

    class A,B,C phase
    class D verify
    class E,F transfer
    class G,H verify
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **solid arrows** (→) show the *progressive* design flow from User Needs down to Production.
> - The **dashed arrows** (-.->) show the *feedback/evaluation* loops of Verification and Validation that close back against Design Inputs and User Needs, respectively.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Design History File (DHF)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER["Stage 1: User Research"]
        UN["User Needs Document UND"]
        IRD["Input Requirements Document IRD"]
    end

    subgraph PLAN["Stage 2: Design Planning"]
        DDP["Design and Development Plan DDP"]
        RM["Risk Management Plan per ISO 14971"]
    end

    subgraph DI["Stage 3: Design Input"]
        DIS["Design Input Specification DIS"]
        DR1["Design Review Report DRR 1"]
    end

    subgraph DO["Stage 4: Design Output"]
        DOS["Design Output Documents"]
        SW["Software Source Code"]
        LBL["Labeling and IFU"]
    end

    subgraph VER["Stage 5: Verification"]
        VR["Verification Test Protocols and Reports"]
        ITR["Inspection and Test Records"]
    end

    subgraph VAL["Stage 6: Validation"]
        US["Usability Study Report"]
        CE["Clinical Evaluation Report CER"]
    end

    subgraph TRANS["Stage 7: Design Transfer"]
        DMR["Device Master Record DMR"]
        DFI["Design for Manufacturability Report"]
    end

    subgraph CHANGE["Stage 8: Design Change Control"]
        ECO["Engineering Change Order ECO"]
        IA["Impact Analysis Report"]
    end

    subgraph DHF["Design History File DHF"]
        DHFF["Complete Regulatory Evidence Package"]
    end

    USER --> PLAN
    PLAN --> DI
    DI --> DO
    DO --> VER
    VER --> VAL
    VAL --> TRANS
    TRANS --> CHANGE
    CHANGE --> DHF

    UN --> DHFF
    IRD --> DHFF
    DDP --> DHFF
    RM --> DHFF
    DIS --> DHFF
    DR1 --> DHFF
    DOS --> DHFF
    SW --> DHFF
    LBL --> DHFF
    VR --> DHFF
    ITR --> DHFF
    US --> DHFF
    CE --> DHFF
    DMR --> DHFF
    DFI --> DHFF
    ECO --> DHFF
    IA --> DHFF

    classDef stageStyle fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px
    classDef dhfStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px
    class USER,PLAN,DI,DO,VER,VAL,TRANS,CHANGE,DHF stageStyle
    class DHFF dhfStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question asks "List the contents of the Design History File," use the 8-stage decomposition shown above. The DHF is a **folder/dossier**, not a single document.

## 4.3 The Verification vs. Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["User Need: 360 degree wrist rotation"]
    P2["Design Input: 360 plus or minus 5 degree rotation"]
    P3["Design Output: Engineering drawing of rotation mechanism"]

    Q1["Verification: Bench test of rotation accuracy with protractor and torque gauge on 10 units"]
    Q2["Validation: Surgeon uses device in simulated laparoscopic surgery and confirms adequate rotation"]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> Q1
    Q1 -.confirms.- P2
    P2 --> Q2
    Q2 -.confirms.- P1

    classDef need fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3 need
    class Q1,Q2 test
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between Design Verification and Design Validation. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Design Verification | Design Validation |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Confirmation that **design output meets design input** requirements. | Confirmation that the device **meets user needs and intended use**. |
| **Key Question** | "Are we building the product **right**?" | "Are we building the **right** product?" |
| **Reference** | Compared against the **Design Input Specification**. | Compared against **User Needs and Intended Use**. |
| **Stage** | Performed at sub-system and system levels (typically earlier). | Performed on the finished, production-equivalent device (typically later). |
| **Activity** | Bench testing, inspection, dimensional checks, code reviews. | Clinical evaluation, usability studies, animal studies, simulated-use testing. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write a single-line answer like "Verification is testing and validation is also testing." This will fetch **at most 1 mark out of 3**. You must use a **tabular or 3-point structure** to score full marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
**What is a Design History File (DHF)? List any four documents it contains. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Design History File (DHF) is a **regulatory compilation of all records** that documents the design history of a finished device, as required by 21 CFR 820.30(j). It serves as objective evidence that the device was developed in accordance with the approved design plan and procedures.

**Four Documents (2 Marks):**

1. **Design and Development Plan** – the project roadmap.
2. **Design Input Requirements Document** – the engineering specifications.
3. **Design Output Documents** – drawings, BOM, software code.
4. **Design Verification and Validation Reports** – test protocols and results.
5. *(Any other four are acceptable: Risk Management File, Design Review Reports, Design Transfer Records, Engineering Change Orders, Labeling/IFU, Clinical Evaluation Report).*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice between two full-length questions. Each typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **eight elements of Design Control** as mandated by 21 CFR 820.30. For each element, give one document type that evidences its execution.

**Model Answer:**

The FDA's Quality System Regulation, 21 CFR 820.30, mandates eight inter-locking elements of design control. Each is explained below with its primary document evidence.

1. **Design and Development Planning (820.30(a))** – A formal plan must be established identifying activities, deliverables, responsibilities, resources, and review/approval criteria. **Evidence:** *Design and Development Plan (DDP) document.*

2. **Design Input (820.30(c))** – Physical, performance, and regulatory requirements for the device, including intended use and user needs. Inputs must be objective, measurable, and reviewed. **Evidence:** *Design Input Requirements Specification (DIRS).*

3. **Design Output (820.30(d))** – Defined, documented results of the design effort (drawings, software, specifications, labeling). **Evidence:** *Engineering Drawings, BOM, Software Source Code, Labeling Artwork.*

4. **Design Review (820.30(e))** – Formal, documented, systematic reviews of design results at appropriate stages by independent participants. **Evidence:** *Design Review Reports with meeting minutes and action items.*

5. **Design Verification (820.30(f))** – Confirmation by examination and objective evidence that design output meets design input. **Evidence:** *Verification Test Protocols and Reports (with measured data vs. spec).*

6. **Design Validation (820.30(g))** – Confirmation by examination and objective evidence that the device conforms to user needs and intended use under simulated/actual use. **Evidence:** *Validation Reports (e.g., Usability Study, Clinical Evaluation Report, Biocompatibility Report).*

7. **Design Transfer (820.30(h))** – Translation of the design into production specifications and ensuring production capability. **Evidence:** *Device Master Record (DMR), Design Transfer Protocol, First Article Inspection Records.*

8. **Design Changes (820.30(i))** – Documented, reviewed, approved, and verified/validated changes after original approval. **Evidence:** *Engineering Change Orders (ECOs) with Impact Analysis Reports.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memorization:** "**P**lease **I**nclude **O**utstanding **R**eviews, **V**erifications, **V**alidations, **T**ransfers, and **C**hanges." *(P-I-O-R-V-V-T-C)*

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks]:** A startup is developing a new **wearable continuous glucose monitor (CGM)** for diabetic patients. Construct a **Design Control Traceability Matrix** linking at least **3 user needs** to their design inputs, outputs, verification, and validation activities.

**Model Answer:**

| UN-ID | User Need | DI-ID | Design Input (SMART Spec) | DO-ID | Design Output | V-ID | Verification | Va-ID | Validation |
|-------|-----------|-------|---------------------------|-------|---------------|------|--------------|-------|------------|
| UN-001 | Patient needs continuous, painless glucose monitoring | DI-001 | Sensor shall measure glucose every 5 min with MARD ≤ 10% over 14 days | DO-001 | Electrochemical sensor spec SS-CGM-01; Firmware FW-CGM-02 | V-001 | Bench accuracy test per CLSI POCT12 on 30 sensors vs. YSI reference | Va-001 | Home-use study with 30 diabetic patients over 14 days |
| UN-002 | Device must be discreet and comfortable to wear | DI-002 | Patch dimensions ≤ 50 × 35 × 8 mm; weight ≤ 12 g; waterproof IPX7 | DO-002 | Mechanical drawing DWG-CGM-100; Material spec MS-014 | V-002 | Dimensional inspection on 20 units; IPX7 immersion test per IEC 60529 | Va-002 | Wearability survey using validated patient questionnaire (n=30) |
| UN-003 | Data must be securely transmitted to a caregiver app | DI-003 | Bluetooth LE 5.0 with AES-128 encryption; latency ≤ 2 s | DO-003 | Wireless protocol doc WP-CGM-03; Cybersecurity risk analysis per FDA 2023 | V-003 | RF performance test per FCC Part 15; penetration test on data flow | Va-003 | Simulated-use test: caregiver app receives alert within 2 s for 50 hypo events |

**Key Engineering Logic:**

- Each **UN** (subjective) is decomposed into a **DI** (objective, measurable).
- Each **DI** is realized by one or more **DOs** (physical/functional artifact).
- Each **DO/DI pair** is confirmed by a **Verification** test in a controlled setting.
- Each **UN** is confirmed by a **Validation** test in a realistic or actual-use environment.
- **Traceability Score = 1.0** — every critical user need is fully linked to a validation activity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to write V and Va activities that are **identical** (e.g., "tested on 10 patients" for both). To score full marks, V must be a **bench/lab test against a spec**, and Va must be a **simulated/actual use test against a user need**. Conflating the two loses **2-3 marks** per sub-question.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **V-Model of Design Control** with a neat block diagram. Explain how Verification and Validation are represented in the model.

**Model Answer:**

The **V-Model** is the industry-standard graphical representation of the design control process. It visualizes the relationship between the **decomposition phases** (left arm) and the **integration/evaluation phases** (right arm).

**Structure of the V-Model:**

```
           USER NEEDS / INTENDED USE
                       |
                DESIGN INPUTS
                  /         \
                 /           \
        CONCEPT   →   DETAILED   →  DESIGN OUTPUTS
        DESIGN        DESIGN
                 \           /
                  \         /
            SUB-SYSTEM    SYSTEM    →  PRODUCTION
            VERIFICATION  VERIFICATION  (Design Transfer)
                       \         /
                        \       /
                       DESIGN VALIDATION
                       (User Validation)
```

**Left Arm (Decomposition):**
- **User Needs and Intended Use** are at the top.
- These cascade down to **Design Inputs** (objective requirements).
- The design is then progressively decomposed into sub-systems, components, and ultimately **Design Outputs** (drawings, code, specs).

**Bottom of the V:**
- Represents **Design Transfer** into production.

**Right Arm (Integration and Evaluation):**
- **Design Verification** is performed at each level of integration (sub-system, system). It checks that the **output** of that level meets the **input** of that level.
- **Design Validation** is performed at the top of the right arm on the finished device. It confirms that the device meets the **user needs and intended use** that started the entire process.

**Key Insight:** The V-Model enforces the principle that **the depth and rigor of verification must match the depth and rigor of the corresponding design decomposition**. The two arms are linked by traceability, and the model closes the loop when validation confirms the original user needs.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Design and Development Plan (DDP)**. What are its minimum required contents per 21 CFR 820.30(a)?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
The Design and Development Plan (DDP) is a formal, controlled document that defines the **scope, objectives, activities, responsibilities, resources, and timelines** for the design and development of a specific medical device. It is the **master roadmap** for the entire project and must be established at the start of the design effort.

**Minimum Required Contents (per 820.30(a)):**

1. **Design and Development Activities** – The specific tasks required to bring the device from concept to market (e.g., requirements gathering, prototyping, testing, regulatory submission, transfer).
2. **Responsibility for Implementation** – Identification of personnel, departments, and external partners (e.g., design firms, test labs) responsible for each activity.
3. **Review and Approval Criteria** – The milestones, deliverables, and decision gates at which design reviews and management approvals are required.
4. **Interfaces Between Different Groups** – Coordination mechanisms between engineering, manufacturing, regulatory, quality, clinical, and marketing teams.
5. **Resource Requirements** – Budget, personnel, equipment, and facilities needed for each activity.
6. **Update and Revision Control** – A mechanism for reviewing and updating the plan as the project evolves (e.g., at each design review).

**Best-Practice Additions (beyond minimum compliance):**

- Risk-based prioritization of activities (per ISO 14971).
- Links to the Risk Management Plan and the regulatory submission strategy.
- Identification of design control deliverables to be filed in the DHF.
- Identification of applicable standards (e.g., IEC 60601, IEC 62304, ISO 10993).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often describe the DDP as "a list of activities" only. For full marks (7/7), the answer must cover **all six minimum elements** above and demonstrate understanding that the DDP is a **living document** that is updated throughout the project.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following bullets for last-minute exam revision:**

- ✅ **Design Control** is mandated by **21 CFR 820.30** (US) and **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3** (International).
- ✅ It is **mandatory for Class II and Class III devices** and certain Class I devices (e.g., automated blood cell separators, surgical gloves).
- ✅ The **eight elements** are: **P**lan, **I**nput, **O**utput, **R**eview, **V**erification, **V**alidation, **T**ransfer, **C**hange.
- ✅ **Verification** = "Did we build the product **right**?" (Output vs. Input, in a lab).
- ✅ **Validation** = "Did we build the **right** product?" (Device vs. User Need, in actual/simulated use).
- ✅ The **Design History File (DHF)** is the **compilation of all design records** that proves regulatory compliance (21 CFR 820.30(j)).
- ✅ The **Device Master Record (DMR)** contains the **production specifications** and is a separate document from the DHF.
- ✅ A **Design Control Traceability Matrix (DCTM)** links every User Need → Design Input → Design Output → Verification → Validation. **Audit-perfect DCTM has a traceability score of 1.0**.
- ✅ The **V-Model** is the industry-standard visualization; the right arm represents evaluation (V and Va) and the left arm represents decomposition (UN, DI, DO).
- ✅ The **Rule of Ten**: each stage of detection is **10x more expensive** than the previous one — design control is financial survival engineering.
- ✅ **Design Review** must include **independent participants** not directly responsible for the design stage being reviewed.
- ✅ **Design Transfer** is the gate at which production capability is confirmed before the device goes into commercial manufacturing.
- ✅ **Design Changes** must be documented, reviewed, approved, and **re-verified/re-validated** to the extent necessary to maintain compliance.
- ✅ **Risk Management** (per ISO 14971) is **integrated with design control**, not separate from it. The Risk Management File (RMF) is part of the DHF.
- ✅ **Usability Engineering** (per IEC 62366-1) and **Software Lifecycle** (per IEC 62304) are **specialized extensions** of design control for specific device types.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing - verification and validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Testing - verification and validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Verification** is the process of confirming that design and development outputs meet the design and development input requirements. It answers the question: *"Are we building the product right?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Validation** is the process of confirming that the resulting product meets the needs of the user and the intended use(s) in the intended use environment. It answers the question: *"Are we building the right product?"*

These two activities form the cornerstone of medical device product development and are explicitly mandated by:
- **ISO 13485:2016** – Clause 7.3 (Design and Development)
- **FDA 21 CFR §820.30** – Design Controls for Medical Devices
- **EU MDR 2017/745** – Annex I, General Safety and Performance Requirements
- **IEC 62366-1:2015** – Usability Engineering
- **ISO 14971:2019** – Risk Management

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Imagine a chef designing a new dish. **Verification** is when the chef tastes the dish against his *own written recipe* (was the salt exactly 5g? was it cooked for 10 minutes?). **Validation** is when the chef hands the dish to a *customer* and asks: *"Does this taste like the birthday meal you wanted?"*
>
> The first checks internal consistency (specification → output). The second checks fitness-for-use (output → user need).

**Key Distinction in One Line:**
- **Verification** = *Specification-driven testing* (Document check, code review, dimensional check, unit test).
- **Validation** = *Use-driven testing* (Clinical evaluation, usability test, simulated-use test, field trial).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Markers in V&V

- **Acceptance Criteria Limit (ACL):** $\le 95\%$ confidence that $<95\%$ of the population falls outside the specification limit (commonly used in **validation** sample-size justifications).
- **Test Coverage:** Ratio of executed test cases to total test cases.
- **Defect Density:** Number of defects per unit of design output.
- **Confidence Level:** Typically **95%** for medical device validation.
- **Sample Size (n):** Determined by statistical rationale or the formula based on the **binomial confidence interval**.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the unit of acceptance for **V&V documentation** is the **Design History File (DHF)**, which is the cumulative record of all design control evidence.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The V-Model of Design Verification & Validation

The V-Model is the most widely used engineering framework that links each design phase to its corresponding V&V activity.

| Left Side (Decomposition) | Connection | Right Side (Integration) |
|----------------------------|------------|--------------------------|
| User Needs & Intended Use | $\longleftrightarrow$ | **Validation** (Clinical / Usability) |
| Design Inputs (Specifications) | $\longleftrightarrow$ | **Integration / System Verification** |
| Design Outputs (Drawings, Code) | $\longleftrightarrow$ | **Subsystem Verification** |
| Detailed Module Design | $\longleftrightarrow$ | **Unit Verification** (Bench / Lab Tests) |

### 2.2 Hierarchy of V&V Activities

**Step 1: Unit-Level Verification**
- Performed on individual components or sub-assemblies.
- Confirms component meets its own specification.
- Example: Tensile test of a single suture material against ISO 6892.

**Step 2: Subsystem / Integration Verification**
- Tests interaction of components.
- Example: Pump + tubing set flow-rate verification.

**Step 3: System-Level Verification**
- Tests the fully integrated device against design inputs.
- Example: Complete infusion pump accuracy test per IEC 60601-2-24.

**Step 4: Design Validation**
- Performed on finished device under simulated or actual use conditions.
- Includes user population, use environment, and use scenarios.
- Example: Simulated-use study with nurses in a clinical environment.

**Step 5: Clinical Evaluation / Validation (where applicable)**
- Per **EU MDR Article 61** and **ISO 14155**, certain devices require clinical investigation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|--------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Statistical Sample Size (n) for Validation | $n = \frac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(p)}$ | $C$ = confidence, $p$ = reliability |
| One-sided 95% Lower Confidence | $L = p - z_{0.95}\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | $z_{0.95} = 1.645$ |
| Test Coverage | $C_{cov} = \frac{T_{exec}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Expressed as a **percentage** |
| Defect Density | $D_d = \frac{N_{defects}}{N_{units}}$ | Defects per unit |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\ge 1.33$ typical acceptance |
| Risk Priority Number (V&V trigger) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | FMEA-derived |
| Confidence Interval (Binomial) | $p \pm z\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Used for attribute data |
| Type I / Type II Error | $\alpha,\ \beta$ | $\alpha = 0.05$ (medical norm) |
| Power of Test | $1 - \beta$ | Should be $\ge 0.80$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The vertical pipe symbol is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Medical Device Engineering

- **FDA Inspections:** Investigators will trace a requirement in the Design Input (DI) to a Verification or Validation protocol, then to a test report. The **traceability matrix** is therefore a critical regulatory artifact.
- **Notified Body Audits (EU MDR):** V&V evidence forms the technical documentation under Annex II.
- **Post-Market:** V&V protocols must be re-executed or re-validated when **design changes** occur (per **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3.9**).

### 2.5 Key Differences — Verification vs. Validation

| Dimension | Verification | Validation |
|------------|--------------|------------|
| **Question** | Are we building the product **right**? | Are we building the **right** product? |
| **Driver** | Design Inputs / Specifications | User Needs / Intended Use |
| **Stage** | Throughout design & development | Late stage, on finished product |
| **Test Subject** | Components, subsystems, system | Production-equivalent device |
| **Environment** | Controlled lab / bench | Simulated / actual use environment |
| **Sample** | Engineering units (limited) | Production units (statistical justification) |
| **Output** | Verification Report | Validation Report |
| **Regulatory Anchor** | 21 CFR 820.30(f), ISO 13485 7.3.7 | 21 CFR 820.30(g), ISO 13485 7.3.8 |
| **Statistical Rigor** | Engineering judgment + standards | Statistical confidence (typically 95%) |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Statistical Sample Size for Validation

A common KTU 14-mark problem involves deriving the minimum sample size required to demonstrate, with 95% confidence, that at least **95% of the lot** of devices will pass the validation test.

**Given:**
- Required confidence level: $C = 0.95$
- Required reliability (zero-failure outcome): $p = 0.95$
- Number of allowed failures: $r = 0$

**Step 1 — State the relationship for attribute (pass/fail) testing:**

For a test with zero observed failures ($r = 0$), the probability of seeing no failures in $n$ samples, given true reliability $p$, is:

$$
P(\text{no failure}) = p^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Enforce the confidence constraint:**

We require that, with confidence $C$, the true reliability is at least $p$. Therefore the probability of seeing zero failures must equal the confidence level:

$$
p^{n} \le 1 - C
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$
\ln(p^{n}) \le \ln(1 - C)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power rule of logarithms:**

$$
n \cdot \ln(p) \le \ln(1 - C)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
n \ge \frac{\ln(1 - C)}{\ln(p)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
n \ge \frac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.95)}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the natural logarithms:**

$$
\ln(0.05) = -2.9957
$$
$$
\ln(0.95) = -0.0513
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the ratio:**

$$
n \ge \frac{-2.9957}{-0.0513} = 58.39
$$

**Step 9 — Round up to the next integer (statistical convention):**

$$
n = 59 \text{ samples}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** To demonstrate, with **95% confidence**, that the device will fail at a rate of no more than **5%** (i.e., reliability $\ge 95\%$), one must validate at least **59 production-equivalent units with zero failures**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — V&V Decision Flow

| Decision Trigger | Activity Type | Statistical Method | Document Output |
|------------------|---------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Single specification check | Verification | None (go/no-go) | Test Report |
| Multiple units, attribute | Verification | Binomial | Test Report |
| Multiple units, variable data | Verification | $C_{pk}$, ANOVA | Test Report + Statistical Appendix |
| End-to-end user need | Validation | Confidence interval | Validation Report |
| Usability / Human factors | Validation | Thematic analysis + error rate | Validation Report + HF Report |
| Sterility / Shelf-life | Validation | $A_{0}$ value, accelerated aging | Validation Report |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Process Capability ($C_{pk}$)

**Problem:** During a verification test, the flow rate of an infusion pump is measured. The specification is **LSL = 95 mL/h**, **USL = 105 mL/h**. The mean is $\mu = 99.8$ mL/h and the standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.2$ mL/h. Determine if the process is capable.

**Step 1 — Compute the lower capability:**

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{99.8 - 95}{3 \times 1.2} = \frac{4.8}{3.6} = 1.333
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the upper capability:**

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{105 - 99.8}{3 \times 1.2} = \frac{5.2}{3.6} = 1.444
$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum (worst case):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.333,\ 1.444) = 1.333
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret against acceptance threshold:**

$$
C_{pk} \ge 1.33 \implies \text{Process is marginally capable.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **medical device design verification**, a $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ is the typical minimum acceptance. For high-risk devices, $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ is often required.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V-Model of V&V (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[userNeeds]:::stage --> B[designInputs]:::stage
    B --> C[designOutputs]:::stage
    C --> D[moduleDesign]:::stage
    D --> E[unitVerification]:::verify
    E --> F[subsystemVerification]:::verify
    F --> G[systemVerification]:::verify
    G --> H[designValidation]:::validate
    H --> I[clinicalValidation]:::validate

    classDef stage fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef verify fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef validate fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

### 4.2 V&V Documentation Flow

```mermaid

    P2 --> P3[VerificationProtocol]:::plan
    P2 --> P4[ValidationProtocol]:::plan
    P3 --> P5[VerificationReport]:::report
    P4 --> P6[ValidationReport]:::report

    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[DesignHistoryFileDHF]:::final

```

### 4.3 Verification vs Validation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NewTestActivity]:::start --> B{DoesItCheckDesignInput?}:::q
    B -- YES --> C[VerificationActivity]:::verify
    B -- NO --> D{DoesItCheckUserNeed?}:::q
    D -- YES --> E[ValidationActivity]:::validate
    D -- NO --> F[ReevaluateScope]:::warn

```

### 4.4 V&V Lifecycle within ISO 13485 Clause 7.3

```mermaid

    S1[7.3.1 General]:::stage --> S2[7.3.2 DesignPlanning]:::stage
    S2 --> S3[7.3.3 DesignInputs]:::stage

    S4 --> S5[7.3.5 DesignReview]:::stage

```
