# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Q: Define the Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN). List any two differences between GMDN and EMDN.**

**Model Answer:**

> **GMDN Definition:** The Global Medical Device Nomenclature (GMDN) is a **comprehensive, international, standardized system of terms and definitions** used to consistently describe and categorize medical devices. It is maintained by the **GMDN Agency** (a UK-based non-profit) and contains **>23,000 preferred terms**, each with a unique numeric code and a precise definition. GMDN is used by regulators in the EU (legacy), UK, Australia, Canada, and others to support market surveillance, UDI assignment, and adverse event reporting.

**Two Differences between GMDN and EMDN:**

| Parameter | GMDN | EMDN |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner** | GMDN Agency (UK private) | European Commission (public) |
| **Cost** | Paid license required | Free for regulatory use |
| **EU Legal Mandate** | Allowed but not mandatory | **Mandatory** under EU MDR |
| **Structure** | Flat 5-level hierarchy | Hierarchical alphanumeric (C, F, P prefixes) |

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q: What is the role of RAPS in the medical device industry? Name any two certifications offered by RAPS.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of RAPS:**
> The **Regulatory Affairs Professionals Society (RAPS)**, founded in **1976** in the United States, is a global, non-profit organization that supports regulatory professionals in the healthcare product sector. RAPS's core functions include:
> 1. **Education and training** through workshops, conferences, and the Regulatory Affairs Certificate Program.
> 2. **Professional certification** to validate competency.
> 3. **Standards development** and best practice publications.
> 4. **Networking and advocacy** with global regulatory bodies.
> 5. **Knowledge dissemination** via the *Regulatory Focus* journal.

**Two Certifications:**
1. **RAC (Regulatory Affairs Certified)** — globally recognized with 4 regional variants (US, EU, Canada, Global).
2. **CIRA (Certified International Regulatory Affairs)** — focuses on Asia-Pacific regulatory frameworks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Both questions follow the **ESE Module Internal Choice** pattern. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: (a)** Explain the **evolution of medical device regulation** in four major eras, citing one key event in each. **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** Discuss the **general structure of medical device regulation** highlighting the five pillars. Why is post-market surveillance given increasing importance under the EU MDR 2017/745? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Evolution in Four Eras:**

1. **Reactive Era (Pre-1976):** Regulation was **incident-driven** and fragmented. Devices were often regulated as drugs. Key event: **Elixir Sulfanilamide disaster (1937)** — over 100 deaths from diethylene glycol poisoning led to the **US Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act of 1938**, mandating safety testing.

2. **Foundational Era (1976–1990):** Dedicated device legislation emerged. Key event: **US Medical Device Amendments of 1976**, which established **risk-based classification (Class I, II, III)**, premarket notification (510(k)), and the **Pre-Market Approval (PMA)** pathway. Simultaneously, the **EU issued Directive 90/385/EEC (1990)** for active implantable devices.

3. **Risk-Based & Harmonized Era (1990–2012):** The **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** was established in 1992. Key event: Publication of the **GHTF SG1 guidance on Summary Technical Documentation (STED)** in 2008, which harmonized global premarket submission formats. **ISO 13485:2003** became the QMS benchmark.

4. **Lifecycle & Evidence-Based Era (2012–Present):** **GHTF transitioned to IMDRF in 2011–12**. Key event: Adoption of **EU MDR 2017/745** in 2017 (effective May 2021) post the **PIP breast implant scandal**, introducing **clinical evidence, Eudamed, UDI, and stringent PMS/PMCF requirements** for the entire device lifecycle.

**(b) Five Pillars of Regulation:**

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-Market Controls** | Classification, conformity assessment, technical documentation | FDA 510(k), EU MDR Annex IX |
| **2. Quality Systems** | ISO 13485 QMS, design controls, CAPA | ISO 13485:2016 audit |
| **3. Clinical Evaluation** | Clinical investigation, literature, equivalence | ISO 14155, MDCG 2020-5 |
| **4. Post-Market Surveillance** | Vigilance, PSUR, trend analysis, FSCA | EU MDR Articles 85–92 |
| **5. UDI & Traceability** | Unique Device Identification system | EUDAMED, GUDID, UDI-DI/PI |

**Increasing PMS Importance under EU MDR:**
- The **PIP scandal (2010)** revealed that substandard silicone implants caused systemic harm undetected at premarket stage.
- EU MDR Article 85 mandates a **PMS plan for every device**, proportionate to risk class.
- **PSUR (Periodic Safety Update Report)** mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, III.
- **PMCF (Post-Market Clinical Follow-up)** studies are compulsory when pre-market data is insufficient.
- **Eudamed database** enables proactive signal detection across the EU market.
- The shift reflects a **lifecycle approach**: safety and performance are continuously demonstrated, not assumed at the moment of approval.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: (a)** Compare **GHTF and IMDRF** in terms of origin, membership, working structure, and regulatory impact. **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** What is a **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)**? List any **eight mandatory elements** of a CIP under EU MDR Annex XV. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GHTF vs IMDRF Comparison:**

| Parameter | GHTF | IMDRF |
|---|---|---|
| **Founded** | 1992 | 2011–2012 |
| **Founders/Members** | 5 (EU, US, Canada, Japan, Australia) | Expanded to 10+ members + WHO as observer |
| **Working Structure** | 5 Study Groups (SG1–SG5) | Multiple Working Groups (AET, MDIT, Nomenclature, RPS, PMD) |
| **Output Type** | Voluntary guidance documents | Converged regulatory frameworks, binding upon adoption |
| **Status (2024)** | **Discontinued (2012)** | **Active and growing** |
| **Key Contribution** | STED, AET code, QMS guidance | UDI, RPS ToC, SaMD framework, AIMD |
| **Membership** | Invited regulator-only | Open to additional regulators (India CDSCO joined 2019) |
| **Decision-Making** | Consensus | Consensus (formal) |

> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** GHTF documents remain the **foundation** for IMDRF work — IMDRF has not reinvented the wheel, but has **legalized and operationalized** the GHTF principles.

**(b) Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP):**

> **Definition:** A **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)** is a comprehensive document describing the **rationale, objectives, design, methodology, statistical considerations, monitoring procedures, and administrative organization** of a clinical investigation, in compliance with **EU MDR Annex XV, ISO 14155:2020, and 21 CFR Part 812** (where applicable).

**Eight Mandatory Elements of CIP (per EU MDR Annex XV):**

1. **General Information** — Sponsor details, investigators, sites, regulatory references, signature page.
2. **Justification for the Clinical Investigation** — Literature review, state of the art, gap analysis.
3. **Objectives and Endpoints** — Primary, secondary, exploratory; clinical performance and safety endpoints.
4. **Investigation Design** — Pilot/pivotal, randomized/blinded, multi-arm, adaptive, sample size.
5. **Population** — Inclusion, exclusion, withdrawal criteria, vulnerable populations, sample size justification.
6. **Investigational Device and Comparator Details** — Description, traceability, accountability.
7. **Procedures and Schedule of Assessments** — Visit schedule, screening, interventions, follow-up.
8. **Safety Reporting and Risk Management** — AE/SAE/SADE definitions, reporting timelines (10/15 days), DSMB charter.
9. **Statistical Analysis Plan** — Hypothesis, sample size, primary analysis, missing data handling.
10. **Data Management, Monitoring, Amendments, Deviations, Ethics, Informed Consent, Publication Policy, Insurance, Financing** — supplementary mandatory sections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing CIP with CSR** — CIP is the *pre-investigation* document; CSR is the *post-investigation* report. Mixing them up = **2–3 marks lost**.
> 2. **Writing "GHTF" in 2024 context** without acknowledging the **IMDRF transition** = loses the "currency of knowledge" credit (**1–2 marks**).
> 3. **Listing GMDN and EMDN without stating EMDN is mandatory under EU MDR** = incomplete answer (**1 mark**).
> 4. **Omitting PSUR/PMCF** when discussing PMS under EU MDR = loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to cite ISO 14155:2020** when discussing clinical investigation = **1 mark** lost.
> 6. **Generic answers without examples** (e.g., "FDA, EU") = partial credit only; always name specific regulations (21 CFR 812, EU MDR Annex XV).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Quick Recall Bullet Points:**

- **Regulatory Landscape** = web of laws, directives, standards, and harmonization bodies governing medical device lifecycle.
- **Evolution** = Reactive (pre-1976) → Foundational (1976–90) → Harmonized (1990–2012) → Lifecycle (2012–present).
- **Key Historical Triggers** = 1937 Elixir, 1962 Thalidomide, 1976 MDA, 2010 PIP scandal.
- **Five Pillars of Regulation** = Premarket, QMS, Clinical Evaluation, PMS, UDI/Traceability.
- **GMDN** = Paid, flat, GMDN Agency; **EMDN** = Free, hierarchical, EU mandatory since 2021.
- **GHTF (1992–2012)** = Voluntary guidance via SG1–SG5. **IMDRF (2012–present)** = Converged regulatory framework, formal Working Groups, binding upon adoption.
- **RAPS** = Founded 1976; **RAC** (4 regional variants) and **CIRA** are top certifications.
- **HTA** ≠ Regulator. HTA = value assessment (NICE, IQWiG, CADTH, ICER, HTAIn); Regulator = safety/efficacy (FDA, NB, CDSCO).
- **ICER Formula** = $\Delta \text{Cost} / \Delta \text{QALY}$ is the foundation of HTA cost-effectiveness.
- **Clinical Investigation** ≠ Drug Trial. Devices have **iterative design, learning curve, smaller samples, sham controls**.
- **CIP** = Pre-investigation blueprint; **CSR** = Post-investigation report.
- **CIP Mandatory Sections (EU MDR Annex XV)** = General info, justification, objectives/endpoints, design, population, procedures, statistics, safety, ethics, data management.
- **ISO 14155:2020** = the GCP-equivalent standard for medical device clinical investigations.
- **PMCF** is mandatory under EU MDR when pre-market clinical data is insufficient.
- **PMS Plan** is mandatory for every device under EU MDR Article 85.
- **PSUR** is mandatory for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices.
- **Eudamed** is the EU central database for device registration, UDI, certificates, vigilance, and CIPs.
- **MDSAP** allows a single audit accepted by 5 jurisdictions (US, Canada, Japan, Australia, Brazil).
- **Always cite** ISO 13485:2016 (QMS), ISO 14971 (Risk Management), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), ISO 14155:2020 (GCP for devices), EU MDR 2017/745, 21 CFR 807/812/820 in any KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Good clinical practice for clinical investigation of medical devices (ISO 14155:2011)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Good clinical practice for clinical investigation of medical devices (ISO 14155:2011)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 14155:2011 — Good Clinical Practice (GCP) for Medical Devices**
> ISO 14155:2011 is an international standard titled *"Clinical investigation of medical devices for human subjects — Good clinical practice."* It establishes the **ethical and scientific quality requirements** for designing, conducting, recording, and reporting clinical investigations carried out in human subjects to assess the **clinical performance, safety, and/or effectiveness** of medical devices.

The standard applies to **all clinical investigations of medical devices** whose results are intended to be submitted to regulatory authorities, including those for **CE marking**, **FDA 510(k)**, **PMA submissions**, and **post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF)** studies.

### Key Definitions (Per ISO 14155:2011, Clause 3)

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Clinical Investigation (CI)** | Systematic investigation in one or more human subjects, conducted to assess the clinical performance, effectiveness, or safety of a medical device. |
| **Clinical Performance** | Ability of a medical device to achieve its intended clinical purpose as claimed by the manufacturer, resulting in a clinical benefit to patients. |
| **Sponsor** | Individual or organization taking responsibility and liability for the initiation, management, and/or financing of a clinical investigation. |
| **Principal Investigator (PI)** | Qualified person responsible for conducting the clinical investigation at an investigation site. |
| **Ethics Committee (EC) / IRB** | Independent body constituted of medical, scientific, and non-scientific members, whose responsibility is to ensure the protection of the rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects. |
| **Informed Consent** | Process by which a subject voluntarily confirms his or her willingness to participate in a particular investigation, after having been informed of all aspects of the investigation that are relevant to the decision to participate. |
| **Serious Adverse Event (SAE)** | Adverse event that led to death, serious deterioration in health, surgical/medical intervention, or fetal distress. |
| **Adverse Device Effect (ADE)** | Adverse event related to the use of an investigational medical device. |
| **Device Deficiency** | Inadequacy of a medical device with respect to its identity, quality, durability, reliability, usability, safety, or performance. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clinical Trial Airport" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Clinical Investigation = A Highly Secured Airport Operation**

Imagine launching a new aircraft (the **medical device**). Before passengers (patients) can board, the aviation authority (regulator) demands proof of safety. ISO 14155:2011 is the **airport's standard operating manual** that governs every step of the trial flight:

| Airport Element | Medical Device Equivalent | ISO 14155 Reference |
|----------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Flight Plan | Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) | Clause 6 |
| Air Traffic Control (ATC) | Ethics Committee / IRB | Clause 5.6 |
| Pilot-in-Command | Principal Investigator (PI) | Clause 5.7 |
| Airline Operator | Sponsor | Clause 5.5 |
| Passenger Manifest | Case Report Form (CRF) | Clause 8.2 |
| Pre-flight Inspection | Risk-Benefit Analysis | Clause 5.4 |
| Flight Recorder (Black Box) | Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) | Clause 10 |
| Incident Reports | Adverse Event Reporting (SAE/ADE) | Clause 9 |

Just as an airport cannot operate without strict protocols, a clinical investigation cannot be ethically or scientifically sound without ISO 14155 compliance. Every safeguard—from informed consent to adverse event reporting—exists to **protect the rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Quantitative Boundaries (Bold Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Thresholds Defined in ISO 14155:2011 and Related Regulatory Frameworks**

- **Minimum number of subjects** for a clinical investigation is determined **statistically**, not by an absolute number, but must provide **statistical significance** ($p < 0.05$) where applicable.
- **Risk classification** of medical device: Class I, IIa, IIb, or III per **EU MDD/MDR Annex VIII** rules.
- **Adverse Event reporting timeline**:
  - **Serious Adverse Events (SAE)**: Report to sponsor within **24 hours** of awareness.
  - **Device deficiencies that could have led to SAE**: Report within **24 hours**.
- **Informed consent** must be obtained **before** any investigation-related procedure is performed.
- **Clinical investigation report (CIR)** must be finalized within **1 year** of completion of the clinical investigation (or per local regulatory requirements).
- **Significance level (α)** typically set at **0.05** for primary endpoint statistical testing.
- **Statistical power (1−β)** typically set at **0.80** (80%) for sample size calculation.

---

## 1.4 Why ISO 14155:2011 Matters — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Standard is Central to KTU PEBRT863 Module 2**

ISO 14155:2011 is the **bridge between technical device performance and ethical human subject research**. Without GCP compliance:
- Regulatory submissions will be **rejected** by FDA, Notified Bodies, and Central Drug Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO).
- Patient safety is **unprotected**.
- Scientific data becomes **non-credible** and **non-reproducible**.
- Manufacturers face **liability, product recall, and market withdrawal**.

The standard aligns with the **Declaration of Helsinki (1964, last updated 2013)**, the **ICH-GCP E6 (R2)** for pharmaceuticals (with device-specific adaptations), and regional regulations such as **EU MDR 2017/745**, **US 21 CFR Part 812**, and **India MDR 2017**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Principles of ISO 14155:2011 (Clause 4)

The standard rests on **three foundational pillars**, mirroring the Belmont Report and Declaration of Helsinki:

### Pillar 1: Ethical Conduct
- Clinical investigations must be conducted in accordance with the **Declaration of Helsinki**.
- The **rights, safety, and well-being of subjects** shall be the primary consideration and shall prevail over interests of science and society.
- Compliance with **good clinical practice (GCP)** is a legal and ethical obligation.

### Pillar 2: Scientific Validity
- Clinical investigations must be **scientifically sound**, with clearly defined objectives, endpoints, and statistical plans.
- Investigation design must be appropriate to the device's **risk classification** and intended use.
- The investigation must generate **reliable, reproducible, and verifiable data**.

### Pillar 3: Regulatory Compliance
- Investigations must conform to **applicable local and international regulations**.
- Documentation must be **traceable, auditable, and retained** for at least **15 years** (or per local law).

---

## 2.2 The Clinical Investigation Lifecycle (Clause-by-Clause Breakdown)

### Step 1: Risk-Benefit Analysis (Clause 5.4)

Before initiating any clinical investigation, the sponsor must demonstrate that:
- The **anticipated benefits** to subjects and/or future patients justify the **foreseeable risks**.
- The investigation is **designed to minimize subject risk**.
- Risk mitigation measures are in place (training, monitoring, stopping rules).

**Risk-Benefit Equation (Conceptual):**

$$
B_{\text{net}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot B_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} Q_j \cdot R_j
$$

Where:
- $B_{\text{net}}$ = Net clinical benefit
- $P_i$ = Probability of benefit outcome $i$
- $B_i$ = Magnitude of benefit outcome $i$
- $Q_j$ = Probability of risk outcome $j$
- $R_j$ = Magnitude of risk outcome $j$
- $n$ = number of anticipated benefits
- $m$ = number of anticipated risks

> **Decision rule:** The investigation is **ethically permissible** only if $B_{\text{net}} > 0$ and **all reasonable risk mitigations** are in place.

---

### Step 2: Ethics Committee Review and Approval (Clause 5.6)

The Ethics Committee (EC) / Institutional Review Board (IRB) must review and approve:
1. The **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP)**.
2. The **Investigator's Brochure (IB)**.
3. The **Informed Consent Form (ICF)** and patient information leaflet.
4. **Subject recruitment procedures** (advertisements, compensation).
5. **Investigator qualifications** and site suitability.
6. **Insurance and indemnity** coverage.

**Composition Requirement (Per ISO 14155:5.6):**

$$
\text{EC Composition} = \geq 5 \text{ members} \quad \text{with} \quad \begin{cases} \geq 1 \text{ medical/scientific member} \\ \geq 1 \text{ non-medical/non-scientific member} \\ \text{Multidisciplinary and gender-diverse} \end{cases}
$$

---

### Step 3: Informed Consent Process (Clause 5.8)

The informed consent process must include:

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Voluntariness** | Subject must participate voluntarily; no coercion. |
| **Comprehension** | Information must be in plain language; translated if needed. |
| **Disclosure** | All risks, benefits, alternatives, and compensation must be disclosed. |
| **Documentation** | Written consent signed + dated by subject and investigator. |
| **Special populations** | Minors, pregnant women, cognitively impaired: additional safeguards. |
| **Right to withdraw** | Subject may withdraw at any time without penalty. |

**Consent for Emergency Situations:**

$$
\text{Emergency Consent} = \begin{cases} \text{Prior consent if possible} \\ \text{Deferred consent (if approved by EC)} \\ \text{Witness present if subject cannot sign} \end{cases}
$$

---

### Step 4: Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) — Clause 6

The CIP is the **master protocol document** that defines the entire investigation. Required content (per ISO 14155 Clause 6.2):

1. **General information** (sponsor, title, identification number).
2. **Identification and description of the device** (name, model, classification, intended purpose).
3. **Justification for the design** of the clinical investigation.
4. **Objectives and hypotheses** (primary, secondary endpoints).
5. **Design type** (randomized, blinded, multi-center, single-arm, etc.).
6. **Subject population** (inclusion/exclusion criteria).
7. **Subject treatment** (procedures, follow-up schedule).
8. **Endpoints** (primary, secondary, safety endpoints).
9. **Adverse event definitions and reporting procedures**.
10. **Statistical analysis plan (SAP)**.
11. **Data management and quality control**.
12. **Deviations from the plan** and amendment procedures.
13. **Statement of compliance** with ISO 14155 and Declaration of Helsinki.

---

### Step 5: Roles and Responsibilities (Clause 5)

| Role | Key Responsibilities |
|------|---------------------|
| **Sponsor** | Initiate, manage, finance; ensure regulatory submissions; provide IB; ensure monitoring; report SAEs to regulators. |
| **Principal Investigator (PI)** | Overall conduct at site; ensure delegation to qualified staff; obtain informed consent; report AEs; maintain source documents. |
| **Investigator Team** | Perform procedures per CIP; protect subject rights; report deviations. |
| **Monitor (CRA)** | Site visits to verify GCP compliance; review CRFs vs. source data; escalate issues. |
| **Auditor** | Independent, systematic examination of trial conduct; reports to sponsor's QA. |
| **Ethics Committee** | Review and approve; conduct continuing review; ensure subject protection. |

---

### Step 6: Adverse Event Reporting (Clause 9)

```
Adverse Event Classification (ISO 14155:2011, Clause 9)
├── Adverse Event (AE) — any untoward medical occurrence
├── Serious Adverse Event (SAE) — AE that:
│    ├── Led to death
│    ├── Led to serious deterioration in health
│    ├── Required surgical/medical intervention
│    ├── Led to fetal distress/death/congenital anomaly
│    └── Other medically significant event
├── Adverse Device Effect (ADE)
│    ├── Related to investigational device
│    └── Includes device malfunction that led to SAE
├── Serious Adverse Device Effect (SADE)
├── Unanticipated Serious Adverse Device Effect (USADE)
│    └── NOT in IFU, IFU, or IB → must be reported to EC and regulators within 7–10 days
└── Device Deficiency
     └── Inadequacy of device that could have led to SAE
```

**Reporting Timeline Matrix:**

| Event Type | To Sponsor | To EC | To Regulator |
|-----------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| SAE | **24 hours** | Per local requirement (typically 7–10 days) | Per local requirement |
| USADE | **24 hours** | **7 days** (FDA) / **10 days** (EU) | **7–10 days** |
| Device Deficiency (no SAE) | **24 hours** | Per CIP | Per local requirement |
| Non-serious AE | In routine CRF | Per CIP | Not typically required |

---

### Step 7: Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) — Clause 10

The CIR is the **final scientific document** that presents the results. Required elements:

1. Cover page (device, sponsor, dates, investigators).
2. Synopsis of investigation.
3. **Summary of results** (effectiveness, safety, statistical analysis).
4. **Discussion** of clinical significance, deviations, limitations.
5. **Conclusion** with risk-benefit assessment.
6. **Appendix** with raw data, CRF, statistical outputs.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS, DEFINITIONS, AND BOUNDARY CONDITIONS — KTU 2024 SCHEME**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| **Net Clinical Benefit** | $B_{\text{net}} = \sum P_i B_i - \sum Q_j R_j$ | Dimensionless index; must be $> 0$ |
| **Sample Size (two proportions)** | $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$ | Subjects per arm; $Z_{0.025}=1.96$, $Z_{0.20}=0.84$ |
| **Confidence Interval (95%)** | $CI_{95\%} = \hat{p} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | For proportion endpoints |
| **Statistical Power** | $1 - \beta$ | Target typically $\geq 0.80$ |
| **Significance Level** | $\alpha$ | Typically $0.05$ |
| **Event Reporting Window (SAE)** | $\Delta t_{\text{SAE}} \leq \mathbf{24 \text{ hours}}$ | From awareness to sponsor |
| **EC Reporting Window (USADE)** | $\Delta t_{\text{USADE}} \leq \mathbf{7\text{--}10 \text{ days}}$ | Varies by jurisdiction |
| **Record Retention** | $T_{\text{retention}} \geq \mathbf{15 \text{ years}}$ | Or per local regulation |
| **Risk Classification (EU MDR)** | Class I, IIa, IIb, III | Annex VIII Rule 1–22 |
| **Informed Consent Validity** | Voluntary + Informed + Documented | All three required |
| **Deviation Classification** | Major / Minor | Major = affects subject rights, safety, or data integrity |
| **Monitoring Frequency** | Risk-based | High-risk devices → more frequent monitoring |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ISO 14155:2011 is not merely a regulatory document—it is an **engineering framework** for:

1. **Biomedical engineers** designing investigational devices and protocols.
2. **Quality assurance professionals** ensuring audit readiness.
3. **Regulatory affairs specialists** preparing submissions to FDA, Notified Bodies, CDSCO.
4. **Clinical research associates (CRAs)** monitoring site compliance.
5. **Data managers** designing CRFs and electronic data capture (EDC) systems.
6. **Risk managers** integrating ISO 14971 risk management with ISO 14155 clinical evaluation.

> **Industry sectors that depend on ISO 14155:**
> Cardiovascular implants (stents, pacemakers), orthopedic devices, diagnostic imaging, in-vitro diagnostics (with ISO 20916), active medical devices, AI/ML-enabled SaMD (Software as a Medical Device), and combination products.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sample Size Calculation for Clinical Investigation

**Problem:** A sponsor plans a two-arm, randomized clinical investigation comparing a new coronary stent (Treatment) against a marketed comparator (Control). The expected primary endpoint success rates are:

$$
p_1 = 0.85 \text{ (treatment arm)}, \quad p_2 = 0.75 \text{ (control arm)}
$$

Significance level: $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided). Statistical power: $1 - \beta = 0.80$.

**Derive the required sample size per arm.**

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the formula (per ISO 14155 statistical considerations).**

For comparing two independent proportions with equal allocation:

$$
n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}
$$

**Step 2: Determine the critical Z-values from the standard normal distribution.**

For $\alpha = 0.05$ two-sided:

$$
Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96
$$

For $1 - \beta = 0.80$:

$$
Z_{\beta} = Z_{0.20} = 0.842 \approx 0.84
$$

**Step 3: Compute the pooled variance term $p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)$.**

$$
p_1(1-p_1) = 0.85 \times (1 - 0.85) = 0.85 \times 0.15 = 0.1275
$$

$$
p_2(1-p_2) = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.75) = 0.75 \times 0.25 = 0.1875
$$

$$
p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2) = 0.1275 + 0.1875 = 0.3150
$$

**Step 4: Compute the squared effect size $(p_1 - p_2)^2$.**

$$
p_1 - p_2 = 0.85 - 0.75 = 0.10
$$

$$
(p_1 - p_2)^2 = (0.10)^2 = 0.01
$$

**Step 5: Compute $(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2$.**

$$
Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta} = 1.96 + 0.84 = 2.80
$$

$$
(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 = (2.80)^2 = 7.84
$$

**Step 6: Compute the numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 7.84 \times 0.3150 = 2.4696
$$

**Step 7: Compute $n$.**

$$
n = \dfrac{2.4696}{0.01} = 246.96
$$

**Step 8: Round up to ensure statistical power is achieved.**

$$
n_{\text{per arm}} = 247
$$

**Step 9: Account for anticipated dropout rate (typically 10–20%).**

With 15% expected dropout:

$$
n_{\text{adjusted}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{per arm}}}{1 - d} = \dfrac{247}{1 - 0.15} = \dfrac{247}{0.85} = 290.59
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{adjusted per arm}} = 291}
$$

**Step 10: Total sample size (both arms).**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 291 = 582 \text{ subjects}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Confidence Interval for Adverse Event Rate

**Problem:** In a clinical investigation of a novel infusion pump, 18 adverse device effects (ADE) were observed in 120 subjects. Compute the **95% confidence interval** for the true ADE rate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the observed ADE proportion.**

$$
\hat{p} = \dfrac{x}{n} = \dfrac{18}{120} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard CI formula for proportions.**

$$
CI_{95\%} = \hat{p} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the standard error term.**

$$
\hat{p}(1-\hat{p}) = 0.15 \times (1 - 0.15) = 0.15 \times 0.85 = 0.1275
$$

$$
\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n} = \dfrac{0.1275}{120} = 0.0010625
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.0010625} = 0.03260
$$

**Step 4: Compute the margin of error.**

$$
ME = 1.96 \times 0.03260 = 0.0639
$$

**Step 5: Compute the lower and upper bounds.**

$$
CI_{\text{lower}} = 0.15 - 0.0639 = 0.0861
$$

$$
CI_{\text{upper}} = 0.15 + 0.0639 = 0.2139
$$

**Step 6: Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} = (8.61\%, \ 21.39\%)}
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true ADE rate for this infusion pump lies between **8.61% and 21.39%** in the target population.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Risk-Benefit Quantification

**Problem:** A novel implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) has the following profile based on pre-clinical studies:

| Outcome | Probability | Severity Score (0–10) |
|---------|------------|----------------------|
| Sudden cardiac death prevention | 0.90 | 10 (benefit) |
| Lead fracture (surgical revision needed) | 0.05 | 7 (harm) |
| Infection requiring explantation | 0.03 | 8 (harm) |
| Inappropriate shock delivery | 0.12 | 4 (harm) |

**Compute the net clinical benefit score.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute weighted benefit.**

$$
B_{\text{weighted}} = P_{\text{benefit}} \times S_{\text{benefit}} = 0.90 \times 10 = 9.00
$$

**Step 2: Compute weighted harms.**

$$
R_1 = 0.05 \times 7 = 0.35
$$

$$
R_2 = 0.03 \times 8 = 0.24
$$

$$
R_3 = 0.12 \times 4 = 0.48
$$

$$
R_{\text{total}} = 0.35 + 0.24 + 0.48 = 1.07
$$

**Step 3: Compute net benefit.**

$$
B_{\text{net}} = B_{\text{weighted}} - R_{\text{total}} = 9.00 - 1.07 = 7.93
$$

**Step 4: Decision rule per ISO 14155:5.4.**

$$
\boxed{B_{\text{net}} = 7.93 \gg 0 \implies \text{Clinically justified, proceed to clinical investigation}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ISO 14155:2011 vs ICH-GCP E6 vs Declaration of Helsinki

| Aspect | ISO 14155:2011 | ICH-GCP E6 (R2) | Declaration of Helsinki |
|--------|---------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Scope** | Medical devices | Pharmaceuticals | All human subject research |
| **Device-specific focus** | Yes (usability, IFU, device deficiencies) | No | No |
| **Pharmacovigilance** | Device-specific (ADE, SADE, USADE) | Drug-specific (ADR, SUSAR) | General AE |
| **Investigator's Brochure** | Required (Clause 5.9) | Required | Not specified |
| **Risk Management Link** | Tied to ISO 14971 | Not linked | General risk principle |
| **Sponsor definition** | Per ISO 14155 | Per ICH-GCP | Not defined |
| **Sample size** | Statistically justified | Statistically justified | Justified |
| **EC composition** | ≥ 5 members | Independent body | Independent committee |
| **Consent for vulnerable populations** | Special provisions | Special provisions | Special safeguards |
| **Post-market follow-up** | PMCF studies covered | Not covered | Not covered |

---

## 3.5 Critical Analytical Insights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering-Level Analytical Conclusions**

1. **Risk-Benefit is continuous, not binary.** The same device may be acceptable in a high-risk population (e.g., terminal cancer) but unacceptable in a low-risk population (e.g., cosmetic use).
2. **Statistical significance ≠ Clinical significance.** A 0.1% improvement in success rate may be statistically significant with $n = 100{,}000$ but clinically meaningless.
3. **Sample size must consider both efficacy and safety endpoints.** A trial powered only for efficacy may miss rare serious adverse events.
4. **Deviations from the CIP are major red flags for auditors.** Every deviation must be documented, justified, and reported.
5. **The Clinical Investigation Report (CIR) is a legal document.** It is submitted to regulators and becomes part of the device's regulatory dossier.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Good Clinical Practice" as per ISO 14155:2011. List its three foundational principles. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PEBRT863, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**

> Good Clinical Practice (GCP) per ISO 14155:2011 is a standard for the **design, conduct, recording, and reporting of clinical investigations** of medical devices involving human subjects, ensuring **ethical and scientific quality**.

The three foundational principles are:

1. **Ethical Conduct:** The rights, safety, and well-being of human subjects shall prevail over the interests of science and society, in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki.
2. **Scientific Validity:** Clinical investigations must be scientifically sound, with clearly defined objectives, endpoints, statistical plans, and reproducible data.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Investigations must comply with all applicable local and international regulatory requirements, with full traceability and documentation.

*(1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for listing and briefly explaining the three principles. Distribution may vary.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between "Adverse Event (AE)," "Adverse Device Effect (ADE)," and "Device Deficiency" as defined in ISO 14155:2011. **[3 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PEBRT863, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Causal Relationship to Device |
|------|-----------|------------------------------|
| **Adverse Event (AE)** | Any untoward medical occurrence experienced by a subject, regardless of causal relationship. | Not required; AE may be unrelated. |
| **Adverse Device Effect (ADE)** | An adverse event related to the use of the investigational medical device. | **Required** — causal relationship established. |
| **Device Deficiency** | An inadequacy of a medical device with respect to its identity, quality, durability, reliability, usability, safety, or performance. | Refers to a **device property issue**, not a clinical event. |

**Key distinction:** An AE is a **clinical occurrence**; an ADE is an AE with a **causal link** to the device; a Device Deficiency is a **technical problem** with the device that may or may not cause an AE.

*(1 mark per term definition, with 0.5 marks for distinction. Award 3 marks for complete, accurate tabular response.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PEBRT863, Module 2]**

#### Part (a): Explain in detail the roles and responsibilities of the Sponsor and Principal Investigator as per ISO 14155:2011. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**SPONSOR RESPONSIBILITIES (per ISO 14155 Clause 5.5):**

1. **Initiation and Management:** The sponsor is responsible for initiating, managing, and financing the clinical investigation. This includes selecting qualified investigators, providing the device, and ensuring regulatory submissions.

2. **Investigator's Brochure (IB):** The sponsor must prepare and update the IB, which contains all relevant pre-clinical and clinical data about the device, including intended use, materials, manufacturing, and known risks.

3. **Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP):** The sponsor develops the CIP in collaboration with the PI, including objectives, design, endpoints, statistical plan, and AE reporting procedures.

4. **Regulatory Submissions:** The sponsor submits the clinical investigation application to the relevant regulatory authorities (FDA, CDSCO, Notified Body) and the Ethics Committee.

5. **Monitoring:** The sponsor must ensure adequate monitoring of the investigation through trained Clinical Research Associates (CRAs) or monitors.

6. **Adverse Event Reporting:** The sponsor must report USADEs, SADEs, and any new findings to the EC, regulatory authorities, and all participating investigators within prescribed timelines (24 hours to 7–10 days).

7. **Quality Assurance and Auditing:** The sponsor must implement QA systems and conduct independent audits.

8. **Record Retention:** The sponsor must retain all clinical investigation records for at least **15 years** (or per local law) after completion.

9. **Risk-Benefit Reassessment:** Continuous reassessment of risk-benefit throughout the investigation; ability to suspend or terminate if risk-benefit becomes unfavorable.

**PRINCIPAL INVESTIGATOR RESPONSIBILITIES (per ISO 14155 Clause 5.7):**

1. **Overall Conduct at Site:** The PI is responsible for the conduct of the clinical investigation at the investigation site, including delegation of tasks to qualified sub-investigators and study coordinators.

2. **Informed Consent:** The PI (or designee) must obtain valid informed consent from every subject before any investigation-related procedure.

3. **Compliance with CIP:** The PI must conduct the investigation in strict accordance with the approved CIP, with deviations only in emergency situations.

4. **Subject Medical Care:** The PI is responsible for the medical care of subjects, including decisions regarding continued participation.

5. **Adverse Event Reporting:** The PI must report all AEs, SAEs, and device deficiencies to the sponsor within 24 hours of awareness.

6. **Source Documentation:** The PI must maintain accurate, complete, and legible source documents and CRFs.

7. **Investigational Device Accountability:** The PI must maintain records of device receipt, use, and disposal.

8. **Continuing EC Review:** Submit progress reports to the EC at prescribed intervals (typically annually).

*(Award 3.5 marks for Sponsor responsibilities and 3.5 marks for PI responsibilities. Use bullet-point clarity.)*

---

#### Part (b): Describe the components of a Clinical Investigation Plan (CIP) as per ISO 14155:2011. Explain the importance of the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP) within the CIP. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of CIP (per ISO 14155:2011, Clause 6.2):**

1. **General Information:** Title, sponsor, identification number, investigators, sites, dates.
2. **Device Description:** Name, model, manufacturer, classification (I, IIa, IIb, III), intended purpose, materials, sterilization, IFU.
3. **Justification for Design:** Rationale for the chosen design based on device risk and research question.
4. **Objectives and Hypotheses:** Primary, secondary, safety objectives with clear hypotheses.
5. **Investigation Design:** Type (randomized, single-arm, multi-center, blinded), allocation, follow-up duration.
6. **Subject Population:** Inclusion and exclusion criteria, screening procedures, vulnerable population considerations.
7. **Subject Treatment:** Procedures, schedule of assessments, device implantation/use protocol.
8. **Endpoints:** Primary, secondary, safety endpoints with operational definitions.
9. **Adverse Event Reporting:** Definitions, reporting timelines, procedures for SAE, USADE, device deficiency.
10. **Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP):** Sample size, statistical methods, significance level, power, handling of missing data.
11. **Data Management:** CRF design, data entry, database lock, query resolution.
12. **Quality Control and Monitoring:** Monitoring plan, frequency, source data verification.
13. **Amendments and Deviations:** Procedures for protocol changes and deviation documentation.
14. **Ethical and Regulatory Compliance:** Statement of adherence to Declaration of Helsinki, ISO 14155, and local regulations.

**Importance of the Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP):**

1. **Sample Size Justification:** The SAP provides the statistical basis for the chosen sample size, ensuring the investigation is powered to detect clinically meaningful differences (typically power $\geq 0.80$ at $\alpha = 0.05$).
2. **Prevents Data-Driven Bias:** By specifying analyses in advance, the SAP minimizes the risk of post-hoc data dredging and selective reporting.
3. **Defines Primary Analysis:** The SAP identifies the **primary analysis population** (intention-to-treat, per-protocol) and the primary statistical test, which determines the investigation's success.
4. **Handles Missing Data:** The SAP specifies methods for handling missing data (last observation carried forward, multiple imputation, sensitivity analysis).
5. **Subgroup Analyses:** Pre-specified subgroup analyses are defined to avoid false positives from multiple comparisons.
6. **Regulatory Acceptance:** A robust, pre-specified SAP is a **regulatory requirement** for FDA, EU MDR, and CDSCO submissions. Lack of SAP leads to submission rejection.
7. **Reproducibility:** A clear SAP enables independent statistical review and reproducibility of results.

*(Award 4 marks for CIP components and 3 marks for SAP importance. Use tabular format for components to enhance clarity.)*

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PEBRT863, Module 2]**

#### Part (a): With the help of a flow diagram, explain the Adverse Event (AE) reporting process in a clinical investigation as per ISO 14155:2011. What is the difference between SAE, SADE, and USADE? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**AE Reporting Process (per ISO 14155:2011, Clause 9):**

**Step 1: AE Occurs at Site** — Any untoward medical occurrence is detected by the PI or study staff.

**Step 2: Assessment by PI** — The PI evaluates:
- Seriousness (serious vs. non-serious).
- Causal relationship to the device (related vs. unrelated).
- Anticipatedness (listed in IB/IFU or not).

**Step 3: Documentation** — All AEs are recorded in the Case Report Form (CRF).

**Step 4: Reporting to Sponsor** —
- **SAE:** Reported to sponsor within **24 hours** of awareness.
- **Device Deficiency that could have led to SAE:** Reported within **24 hours**.
- **Non-serious AE:** Recorded in routine CRF.

**Step 5: Sponsor's Causality Assessment** — Sponsor reviews and concurs with PI's assessment.

**Step 6: Regulatory Reporting by Sponsor** —
- **USADE:** Reported to EC and Regulatory Authority within **7 days (FDA)** or **10 days (EU)**.
- **SAE/SADE:** Per local regulatory requirements.

**Step 7: EC Review and Continuous Monitoring** — Ethics Committee reviews periodic safety reports.

**Flow Diagram:**

```
Adverse Event Occurs at Site
        |
        v
PI Assessment (Serious? Related? Anticipated?)
        |
   +----+----+----+
   |    |    |    |
Serious  Serious  Non-serious
Unrelated Related  AE
   |       |       |
   v       v       v
Report    Report  Record in CRF
to        to
Sponsor   Sponsor (routine)
within    within
24 hrs    24 hrs
   |       |
   v       v
Sponsor  Sponsor
Review   Review
   |       |
   v       v
   SAE    SADE
           |
      Anticipated? 
      /          \
    Yes           No
     |             |
     v             v
  ASADE         USADE
     |             |
     v             v
  Report to     Report to
  EC and        EC and
  Regulator     Regulator
  (per local)   within 7-10 days
```

**Differences: SAE vs. SADE vs. USADE:**

| Term | Definition | Causal to Device | Anticipated | Reporting Timeline |
|------|-----------|------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **SAE** | Serious untoward medical occurrence | Not required | Not required | 24 hours to sponsor |
| **SADE** | Serious AE related to investigational device | **Required** | May be anticipated | 24 hours to sponsor; regulator per local rule |
| **USADE** | Serious device-related AE that was **not anticipated** in IFU/IB | **Required** | **Not anticipated** | 24 hours to sponsor; **7-10 days** to EC and regulator |

*(Award 3.5 marks for process explanation with diagram, 3.5 marks for differences table.)*

---

#### Part (b): Discuss the informed consent process as per ISO 14155:2011. What are the special considerations for vulnerable populations? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Informed Consent Process (per ISO 14155:2011, Clause 5.8):**

**1. Definition:** Informed consent is a process by which a subject **voluntarily confirms** his or her willingness to participate in a particular investigation, after having been informed of **all aspects of the investigation** relevant to the decision to participate.

**2. Required Elements of Informed Consent (per ISO 14155:5.8.2):**

a) **Statement of Investigation Purpose:** That the study is research and that participation is voluntary.
b) **Device Information:** Description of the investigational device, its intended use, and procedures.
c) **Subject's Responsibilities:** What the subject must do during the investigation.
d) **Foreseeable Risks and Discomforts:** All known risks, including device-related and procedure-related.
e) **Anticipated Benefits:** To the subject and/or others, or absence of benefit.
f) **Alternative Treatments:** Available alternatives to participation.
g) **Compensation and Medical Treatment for Injury:** Description of compensation for injury and medical treatment.
h) **Confidentiality:** How subject's personal data will be protected.
i) **Right to Withdraw:** Subject may withdraw at any time without penalty or loss of benefits.
j) **Contact Information:** Whom to contact for questions about the investigation and the subject's rights.

**3. Documentation:**
- **Written consent** signed and dated by the subject.
- **Witness present** if subject is illiterate or cannot write.
- **Copy of consent** given to subject.
- **Consent re-affirmed** at every follow-up visit (especially after any protocol amendment).

**4. Special Considerations for Vulnerable Populations:**

**a) Minors (Children):**
- **Assent** (in addition to consent) for children aged 7–17 years, using age-appropriate language.
- **Parental/Guardian consent** required.
- EC must include a member with pediatric expertise.

**b) Pregnant and Lactating Women:**
- Extra precaution; investigations allowed only if the potential benefit justifies the risk to the fetus/infant.
- Fetal monitoring must be included if relevant.

**c) Cognitively Impaired Individuals:**
- Consent from **legally authorized representative (LAR)**.
- Re-consent (assent) obtained from subject if/when capacity is regained.

**d) Elderly Subjects:**
- Need for clear, simple language; risk of undue influence from caregivers.
- Capacity to consent must be verified.

**e) Prisoners:**
- Subject to additional safeguards (e.g., 45 CFR 46 Subpart C).
- Cannot be a population of convenience; only certain research allowed.

**f) Emergency Situations:**
- **Exception from informed consent (EFIC)** may apply if:
  - Subject cannot consent due to emergency.
  - No standard treatment available.
  - EC has approved the protocol.
  - Community consultation has occurred.
  - Consent obtained from subject or LAR as soon as possible afterward.

**5. ISO 14155:2011 Requirements for Vulnerable Populations:**
- EC must specifically approve the inclusion of vulnerable populations.
- Justification of their inclusion must be in the CIP.
- Additional monitoring and safety measures.
- Special informed consent procedures.
- Assent procedures for children.

*(Award 3.5 marks for informed consent process and 3.5 marks for vulnerable populations. Use bold for key terms.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL ALERT — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS**
>
> **Common Mistakes in ISO 14155:2011 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating ISO 14155 (Medical Devices) with ICH-GCP (Pharmaceuticals):** Many students write drug-specific examples (e.g., randomization to drug vs. placebo) instead of device-specific (e.g., investigational stent vs. comparator stent). **Examiner will deduct 1–2 marks** for this confusion.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "Device Deficiency" term:** This is unique to ISO 14155:2011 and is often missing in answers. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong reporting timeline:** SAE → 24 hours; USADE → 7–10 days. Writing "USADE in 24 hours" is incorrect. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Declaration of Helsinki:** ISO 14155:2011 explicitly references it. **Loss: 0.5 mark.**
>
> 5. **Vague risk-benefit answer:** Saying "risks and benefits" without showing the formula or quantification loses 1–2 marks. **Use the $B_{\text{net}}$ formula.**
>
> 6. **Missing "PMCF" link:** Post-Market Clinical Follow-up is a key application of ISO 14155. Not mentioning this loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 7. **Forgetting vulnerable population safeguards:** Special populations are **high-weightage** in KTU exams. **Loss: 2 marks** if not addressed.
>
> 8. **Inadequate CIP content:** Listing only 4–5 components instead of the 14 required components of Clause 6.2. **Loss: 2 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION — READ BEFORE EXAM**

- **ISO 14155:2011** = GCP for medical device clinical investigations.
- **Three pillars:** Ethical conduct, scientific validity, regulatory compliance.
- **Foundational document:** Declaration of Helsinki.
- **Risk-Benefit:** $B_{\text{net}} = \sum P_i B_i - \sum Q_j R_j > 0$.
- **Sample size formula:** $n = \dfrac{(Z_{\alpha/2} + Z_{\beta})^2 [p_1(1-p_1) + p_2(1-p_2)]}{(p_1 - p_2)^2}$.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$ (typical).
- **Power:** $1 - \beta \geq 0.80$ (typical).
- **SAE reporting to sponsor:** **24 hours**.
- **USADE reporting to EC/Regulator:** **7–10 days**.
- **Record retention:** **15 years** minimum.
- **EC composition:** ≥ **5 members**, multidisciplinary, gender-diverse.
- **CIP** has 14 required components (Clause 6.2).
- **Investigator's Brochure (IB)** is **mandatory** for medical device investigations.
- **CIR** must be submitted to regulators within **1 year** of investigation completion.
- **Vulnerable populations** require assent + parental consent + EC justification.
- **Emergency consent** has strict criteria (EFIC).
- **Linkage to ISO 14971:** Risk management is integrated with clinical investigation.
- **PMCF** = post-market clinical follow-up; also governed by ISO 14155 principles.
- **Three key roles:** Sponsor, PI, EC/IRB.
- **Adverse event hierarchy:** AE → SAE → SADE → USADE.
- **Device Deficiency** = technical issue with device (unique to ISO 14155).
- **Deviations:** Major (affects subject rights/safety/data) vs. Minor.
- **Monitoring:** Risk-based; CRA conducts site initiation, monitoring, and close-out visits.
- **EU MDR alignment:** ISO 14155 supports conformity assessment under EU MDR 2017/745 Annex XIV.
- **US alignment:** US 21 CFR Part 812 (IDE) shares similar principles.
- **Data integrity:** ALCOA+ principles (Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate).
- **Quality management:** ISO 14155 is part of the broader QMS per ISO 13485.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — ISO 14155:2011**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes (CO2: Apply regulatory standards to medical device clinical investigations). Practice the 14-mark question patterns and use the formula sheet for all numerical problems.*
