# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Design History File (DHF) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DHF (Design History File)** — A formal, regulatory-mandated compilation of records that describes the **complete design history of a medical device**, from initial user needs and design inputs through verification, validation, and design transfer to production. Defined under **21 CFR 820.30 (US FDA)** and **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3**.

It is the **chronological evidence** that proves the device was developed in accordance with the manufacturer's design and development planning documents.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Device Baby Album" 🍼

Imagine you are raising a child. From the day they are born until they go to college, you keep a **photo album + report card** that records:
- Why the child was conceived (user needs)
- What milestones were expected (design inputs)
- How the child was trained (verification)
- Whether they passed exams (validation)
- How they finally became independent (design transfer)

If a teacher or government inspector later questions *how this child became what they are*, you simply hand over the album. The **DHF is exactly that album for a medical device** — and regulators (FDA, Notified Bodies, CDSCO) can demand to see it during an audit at any point in the device's lifecycle.

---

## 1.2 Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)** — A **quality assurance system** mandated by regulatory authorities that ensures medical devices are **consistently produced and controlled** to standards appropriate to their intended use, as defined by **21 CFR Part 820 (US FDA QSR)**, **ISO 13485:2016**, **EU MDR 2017/745**, and **WHO GMP for Medical Devices**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Five-Star Kitchen" 🍳

Think of a Michelin-star restaurant. Every dish must:
- Use **approved ingredients** (raw material controls)
- Be cooked by a **certified chef** (trained personnel)
- Be prepared in a **clean kitchen** (cleanroom/controlled environment)
- Follow a **standard recipe every time** (SOPs)
- Be **tasted before serving** (in-process & final QC)

GMP is the **regulatory recipe book** that ensures every medical device leaving the factory is just as safe and effective as the first one tested.

---

## 1.3 Adverse Event Reporting — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adverse Event (AE) Reporting** — The **mandatory post-market surveillance activity** by which manufacturers, importers, healthcare professionals, and users notify a regulatory authority of any **death, serious injury, or malfunction** of a medical device that could cause or contribute to such outcomes. Governed by **21 CFR Part 803 (MDR — Medical Device Reporting)** in the US and **MEDDEV 2.12-1 / EU MDR Article 87–92** in Europe.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Neighbourhood Watch" 🚨

In a community, if a streetlight falls and injures someone, neighbours report it to the municipality so it can be **fixed or removed**. Adverse event reporting is the **medical device world's neighbourhood watch** — users and manufacturers report problems so regulators can issue recalls, alerts, or label changes before more people get hurt.

---

## 1.4 Regulatory Bodies, Notified Bodies & Leading Organizations

| Entity | Definition | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Regulatory Body** (e.g., US FDA, EU Commission, CDSCO India) | Government authority that **creates and enforces** medical device laws. | The **legislator & police** |
| **Notified Body** (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA) | Third-party, **accredited organization** that performs **conformity assessment** for CE marking. | The **independent auditor** |
| **Leading Organizations** (e.g., WHO, IMDRF, GHTF, ISO) | International bodies that develop **harmonized standards, guidance, and global frameworks**. | The **United Nations of medical devices** |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence System" 🚗

- **Regulatory Body** = RTO (Regional Transport Office) — sets traffic law
- **Notified Body** = Empanelled testing agency — certifies your car passed tests
- **Leading Organizations** = UN-WP.29 / SAE — write international vehicle standards

---

## 1.5 Health Organization (HO) Medical Devices — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HO Medical Devices** (sometimes termed **Priority Medical Devices** or **WHO Essential Medical Devices**) — Devices that address the **world's leading disease burden** (communicable + non-communicable diseases, reproductive/maternal/child health, emergency care). Classified by **WHO Priority Medical Devices List** for universal health coverage.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "WHO Essential Medicines List" but for Devices 💊➡️🔧

Just as WHO publishes a list of **essential medicines** every country should stock, the WHO also publishes a corresponding list of **essential medical devices** (oximeters, ventilators, X-ray machines, infusion pumps) that every health system must have access to.

---

## 1.6 Availability, Affordability & Accessibility — The AAA Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AAA Framework (WHO)** — A trinity ensuring medical devices reach patients who need them:
> - **Availability** = sufficient supply exists
> - **Affordability** = cost is not a barrier
> - **Accessibility** = physically reachable in time

> [!NOTE]
> A 2024 WHO report estimates **one billion people** lack access to essential medical devices. The **WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework (GMRF)** for medical devices (including in-vitro diagnostics) provides guidance on technical specifications, regulation, and procurement to address this.

---

## 1.7 Basic Documents of National / State Level Regulatory Importance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **building-block documents** every country/state requires to operate a functional medical device regulatory system:
> - **Medical Device Rules** (e.g., India: Medical Devices Rules 2017, amended 2020, 2022, 2023)
> - **National List of Essential Medical Devices**
> - **Drugs & Cosmetics Act** (in jurisdictions where devices are regulated under drug law)
> - **State Drug Controller Licences**
> - **Import Licence / Registration Certificate**
> - **Form 40 / Form 41** (India-specific manufacturing & loan licences)
> - **CE Certificate / 510(k) / PMA / CDSCO Licence**
> - **ISO 13485 Certificate**, **Free Sale Certificate**, **Certificate of Pharmaceutical Product (CoPP)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 DHF — The 9 Logical Pillars (ISO 13485:2016 §7.3 + 21 CFR 820.30)

The DHF is not a single file but a **set of records** documenting each design phase. Below are the **9 core elements** a KTU examiner expects you to know:

1. **Design & Development Plan** — Who, what, when, how
2. **Design Inputs** — User needs, intended use, regulatory requirements
3. **Design Outputs** — Specifications, drawings, software code, labels
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary
5. **Design Verification** — "Did we make the device right?" (confirms outputs meet inputs)
6. **Design Validation** — "Did we make the right device?" (confirms it meets user needs in simulated/actual use)
7. **Design Transfer** — Hand-off from R&D to manufacturing
8. **Design Changes** — Documented control of every change
9. **Design History File Index** — Master index linking all above

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification ≠ Validation** — this is the **#1 KTU trap**:
> - **Verification =** Outputs meet Inputs (analytical + testing on bench)
> - **Validation =** Device meets User Needs (clinical/simulated-use testing on humans or representative users)

### Why each step matters

- **Inputs prevent ambiguity** — they translate vague "make a good syringe" into measurable specs
- **Outputs are traceable** — every output must be linked back to an input
- **Reviews catch errors** — a 3-person review saves 30-person re-work
- **Verification/Validation separate technical from clinical truth** — a device can be technically perfect (verification ✓) but clinically useless (validation ✗)

---

## 2.2 GMP — The 6 Subsystems (per ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820)

| # | Subsystem | What it Controls | KTU 1-liner |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Management Responsibility** | Quality policy, management review, resources | "Top management owns quality" |
| 2 | **Resource Management** | Human resources, training, infrastructure, work environment | "Right people, right place" |
| 3 | **Product Realization** | Planning, customer-related processes, design & development, purchasing, production, validation | "From idea to shipment" |
| 4 | **Measurement, Analysis & Improvement** | Internal audit, CAPA, monitoring, non-conforming product | "Measure to improve" |
| 5 | **Production & Process Controls** | SOPs, labelling, packaging, installation, servicing, sterilization | "Control every step" |
| 6 | **Document & Record Controls** | Documents, records, change control | "If it's not documented, it didn't happen" |

### Quality Management System (QMS) Hierarchy

$$\text{QMS} \supset \text{GMP} \supset \text{Production Controls} \supset \text{SOPs} \supset \text{Records}$$

> **Real-world utility:** GMP is the **operational backbone** that turns a designed device (DHF) into a manufactured, sterile, labelled, traceable product. Without GMP, even a perfectly designed device can be **unsafe in production**.

---

## 2.3 Adverse Event Reporting — Reporting Timelines & Logic

Under **21 CFR Part 803**, a manufacturer must report:

$$
T_{\text{report}} =
\begin{cases}
30 \text{ days} & \text{if event requires remedial action OR is a serious injury} \\
10 \text{ working days} & \text{if event is a serious injury that was not previously reportable OR a malfunction that could cause/contribute to death/serious injury} \\
5 \text{ working days} & \text{if remedial action is necessary to prevent an unreasonable risk of substantial harm} \\
\text{Untimely} & \text{if information suggests event may require remedial action} \\
\end{cases}
$$

> **Engineering utility:** This timeline triggers a **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** cycle within the QMS — linking post-market surveillance back to design & production improvements.

### EU MDR Adverse Incident Reporting Timeline

- **Serious Incident → Competent Authority within 15 days** (or 10 days for serious public health threat)
- **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) →** Notification within 48 hours of initiation
- **Field Safety Notice (FSN)** — communicated to users and patients

### India CDSCO Adverse Event Reporting (Materiovigilance Programme of India — MvPI)

- **Serious AE → within 15 days** to CDSCO / IPC
- **Death or serious public health threat → within 10 days**
- Reported via **MDMC (Medical Device Monitoring Centre)**

---

## 2.4 Medical Device Approval Pathway — Decision Matrix

| Risk Class (EU MDR) | Conformity Assessment Route | Notified Body Required? |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I (non-sterile, non-measuring)** | Self-declaration (Annex VIII) | **No** |
| **Class I (sterile OR measuring)** | Annex IX or XI | **Yes** |
| **Class IIa** | Annex IX or XI | **Yes** |
| **Class IIb** | Annex IX or XI | **Yes** |
| **Class III** | Annex IX (with mandatory consultation for medicinal / animal-tissue) | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notified Body ≠ Regulator.** A Notified Body **certifies** the manufacturer's QMS and product conformity. The **regulator (e.g., EU Competent Authority, CDSCO)** issues the **registration / licence / market authorization**.

### KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | KTU Trap |
|---|---|---|
| DHF Verification | $V_{\text{output}} \stackrel{?}{=} I_{\text{input}}$ | Many write it as "V = I" — wrong symbol; write full |
| DHF Validation | $D_{\text{final}} \stackrel{?}{=} U_{\text{need}}$ | Don't confuse with verification |
| Risk Class × Reporting | $\text{Class III} \Rightarrow$ longest report content | High risk ≠ most reports |
| QMS Hierarchy | $\text{QMS} \supset \text{GMP} \supset \text{SOPs}$ | GMP is not QMS; it is part of it |
| AAA | $\text{Access} = f(\text{Avail}, \text{Afford}, \text{Geography})$ | All three must hold simultaneously |

### Real-world Engineering Utility

These frameworks allow engineers, regulatory affairs professionals, and quality managers to:
1. **Predict regulatory costs and timelines** before a device launch
2. **Streamline multi-market launches** (e.g., a single DHF can support US FDA 510(k), EU CE, India CDSCO)
3. **Build post-market data loops** that feed back into next-generation design
4. **Justify pricing & reimbursement** to insurance bodies and tender committees

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Approval Pathways

> **Purpose:** Equip the KTU student with a side-by-side reasoning model for answering 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions.

| Dimension | **US FDA (510(k) / PMA)** | **EU MDR (CE Mark)** | **India (CDSCO — MD Rules 2017)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk Classification | Class I, II, III | Class I, IIa, IIb, III (Rule 1–22) | Class A, B, C, D |
| Pathway | 510(k) (substantial equivalence) for most Class II; PMA for Class III | Annex IX (QMS + Technical Documentation) / Annex X (Type Examination) / Annex XI (Production Assurance) | Class A: self-registration; Class B–C–D: audit-based licence by CDSCO |
| Notified Body | **No** (FDA does audit) | **Yes** (Notified Body) | Only for Class C & D (Indian NB or in some cases, MRA) |
| Designated Authority | US FDA | EU Competent Authority + NANDO-listed NB | CDSCO (DCGI) + State Licencing Authority (SLA) |
| Time to Market | 510(k) ~ 6–9 months; PMA ~ 12–36 months | 6–18 months | 3–6 months for licence after dossier submission |
| Clinical Data | Usually for Class III | Mandatory for Class III & implantable | Required for Class C & D |
| Quality System | 21 CFR 820 (QSR) | ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 13485:2016 + Schedule V (Indian) |
| Post-Market | 21 CFR 803 (MDR) | EU MDR Article 87–92 (Vigilance) | MvPI (Materiovigilance) + Form MD-15 |

### Step-by-Step — How a 510(k) Pathway is Derived

> **Goal:** Derive the regulatory submission strategy for a hypothetical **Class II non-invasive blood pressure monitor**.

**Step 1 — Determine Device Classification**

$$\text{Device} = \text{BP Monitor (non-invasive)} \xrightarrow{\text{21 CFR 870.1130}} \text{Class II}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Predicate Device(s)**

A **predicate device** is a legally marketed device to which the new device is compared for **substantial equivalence (SE)**.

$$
\text{SE} = f(D_{\text{intended use}}, D_{\text{technological characteristics}}, D_{\text{performance testing}})
$$

**Step 3 — Choose Submission Type**

- If SE is established → **Traditional 510(k)**
- If modifications are minor → **Special 510(k)** (uses design controls / DHF data)
- If device is processed by a third party → **Third-Party 510(k)**
- If SE is to a predicate only in intended use → **Abbreviated 510(k)** (relies on FDA-recognized standards)

**Step 4 — Assemble Technical Documentation**

$$
\text{510(k) Dossier} = \{\text{Cover Letter, Indications for Use, 510(k) Summary}, \text{Truthful & Accurate Statement}, \text{Class III Certification (if applicable)}, \text{Financial Cert/Disclosure}, \text{Performance Testing (Verification)}, \text{Bench Testing}, \text{Software Documentation}, \text{Biological Evaluation}, \text{Labelling}, \text{Comparison Table to Predicate}\}
$$

**Step 5 — Pay User Fee & Submit**

$$
\text{Total Time} = T_{\text{acceptance}} + T_{\text{substantive review}} + T_{\text{deficiency response}} \approx 6{-}9 \text{ months}
$$

> **Examiner tip:** Show the *logical chain* (Classification → Predicate → Type → Dossier → Submit) — this scores full marks in 14-mark "approval pathway" questions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Notified Body Roles vs. Regulatory Body Roles

| Activity | Regulatory Body | Notified Body |
|---|---|---|
| Sets legal framework | ✅ | ❌ |
| Performs QMS audit | Sometimes (FDA) | ✅ (for Class IIa–III) |
| Reviews Technical Documentation | ✅ (PMA, De Novo) | ✅ (CE marking) |
| Issues market authorization | ✅ | ❌ (issues CE Certificate only) |
| Conducts post-market surveillance | ✅ | ✅ (audit-based) |
| Issues Certificate of Free Sale | ✅ (CDSCO, FDA) | ❌ |
| Performs unannounced audits | ❌ (FDA does) | ✅ (NB can) |

> **The relationship is complementary, not duplicative.** Think of it as:
> - **Regulator** = University (issues the degree)
> - **Notified Body** = External examiner (signs off that you passed)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AAA (Availability, Affordability, Accessibility)

$$\text{Patient Outcomes} = f(A_{\text{available}} \times A_{\text{affordable}} \times A_{\text{accessible}})$$

If any one of the three is **zero**, the outcome is zero. This is a classic **AND-gate** in engineering terms.

| Dimension | Definition | Technical Specification Lever | Regulatory Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | Right device exists at right time | Manufacturing scale, supply-chain maturity | Pooled procurement (WHO, UNICEF), strategic stockpile regulations |
| **Affordability** | Price is within payer reach | Design-for-cost (DfC), local manufacturing | Price control, tender-based pricing, tax incentives |
| **Accessibility** | Reachable in time (geography, awareness) | Portability, ruggedness for low-resource settings | Health technology assessment (HTA), reimbursement lists |

### Step-by-Step — WHO's 4-Step Framework for HO Medical Device Prioritization

1. **Identify disease burden** (DALYs) for a country/region
2. **Map existing device inventory** in healthcare facilities
3. **Gap analysis** — match inventory to required interventions
4. **Regulatory + procurement alignment** — list devices by **technical specification + regulatory status**

$$
\text{Priority List} = \text{argmax}_{\text{device}} \left( \frac{\text{Burden Reduction}}{\text{Cost} \times \text{Regulatory Complexity}} \right)
$$

> **Engineering utility:** This optimization helps public health ministries decide **which devices to regulate first** when budgets are constrained.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Global Organizations

| Organization | Acronym | Role | KTU 1-liner |
|---|---|---|---|
| World Health Organization | **WHO** | Global health, publishes Priority Medical Devices list, GMRF | "World's public health ministry" |
| International Medical Device Regulators Forum | **IMDRF** | Harmonizes regulatory practice across jurisdictions | "Global council of regulators" |
| Global Harmonization Task Force (predecessor of IMDRF) | **GHTF** | Historical body; created the 5-member summary for adverse events | "Where IMDRF's DNA came from" |
| International Organization for Standardization | **ISO** | Publishes ISO 13485, 14971, IEC 60601 family | "Standards factory" |
| International Electrotechnical Commission | **IEC** | Electrical safety & EMC standards (IEC 60601) | "Electrical safety twin of ISO" |
| World Bank, UNICEF, Global Fund | — | Procurement & funding | "Pocketbook + checkbook" |
| Asian Harmonization Working Party | **AHWP** | Asian regulatory harmonization | "Asia's IMDRF" |
| Bureau of Indian Standards | **BIS** | India standards body | "India's ISO" |
| Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation | **CDSCO** | India regulator for medical devices | "India's FDA" |
| International Medical Device Industry Association | **GMTA / AdvaMed / MedTech Europe** | Industry trade groups | "Industry's voice" |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — National vs. State Level Documents (India Example)

> **KTU note:** The KTU examiner loves "list and explain" questions worth 7 marks. Memorize this matrix.

| Document | Authority | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (amended 2020, 2022, 2023) | CDSCO | Master rule book — classification, registration, manufacturing, import |
| **Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940** (Section 3B) | Parliament of India | Empowers Central Govt. to regulate devices |
| **National List of Essential Medical Devices (NLEMD)** | MoHFW / NPPA | Reference for public procurement & price ceiling |
| **State Drug Controller Licence (Form MD-3 / MD-5 / MD-9)** | State Licensing Authority (SLA) | Manufacturing licence for Class A & B |
| **Central Licence (Form MD-4 / MD-6 / MD-10)** | CDSCO | Manufacturing licence for Class C & D |
| **Import Licence (Form MD-14 / MD-15)** | CDSCO | Import registration certificate (IRC) for foreign manufacturers |
| **Free Sale Certificate** | CDSCO / SLA | Issued to Indian exporters |
| **Certificate of Pharmaceutical Product (CoPP)** | CDSCO | For export to WHO-prequalification markets |
| **Quality Management System Certificate (ISO 13485)** | Certification Body (e.g., TÜV) | QMS attestation |
| **Drugs & Magic Remedies (Objectionable Advertisements) Act, 1954** | D&C Act arm | Restricts misleading device advertising |
| **Legal Metrology Act, 2009 + Packaged Commodities Rules** | Legal Metrology Dept. | Labelling, weights, dimensions |
| **E-waste (Management) Rules, 2022** | MoEFCC | End-of-life device disposal |
| **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) — IS series** | BIS | Indian standards for devices (e.g., IS 13450 for ECG machines) |

> **Engineering utility:** Engineers working on device commercialization must ensure all 4 layers are satisfied — **Design (BIS/ISO) + QMS (ISO 13485) + Registration (CDSCO/SLA) + Post-market (MvPI)**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 DHF — Design Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Needs and Intended Use] --> B0[Design Inputs]
    B0 --> C0[Design Outputs]
    C0 --> D0[Design Review]
    D0 --> E0[Design Verification]
    E0 --> F0[Design Validation]
    F0 --> G0[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    G0 --> H0[Design Changes and CAPA]
    H0 --> I0[Design History File Compilation]

    E0 -.confirms outputs meet inputs.-> B0
    F0 -.confirms device meets user needs.-> A0

    I0 --> J0[Regulatory Audit by FDA or Notified Body]
```

> **Read this as:** *Start with user needs → derive inputs → produce outputs → review → verify (bench) → validate (clinical) → transfer → capture changes → file as DHF → make audit-ready.*

---

## 4.2 GMP — 6-Subsystem QMS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Management Responsibility] --> P2[Resource Management]
    P2 --> P3[Product Realization]
    P3 --> P4[Production and Process Controls]
    P4 --> P5[Measurement Analysis and Improvement]
    P5 --> P6[Document and Record Controls]
    P5 -.feeds back to.-> P1
```

> **Read this as:** *QMS is a closed loop — Management commits → Resources are provided → Product is realized → Process is controlled → Performance is measured → Documents are kept → Feedback drives management review.*

---

## 4.3 Adverse Event Reporting — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Event Occurred or Reported] --> Q2{Death or Serious Injury}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Manufacturer Aware}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Remedial Action Needed}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[5 Working Days MDR]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[10 Working Days MDR]
    Q2 -- No --> Q7{Malfunction Likely to Cause Harm}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[30 Calendar Days MDR]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9[Record in Internal Log]
    Q5 --> Q10[FDA or CDSCO or NB Notified]
    Q6 --> Q10
    Q8 --> Q10
    Q9 --> Q11[Periodic Summary if Applicable]
```

> **Read this as:** *Severity + Remediability drives the timeline. Death/serious harm with remediation = 5 days; without = 10 days; malfunction = 30 days; minor event = log only.*

---

## 4.4 Medical Device Approval — Global Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Manufacturer] --> S1{Classify Device}
    S1 -- US --> S2[FDA 510k or PMA]
    S1 -- EU --> S3[Notified Body CE Mark]
    S1 -- India --> S4[CDSCO Registration or Licence]
    S1 -- Japan --> S5[PMDA Approval]
    S1 -- Canada --> S6[Health Canada MDL]
    S1 -- Australia --> S7[TGA ARTG]
    S1 -- Brazil --> S8[ANVISA Registration]

    S2 --> S9[Market Authorization]
    S3 --> S9
    S4 --> S9
    S5 --> S9
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9

    S9 --> S10[Post Market Surveillance Vigilance and CAPA]
    S10 -.feeds back to.-> S1
```

---

## 4.5 AAA Framework — Device Access Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A00[Patient Need] --> B00{Device Available}
    B00 -- No --> C00[Outcome = ZERO]
    B00 -- Yes --> D00{Device Affordable}
    D00 -- No --> E00[Outcome = ZERO]
    D00 -- Yes --> F00{Device Accessible}
    F00 -- No --> G00[Outcome = ZERO]
    F00 -- Yes --> H00[Outcome ACHIEVED]
```

> **Read this as:** *AAA is an AND-gate. Any failure → no patient benefit.*

---

## 4.6 National & State Level Document Flow (India)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940] --> L2[Medical Devices Rules 2017]
    L2 --> L3[CDSCO Central Licence for Class C and D]
    L2 --> L4[State Licensing Authority Licence for Class A and B]
    L2 --> L5[Import Registration Certificate]
    L2 --> L6[Free Sale Certificate and CoPP]
    L3 --> L7[Manufacturing and Market]
    L4 --> L7
    L5 --> L7
    L6 --> L8[Export Market]
    L7 --> L9[Materiovigilance Post Market]
    L8 --> L9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Verifying vs. Validating:** Students routinely write "validation = checking specifications." That is **verification**. Validation = clinical / user-need proof. **3 marks lost per occurrence.**
> - **Notified Body = Regulator confusion:** Many answers incorrectly describe the Notified Body as the issuer of the CE licence. The NB issues the **CE Certificate**; the **Competent Authority** authorises placement on the EU market.
> - **5-day / 10-day / 30-day MDR timeline:** Students often write "30 days for all events" — the 5-day timeline (for events requiring remedial action) is the **favourite KTU trap**.
> - **GMP ≠ QMS:** GMP is a **subsystem** of QMS, not the same thing. Examiners specifically test this.
> - **AAA "AND" gate:** Students treat Availability, Affordability, Accessibility as independent — they are **conjunctive**. Losing any one = no access.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. Define the Design History File (DHF) and list its four key components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Design History File (DHF)** is a regulatory-mandated compilation of records that documents the design history of a medical device, demonstrating that it was developed in accordance with an approved design plan. It is required under **21 CFR 820.30** (US FDA) and **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3**.

**Four key components:**
1. **Design & Development Plan**
2. **Design Inputs** (user needs, regulatory requirements)
3. **Design Outputs** (specifications, drawings, software, labels)
4. **Verification & Validation records, Design Review records, Design Transfer records, Design Change records, and a Master Index.**

---

### Q2. What is the role of a Notified Body in medical device approval? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Notified Body (NB)** is a third-party, **accredited private organization** designated by an EU Member State to perform **conformity assessment** of medical devices under the **EU MDR 2017/745**.

Its role is to:
1. **Audit the manufacturer's QMS** (typically ISO 13485:2016 compliance)
2. **Review the Technical Documentation / Design Dossier**
3. **Issue the CE Certificate** that allows the manufacturer to affix the **CE mark** and place the device on the EU market

> The NB does **not** issue the market authorization; it certifies conformity. The **Competent Authority** in each Member State enforces post-market controls.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain in detail the structure of GMP under ISO 13485:2016. How does it differ from a generic QMS? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
**Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** is the part of quality assurance that ensures medical devices are **consistently produced and controlled** to quality standards appropriate to their intended use, as per **ISO 13485:2016** and **21 CFR 820 (US QSR)**.

**Six Subsystems of GMP under ISO 13485:2016:**

| # | Subsystem | Key Requirements | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Management Responsibility (Clause 5)** | Quality policy, management review, resource provision | Top management must own quality |
| 2 | **Resource Management (Clause 6)** | Human resources, training, infrastructure, work environment | Right people, right place |
| 3 | **Product Realization (Clause 7)** | Planning, customer-related processes, design & development, purchasing, production, validation | From idea to shipment |
| 4 | **Design & Development (Clause 7.3)** | Inputs, outputs, review, verification, validation, transfer, changes | The "DHF" lives here |
| 5 | **Production & Service Provision (Clause 7.5)** | Production controls, validation, sterilization, labelling, installation, servicing | Controls day-to-day manufacture |
| 6 | **Measurement, Analysis & Improvement (Clause 8)** | Monitoring, internal audit, CAPA, non-conforming product, analysis of data | "If you can't measure it, you can't improve it" |

**Difference between GMP and QMS (Generic):**

| Dimension | **QMS (Generic — ISO 9001)** | **GMP (Medical Device — ISO 13485)** |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Any industry | Specifically medical devices |
| Risk Management | Optional | **Mandatory (ISO 14971)** |
| Documented Procedures | General | Specific to medical device lifecycle |
| Validation Requirements | Less prescriptive | Mandatory for sterile, software, processes |
| Regulatory Focus | Customer satisfaction | **Patient safety + regulatory compliance** |
| Design Controls | Less detailed | Exhaustive (Clause 7.3 + DHF) |
| Post-market | Not central | **Central — feedback into design** |

> **Conclusion:** GMP is a **medical-device-specific implementation** of a generic QMS, with risk management, design controls, and post-market surveillance as non-negotiable pillars.

#### (b) Discuss the adverse event reporting system under US FDA's 21 CFR Part 803 and compare it with the EU MDR vigilance framework. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**US FDA — Medical Device Reporting (MDR) — 21 CFR Part 803:**

A manufacturer must report:

| Trigger | Timeline |
|---|---|
| Event that requires remedial action to prevent unreasonable risk of substantial harm | **5 working days** |
| Serious injury that was not previously reportable, or malfunction that could cause/contribute to death/serious injury | **10 working days** |
| Death, serious injury, or malfunction (general) | **30 calendar days** |

Reports are submitted via **eMDR (Electronic Medical Device Reporting)**. User facilities (hospitals) must report deaths to FDA and manufacturer within **10 working days**; serious injuries to manufacturer only.

**EU MDR — Vigilance System (Article 87–92):**

| Trigger | Timeline |
|---|---|
| Serious public health threat | **2 days** (preliminary) + **10 days** (final) |
| Death or serious deterioration in health | **10 days** (after manufacturer becomes aware) |
| Other serious incidents | **15 days** |
| Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) | **Notification to Competent Authority without undue delay** |
| Field Safety Notice (FSN) | Communicated to users by manufacturer |

Reports are submitted to the **Competent Authority** of the Member State where the incident occurred, with **vigilance co-ordinator** review.

**Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | US FDA (MDR) | EU MDR |
|---|---|---|
| Reporting body | FDA (federal) | Competent Authority (Member State) |
| Submission mode | eMDR system | EUDAMED (Vigilance module) |
| Responsibility | Manufacturer + Importer + User Facility | Manufacturer + Authorised Representative |
| Trigger language | "Death, serious injury, malfunction" | "Serious incident" (broader) |
| Time for FSCA | Implicit in 5-day rule | Explicit, "without undue delay" |
| Database | MAUDE (public) | EUDAMED (under rollout) |

> **Conclusion:** Both systems aim to detect safety signals early and trigger CAPA, but the EU MDR vigilance system is **broader in scope** (includes "serious public health threat") and **more integrated** with corrective action (FSCA / FSN).

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the global efforts to ensure availability, affordability, and accessibility of HO (health organization) medical devices. Include WHO's role and at least two national programs. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**The AAA Framework:**
WHO defines the AAA of essential medical devices as a **trinity**:
- **Availability** — Right device, right quantity, right place, right time
- **Affordability** — Price is not a barrier to patient or health system
- **Accessibility** — Geographically and operationally reachable in time

Failure of **any one** = no patient benefit (AND-gate logic).

**WHO's Global Efforts:**

1. **WHO Priority Medical Devices List (PMDL)** — Identifies devices that address the **highest disease burden** in a population. Used by member states for procurement planning.

2. **WHO Global Model Regulatory Framework (GMRF) for Medical Devices including IVDs** — Provides guidance to countries (especially low- and middle-income) on:
   - Regulatory system establishment
   - Risk-based classification
   - Pre-market evaluation
   - Post-market surveillance

3. **WHO Prequalification Programme** — Evaluates IVDs, vaccines, and devices for **UN agency procurement**. This opens markets for manufacturers and ensures safety for buyers.

4. **WHO Health Technology Assessment (HTA) Programme** — Provides evidence-based evaluation of devices' clinical and cost-effectiveness.

5. **WHO Compendium of Innovative Health Technologies** — Curates **low-cost, rugged, scalable** devices for low-resource settings.

**Two National Programs (Examples):**

**A. India's National Medical Device Policy 2023 + PLI Scheme:**
- Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme offers **USD 1.7 Bn** to promote domestic manufacturing of medical devices.
- Aims to **reduce import dependence** (currently 80%+) and lower costs.
- Medical Device Parks in **Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Himachal Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Telangana, Uttar Pradesh, and Maharashtra** provide common infrastructure.

**B. Brazil's SUS (Sistema Único de Saúde):**
- Brazil's unified public health system maintains an **approved device list** for tender-based procurement.
- **ANVISA** (regulator) and **SUS** (procurement) coordinate to ensure affordability.

**Other Examples:**
- **Thailand's Universal Coverage Scheme** — uses HTA for device inclusion
- **Kenya's KEMSA** — central medical supplies agency for affordability
- **USA's 510(k) third-party review programme** — speeds up availability

> **Conclusion:** Global AAA achievement requires a **multi-stakeholder** approach: **WHO sets norms**, **regulators enforce**, **procurement agencies** ensure access, and **industry** manufactures. The KTU student must understand that regulation is not an obstacle to AAA — it is a **prerequisite**.

#### (b) List and briefly explain the basic documents of national and state level importance for medical device regulatory efforts in India. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (amended 2020, 2022, 2023):**
- The **master rule book** notified under the Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940.
- Defines **risk-based classification** (Class A, B, C, D).
- Prescribes **registration, manufacturing, import, labelling, post-market** requirements.
- Amended to incorporate **voluntary registration for Class A (non-sterile, non-measuring)** in 2020.
- In 2023, **online licensing & single-window** were introduced.

**2. Drugs & Cosmetics Act, 1940 (Section 3B):**
- The **parent act** empowering the Central Government to regulate devices "in the public interest."
- Essential legal backbone; without it, the MD Rules would not have legislative authority.

**3. National List of Essential Medical Devices (NLEMD) — MoHFW:**
- First published in 2018, revised in 2020.
- Lists **essential devices** for public health facilities, used for **price ceiling** under **Drugs Prices Control Order (DPCO)**.

**4. Form MD-3 / MD-5 / MD-9 — State Licences (Class A & B):**
- Issued by the **State Licensing Authority (SLA)**.
- Authorises manufacture of **Class A (non-sterile, non-measuring) and Class B (low-moderate risk)** devices in that state.

**5. Form MD-4 / MD-6 / MD-10 — Central Licences (Class C & D):**
- Issued by **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)**.
- Authorises manufacture of **Class C (moderate-high) and Class D (high risk)** devices — e.g., ventilators, stents, implants.

**6. Form MD-14 / MD-15 — Import Registration Certificate (IRC):**
- Issued to **foreign manufacturers** who wish to export to India.
- Mandatory for all classes of imported devices (with some exemptions).

**7. Free Sale Certificate (FSC):**
- Issued by **CDSCO** to Indian exporters as proof that the device is freely sold in India.
- Required for export to many countries.

**8. Certificate of Pharmaceutical Product (CoPP):**
- Issued by **CDSCO** under **WHO Scheme** for export to countries with WHO-prequalification requirements.

**9. ISO 13485:2016 Certificate:**
- Issued by a **Certification Body** (e.g., TÜV, BSI, DEKRA).
- Attests that the manufacturer's **QMS** meets ISO 13485 requirements — mandatory for CE marking and CDSCO licensing.

**10. BIS Standards (Bureau of Indian Standards):**
- Indian standards like **IS 13450 (ECG machines), IS 14627 (dialyzers), IS 7620 (sterile disposable syringes)**.
- Devices conform to BIS standards for **public procurement eligibility**.

**11. E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022:**
- Govern **end-of-life disposal** of electrical and electronic medical devices.

**12. Legal Metrology Act, 2009 + Packaged Commodities Rules:**
- Governs **labelling, weight, dimensions** on retail-packaged devices.

> **Conclusion:** Medical device regulation in India is a **layered architecture** — Parliament (Act) → Central Government (Rules) → CDSCO / SLA (Licences) → BIS (Standards) → Certification Bodies (QMS) → E-waste/Metrology (End-of-life & labelling). The KTU student must appreciate that **all layers must align** for legal market access.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **DHF** = design history file required by **21 CFR 820.30 / ISO 13485 §7.3**; contains design plan, inputs, outputs, reviews, verification, validation, transfer, changes, index.
- **Verification** = outputs meet inputs (bench testing).
- **Validation** = device meets user needs (clinical / simulated use).
- **GMP** = part of QMS that ensures consistent production; ISO 13485 has 6 main clauses (5, 6, 7, 7.3, 7.5, 8).
- **QMS ⊃ GMP** — GMP is a subset, not the whole.
- **Adverse Event Reporting** — US FDA: 5/10/30 day MDR under 21 CFR 803; EU: 2/10/15 day vigilance under MDR 2017/745.
- **Notified Body** — third-party that audits QMS & reviews Technical Documentation; issues **CE Certificate**, not market authorization.
- **Regulatory Body** — government agency that issues market authorization; e.g., US FDA, EU Competent Authority, CDSCO, PMDA, TGA, Health Canada.
- **Leading Organizations** — WHO (Priority Medical Devices), IMDRF (harmonization), GHTF (predecessor), ISO (standards), IEC (electrical), AHWP (Asia), CDSCO (India), BIS (India).
- **HO Medical Devices** — devices on the **WHO Priority Medical Devices List** for universal health coverage.
- **AAA Framework** — Availability, Affordability, Accessibility are **conjunctive** (AND-gate).
- **National Documents (India)** — Medical Devices Rules 2017, Drugs & Cosmetics Act 1940, NLEMD, Form MD-3 to MD-15, ISO 13485 Certificate, BIS Standards, Free Sale Certificate, CoPP, E-Waste Rules 2022, Legal Metrology Act.
- **State-level Documents (India)** — Form MD-3 / MD-5 / MD-9 issued by **State Licensing Authority** for Class A & B devices.
- **Global Submission Pathways** — 510(k) (Class II, US), PMA (Class III, US), CE Mark (EU), CDSCO Licence (India), PMDA (Japan), MDL (Canada), ARTG (TGA, Australia), ANVISA (Brazil).
- **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI)** — Indian adverse event reporting system for medical devices; coordinated by **Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC)** with **MDMC (Medical Device Monitoring Centres)**.
- **EUDAMED** — EU's new medical device database, being rolled out; will integrate UDI, vigilance, and registration data.
- **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** — globally harmonised system (US FDA, EU MDR) for traceability of devices.
- **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** — links every non-conformance and adverse event back to the QMS for root-cause elimination.

> **Final KTU tip:** When answering **14-mark "compare" questions**, ALWAYS use a **two-column comparison table** with at least **5 dimensions** — this is what examiners look for. When answering **3-mark "define" questions**, give the **definition + statutory reference + 2–3 examples** — never just the definition.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of US FDA, CE and Indian medical device rule
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Comparison of US FDA, CE and Indian Medical Device Rules"?

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of US FDA, CE and Indian medical device rule

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):**
> The comparative study of three global medical device regulatory frameworks — the **United States Food and Drug Administration (US-FDA)** system, the **European Union Conformité Européenne (CE) Marking** system under the **Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745)**, and the **Indian Medical Device Rules, 2017** administered by the **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** under the **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, Government of India**. Comparison parameters include classification schema, conformity assessment routes, premarket approval pathways, post-market surveillance obligations, quality management system requirements, and clinical evaluation mandates.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like a GLOBAL DRIVING LICENCE SYSTEM:**
> Imagine you have invented a new type of car (your **medical device**). Before you can sell it on any road in the world, you need a "driving test" certificate from the local traffic authority. The **US-FDA** is like the American DMV — extremely strict, takes a long time, classifies cars by risk, and demands exhaustive crash-test reports. The **EU CE Marking** is like the European Union driving licence — you show that your car meets the *essential requirements* (General Safety and Performance Requirements) through a Notified Body who vouches for you. The **Indian CDSCO/MDR 2017** is like the Indian RTO system — newer, evolving, increasingly aligned with global standards, but with its own unique documentation, license numbers (like **MD-9, MD-12, MD-15**), and a "Notified Body" system that is being progressively strengthened under the **Medical Devices Rules, 2017**.

### Key Stakeholder Entities at a Glance

| Framework | Regulatory Body | Governing Law | Year of Current Major Law |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **USA** | **US-FDA (CDRH)** | **FD&C Act + 21 CFR** | 1938 (FD&C Act) / 1976 (MD Amendment) |
| **EU** | **European Commission + National Competent Authorities** | **EU MDR 2017/745** | **2017 (effective May 2021)** |
| **India** | **CDSCO + State Licensing Authorities** | **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** | **2017 (under D&C Act 1940)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to tabulate differences in *device classification, conformity assessment, QMS requirements, clinical evaluation, and post-market surveillance* across the three jurisdictions.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the Three Regulatory Frameworks

### 2.1.1 US-FDA Framework — Structural Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** The US-FDA classifies devices based on **risk and intended use**, and the level of regulatory control increases progressively from Class I to Class III.

- **Governing Body:** US-FDA, Center for Devices and Radiological Health (**CDRH**).
- **Primary Legislation:** **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), 1938** + **Medical Device Amendments, 1976**.
- **Classification System:** **Class I, II, III** (based on risk).
- **Premarket Pathways:** 
  - **510(k) Premarket Notification** — for Class II (substantial equivalence to a predicate device).
  - **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — for Class III (highest risk; clinical data required).
  - **De Novo Classification** — for novel low-to-moderate risk devices without a predicate.
  - **Humanitarian Device Exemption (HDE)** — for rare diseases.
- **Quality System:** **21 CFR Part 820** (Quality System Regulation, QSR) — soon to transition to **21 CFR Part 820 aligned with ISO 13485:2016**.
- **Unique Device Identification (UDI):** **21 CFR Part 830** mandatory.
- **Post-Market Surveillance:** **MDR (Medical Device Reporting) — 21 CFR Part 803**; **Postmarket Surveillance Studies — 522**; **MAUDE database** for adverse events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "510(k)"?** Because the section is in the **Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act, Section 510(k)**. It is NOT a "kilo" weight — it is a *paper submission* proving "substantial equivalence."

### 2.1.2 EU CE Marking under EU MDR 2017/745 — Structural Breakdown

- **Governing Body:** **European Commission** frames the law; **National Competent Authorities (NCAs)** of each member state enforce; **Notified Bodies (NB)** perform conformity assessments.
- **Primary Legislation:** 
  - **EU MDR 2017/745** (replaced MDD 93/42/EEC from **26 May 2021**).
  - **EU IVDR 2017/746** (for in-vitro diagnostics).
- **Classification System:** **Class I, IIa, IIb, III** — based on **Rules 1–22** in Annex VIII of EU MDR.
- **Conformity Assessment Routes (Annexes IX–XI):**
  - **Annex IX** — Quality Management System and Technical Documentation Assessment.
  - **Annex X** — Type Examination (sample-based).
  - **Annex XI** — Production Quality Assurance.
- **Key New Concepts in EU MDR 2017:**
  - **General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR)** — Annex I.
  - **Clinical Evaluation (MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4 & MDR Annex XIV)**.
  - **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** — mandatory.
  - **EUDAMED database** for transparency.
  - **Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC)** — Article 15.
  - **UDI System** (UDI-DI + UDI-PI) per **Article 27**.
  - **Economic Operators** — Manufacturer, Authorised Representative, Importer, Distributor (all regulated).

### 2.1.3 Indian Medical Devices Rules, 2017 — Structural Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **India's MDR 2017 is a watershed moment** — it removed medical devices from the *over-the-counter drug* regulation under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, and created a **separate, dedicated regulatory framework** aligned with **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** principles.

- **Governing Body:** **Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)** under the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare**; **State Licensing Authorities (SLA)** for certain classes.
- **Primary Legislation:** **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** (notified under the **Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, Section 3 & 12**).
- **Classification System (Rule 4):** **Class A (low), B (low-moderate), C (moderate-high), D (high)** — based on **risk and duration of use**.
- **Licensing Structure (Key Forms):**
  - **MD-1** — Application for Grant of Licence (Class A).
  - **MD-2** — Licence to Manufacture (Class A).
  - **MD-3** — Application for Class B/C/D.
  - **MD-5** — Licence to Manufacture (Class B/C/D).
  - **MD-7** — Loan Licence.
  - **MD-9** — Import Licence (Class A).
  - **MD-10** — Licence to Import (Class A).
  - **MD-12** — Wholesale Licence.
  - **MD-14** — Test Licence.
  - **MD-15** — Import Licence (Class B/C/D).
  - **MD-16** — Licence to Import (Class B/C/D).
- **Quality System:** **ISO 13485:2016** mandatory for all classes (Rule 7).
- **Notified Bodies:** Appointed by **CDSCO** for Class C and D devices; SLA for Class A and B.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | **US-FDA** | **EU CE (MDR 2017/745)** | **India (MDR 2017)** |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Regulatory Authority** | US-FDA (CDRH) | European Commission + NCAs | CDSCO + State Licensing Authority |
| **Governing Law** | FD&C Act 1938; 21 CFR | EU MDR 2017/745; IVDR 2017/746 | Medical Devices Rules 2017 under D&C Act 1940 |
| **Classification** | Class I, II, III (low→high) | Class I, IIa, IIb, III (Rules 1–22, Annex VIII) | Class A, B, C, D (Rule 4) |
| **Risk Basis** | Intended use, duration, invasiveness | Vulnerability of body, contact duration, invasiveness | Risk, contact duration, invasiveness |
| **QMS Standard** | 21 CFR Part 820 (→ ISO 13485) | ISO 13485:2016 (mandatory) | ISO 13485:2016 (mandatory) |
| **Conformity Assessment** | 510(k), PMA, De Novo, HDE | Annex IX, X, XI of EU MDR | Audit by NB (C, D) or SLA (A, B) |
| **Premarket Clinical Data** | Required for Class III (PMA) | Required for Class III, implantables, long-term | Required for Class C and D |
| **Technical File** | 510(k) submission / PMA dossier | **Technical Documentation** (Annexes II & III) | **Dossier in Form MD-5 / MD-16** |
| **Authorised Representative** | US Agent (foreign mfr) | Authorised Representative (mandatory, Article 11) | Authorised Agent (Rule 4, Schedule I) |
| **Device Identifier** | UDI (21 CFR 830) | UDI (Article 27, EUDAMED) | UDI (in pipeline, aligned with IMDRF) |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | MDR 21 CFR 803; 522 studies | PMS Plan (Article 84), PSUR (Article 86) | PMS Report (Rule 36), MD-37 |
| **Adverse Event Reporting** | MAUDE database; MDR within 30 days | Vigilance system (Article 87–92) | Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI) |
| **Notified Body Required** | No (FDA audit) | Yes (for IIa, IIb, III) | Yes (for Class C and D) |
| **Labelling Requirements** | 21 CFR Part 801 | Annex I, Chapter III (GSPR 23) | Schedule II of MDR 2017 |
| **Time to Market (Average)** | 6–18 months (510k); 3+ years (PMA) | 6–12 months | 6–9 months (Class A/B); 9–15 months (C/D) |
| **Fees** | High (PMA > $300K) | Moderate (NB-based) | Significantly lower |
| **Unique Document** | 510(k) Premarket Notification | **Declaration of Conformity** | **License in Form MD-5/9/12/15/16** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Exam Pearl):** 
> - **US-FDA = I-II-III (Roman numerals)**
> - **EU = I-IIa-IIb-III (lowercase 'a' and 'b' for II)**
> - **India = A-B-C-D (Alphabets)**
> - All three move from LOW risk to HIGH risk. 

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Symbolic)

While this topic is **regulatory/qualitative**, here are the *logic equations* examiners use to test understanding:

$$
\text{Risk Class} = f(\text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Body Contact}, \text{Energy Source})
$$

$$
\text{Conformity Assessment Burden} \propto \text{Risk Class}
$$

$$
\text{Time to Market} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Quality of Technical File} \times \text{Risk Class}}
$$

$$
\text{Post-Market Cost} \propto \text{Risk Class} \times \text{Market Volume}
$$

$$
\text{Global Market Access} = \text{CE Mark} + \text{FDA Clearance/Approval} + \text{India License} + (\text{PMDA, NMPA, TGA, etc.})
$$

> **Units of Regulatory Burden:** Not in SI units — measured in *man-hours, document pages, audit days, and clinical study patients*.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Comparative Logic — A Step-by-Step Approach

This section systematically derives the **comparative framework** that KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark essay question.

### Step 1 — Identify the Common Anchor

> **The Common Goal of All Three Frameworks:**
> $$\text{Common Goal} = \text{Patient Safety} + \text{Device Efficacy} + \text{Transparent Regulatory Process}$$

All three systems (FDA, CE, India) aim to ensure that:
1. The device is **safe** when used as intended.
2. The device performs as **claimed** by the manufacturer.
3. The manufacturer has a **traceable QMS** for consistent production.
4. There is a **post-market mechanism** to catch problems.

### Step 2 — Derive the Risk Classification Logic

The risk class is determined by four orthogonal axes:

$$
R = f(D, I, C, E)
$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk Class** (categorical, e.g., I/II/III or A/B/C/D).
- $D$ = **Duration of contact** (transient, short-term, long-term, permanent).
- $I$ = **Invasiveness** (non-invasive, body orifice, surgical, implantable).
- $C$ = **Critical body contact** (skin, mucosa, blood, CNS, heart).
- $E$ = **Energy source / biological effect** (passive, active therapeutic, active diagnostic, life-supporting).

> **Derivation Reasoning:**
> - As any of $D, I, C, E$ **increases**, $R$ **increases**.
> - The classification system in each jurisdiction maps this 4-dimensional vector to a single risk class.

#### Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** A **cardiac stent** is an **implantable**, **long-term (>30 days)**, **in contact with blood and heart tissue**, and is a **passive mechanical device**.
> 
> **Solution Logic:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D &= \text{Long-term (implantable)} \Rightarrow \text{High} \\
> I &= \text{Implantable} \Rightarrow \text{High} \\
> C &= \text{Blood + Heart (Critical)} \Rightarrow \text{High} \\
> E &= \text{Passive mechanical} \Rightarrow \text{Low} \\
> \Rightarrow R &= \text{Max}(D,I,C,E) = \text{High Risk}
> \end{aligned}$$
> 
> Therefore:
> - **US-FDA:** Class III (PMA required).
> - **EU CE:** Class III (Annex IX conformity assessment with NB).
> - **India:** Class D (Notified Body audit by CDSCO).

### Step 3 — Derive the Conformity Assessment Pathway

For each jurisdiction, the conformity assessment pathway is derived as:

$$
\text{Pathway} = \begin{cases}
\text{Self-certification (with QMS)} & \text{if } R = \text{Lowest} \\
\text{Notified Body + QMS} & \text{if } R = \text{Medium} \\
\text{Notified Body + QMS + Clinical Investigation + Design Dossier Review} & \text{if } R = \text{Highest}
\end{cases}
$$

Mapping this to the three jurisdictions:

| Risk Level | US-FDA Pathway | EU CE Pathway | India Pathway |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Lowest** | Class I — Most exempt; only 510(k) for sterile/measuring | Class I — Self-declaration; UDI registration | Class A — SLA license (MD-1/MD-2) |
| **Low-Medium** | Class II — 510(k) | Class IIa — NB involvement | Class B — SLA license (MD-3/MD-5) |
| **Med-High** | Class II — 510(k) + Special Controls | Class IIb — NB assessment | Class C — NB audit by CDSCO |
| **Highest** | Class III — PMA | Class III — NB + Design Dossier + Clinical | Class D — NB audit + Clinical Investigation |

### Step 4 — Derive the QMS Equivalence

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The three systems have **converged** on **ISO 13485:2016** as the QMS backbone.

$$
\text{QMS Compliance} = \begin{cases}
\text{21 CFR Part 820} & \text{(US-FDA — transitioning to harmonized ISO 13485)} \\
\text{ISO 13485:2016} & \text{(EU MDR — mandatory)} \\
\text{ISO 13485:2016} & \text{(India MDR 2017 — mandatory under Rule 7)}
\end{cases}
$$

> **Derivation Logic:** The FDA published a proposed rule (Feb 2024) to harmonize 21 CFR 820 with ISO 13485:2016 to reduce manufacturer burden and enable a **single QMS audit** satisfying both US and EU/global requirements.

### Step 5 — Post-Market Surveillance Burden

$$
\text{PMS Burden} = \sum_{i} (\text{Reports per Year} \times \text{Report Complexity}_i) \times \text{Risk Class Weight}
$$

The PMS obligations escalate with risk class:
- **EU MDR 2017:** Class I devices need a PMS report; Class IIa/IIb/III need a **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** updated annually.
- **US-FDA:** Class I — 30-day MDR for serious injuries and deaths; Class III — more rigorous Post-Approval Studies (PAS).
- **India:** All classes — adverse event reporting under **MvPI**; Class C and D manufacturers must submit a PMS report (Rule 36).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — 14-Mark Essay Architecture

For a KTU 14-mark question, the recommended answer architecture is:

$$
\text{Answer} = \underbrace{\text{Introduction}}_{1 \text{ mark}} + \underbrace{3 \text{ Comparative Tables}}_{3 \times 3 = 9 \text{ marks}} + \underbrace{\text{Conclusion}}_{1 \text{ mark}} + \underbrace{\text{Key Differences}}_{3 \text{ marks}}
$$

**Three tables to draw:**
1. **Table 1:** Classification System (US I/II/III vs EU I/IIa/IIb/III vs India A/B/C/D) with **examples**.
2. **Table 2:** Premarket Submission Pathway (510k, PMA, CE, MD Forms).
3. **Table 3:** Post-Market Surveillance (MDR, PSUR, MvPI).
