# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Medical Device Classification as per US-FDA and EU MDR 2017/745. Provide at least three distinguishing points. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition:** The US-FDA classifies medical devices into **Class I, II, and III** based on the level of risk and the level of regulatory control needed to ensure safety and effectiveness. The EU MDR 2017/745 classifies devices into **Class I, IIa, IIb, and III** based on Rules 1–22 in Annex VIII.
>
> **Three Distinguishing Points:**
> 1. **Number of Risk Tiers:** US-FDA has **three tiers** (I, II, III); EU MDR has **four tiers** (I, IIa, IIb, III).
> 2. **Default Assumption:** US-FDA places **most devices in Class I** by default; EU MDR uses **detailed classification rules** (22 rules) with stricter defaults for active devices.
> 3. **Special Categories:** EU MDR explicitly addresses **software (Rule 11)**, **substances introduced into the body (Rule 21)**, and **nanomaterials (Rule 19)**, while US-FDA handles these via guidance documents and 510(k) jurisprudence.

---

### Question 2: List the license forms used in Indian Medical Devices Rules, 2017 for manufacturing and importing Class C and Class D devices. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Manufacturing:**
> 1. **Form MD-3** — Application for grant of licence to manufacture Class C and D devices.
> 2. **Form MD-5** — Licence to manufacture Class C and D devices (for sale or distribution).
>
> **Importing:**
> 1. **Form MD-15** — Application for grant of import licence for Class C and D devices.
> 2. **Form MD-16** — Import licence for Class C and D devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Compare the regulatory frameworks of US-FDA, EU CE Marking under EU MDR 2017/745, and Indian Medical Devices Rules, 2017 with respect to: (a) Risk-based classification system with examples (7 marks) (b) Premarket conformity assessment pathways and Quality Management System requirements (7 marks)."**

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Risk-Based Classification (7 Marks)

The three jurisdictions classify medical devices on a **risk-based approach** where the regulatory burden is directly proportional to the risk class.

**US-FDA Classification (Class I, II, III):**

$$
\text{US-FDA Risk Class} = f(\text{Intended Use}, \text{Indication}, \text{Duration}, \text{Invasiveness})
$$

- **Class I — Low Risk (e.g., elastic bandages, examination gloves, stethoscopes).** Subject to General Controls only. ~47% of devices are Class I, of which ~95% are exempt from 510(k).
- **Class II — Moderate Risk (e.g., infusion pumps, surgical drapes, blood pressure cuffs, pregnancy test kits).** Subject to General Controls **plus Special Controls** (e.g., performance standards, post-market surveillance, labelling). ~43% of devices. Requires 510(k) clearance.
- **Class III — High Risk (e.g., heart valves, coronary stents, implantable pacemakers, silicone gel breast implants).** Subject to General Controls **plus Premarket Approval (PMA)** — the most stringent pathway requiring independent demonstration of safety and effectiveness. ~10% of devices.

**EU MDR 2017/745 Classification (Class I, IIa, IIb, III):**

$$
\text{EU Risk Class} = f(\text{Rule 1–22 in Annex VIII})
$$

- **Class I — Low Risk** (e.g., non-invasive devices like wheelchairs, stethoscopes).
- **Class IIa — Low-to-Moderate Risk** (e.g., hearing aids, short-term contact lenses).
- **Class IIb — Moderate-to-High Risk** (e.g., ventilators, dialysis equipment, surgical lasers).
- **Class III — Highest Risk** (e.g., implantable pacemakers, drug-eluting stents, heart valves, devices with integral medicinal substance).

**Indian MDR 2017 Classification (Class A, B, C, D):**

$$
\text{India Risk Class} = f(\text{Risk}, \text{Invasiveness}, \text{Duration}, \text{Contact})
$$

- **Class A — Low Risk** (e.g., surgical dressings, alcohol swabs).
- **Class B — Low-Moderate Risk** (e.g., hypodermic needles, suction equipment).
- **Class C — Moderate-High Risk** (e.g., orthopedic implants, ventilators).
- **Class D — High Risk** (e.g., heart valves, coronary stents, implantable defibrillators).

**Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | US-FDA | EU CE | India |
|--------|--------|-------|-------|
| Tiers | 3 (I, II, III) | 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III) | 4 (A, B, C, D) |
| Naming | Roman Numerals | Roman + Letter | Alphabets |
| Example of high risk | Heart valve (III) | Heart valve (III) | Heart valve (D) |

> All three frameworks **converge on a risk-based philosophy** and **align globally through IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum).

#### Part (b) — Premarket Conformity Assessment and QMS (7 Marks)

**US-FDA Premarket Pathways:**

$$
\text{US Premarket} = \begin{cases}
\text{Exempt} & \text{Class I (most)} \\
\text{510(k)} & \text{Class II — Substantial Equivalence} \\
\text{De Novo} & \text{Novel low-moderate risk} \\
\text{PMA} & \text{Class III — Safety + Effectiveness} \\
\text{HDE} & \text{Humanitarian Use Device (rare diseases)}
\end{cases}
$$

**EU MDR 2017/745 Conformity Assessment Routes:**

$$
\text{EU Conformity} = \begin{cases}
\text{Annex IV Self-Declaration} & \text{Class I (non-sterile, non-measuring)} \\
\text{Annex IX + X or XI} & \text{Class IIa} \\
\text{Annex IX (QMS + Tech Doc)} & \text{Class IIb} \\
\text{Annex IX + X (Type Exam) + Clinical} & \text{Class III}
\end{cases}
$$

**India MDR 2017 Pathways:**

$$
\text{India Conformity} = \begin{cases}
\text{SLA Audit} & \text{Class A, B} \\
\text{CDSCO Notified Body Audit} & \text{Class C, D}
\end{cases}
$$

**Quality Management System (QMS) Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Jurisdiction | QMS Standard | Audit Body |
|--------------|-------------|------------|
| US-FDA | **21 CFR Part 820** (transitioning to ISO 13485:2016) | FDA Inspectors |
| EU CE | **ISO 13485:2016** (mandatory) | Notified Body |
| India | **ISO 13485:2016** (mandatory under Rule 7) | Notified Body / SLA |

> **Common Thread:** All three frameworks have **converged on ISO 13485:2016** as the *de facto* global QMS standard. The US-FDA's 2024 harmonization rule reduces redundancy for manufacturers.

**Conclusion:** While the three frameworks are philosophically aligned (risk-based, QMS-driven, post-market vigilance), they differ in **classification nomenclature, conformity assessment routes, audit bodies, and document requirements**. A manufacturer seeking global market access must satisfy the **highest common denominator** — typically **ISO 13485:2016 + EU MDR Technical Documentation + Clinical Evaluation + US-FDA 510(k) or PMA + India License**. (1 Mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Explain in detail the post-market surveillance (PMS) requirements under: (a) US-FDA's Medical Device Reporting (MDR) and MAUDE database system (7 marks) (b) EU MDR 2017/745 PMS and PSUR, and India's MvPI and PMS report under Rule 36 (7 marks)."**

### **Solution:**

#### Part (a) — US-FDA PMS System (7 Marks)

**Legal Basis:** **21 CFR Part 803** (Medical Device Reporting, MDR) and **21 CFR Part 822** (Postmarket Surveillance).

**Who Must Report:**
- **Manufacturers** — must report deaths, serious injuries, and malfunctions.
- **Importers** — must report deaths and serious injuries, and certain malfunctions.
- **Device User Facilities (Hospitals, Ambulatory Surgical Centers)** — must report deaths to FDA and manufacturer; serious injuries to manufacturer.

**Reporting Timelines:**

$$
\text{US-FDA MDR Timeline} = \begin{cases}
30 \text{ calendar days} & \text{Standard event} \\
5 \text{ working days} & \text{Event requiring remedial action to prevent risk} \\
\text{Immediately} & \text{Public health emergency}
\end{cases}
$$

**MAUDE Database (Manufacturer and User Facility Device Experience):**
- **Publicly searchable database** updated monthly.
- Contains reports of adverse events involving medical devices.
- Manufacturers submit **30-day reports**; user facilities submit within **10 working days** for deaths.

**522 Postmarket Surveillance Studies:**
- FDA can order a manufacturer to conduct a **Post-Approval Study (PAS)** for Class II or III devices when:
  - Device failure could cause serious adverse health consequences.
  - Device is intended to be implanted in the body for more than 1 year.
  - Device is intended to be a life-sustaining or life-supporting device.

**Conclusion (US side):** The US-FDA PMS is **reactive and event-based**, with strong emphasis on **public transparency** (MAUDE) and **risk-based escalation** (522 studies). (1 Mark)

#### Part (b) — EU MDR 2017/745 and India MvPI (7 Marks)

**EU MDR 2017/745 PMS Framework:**

$$
\text{EU PMS} = \text{PMS Plan (Article 84)} + \text{PMS Report or PSUR (Article 86)} + \text{Vigilance Reporting (Article 87-92)}
$$

- **PMS Plan (Article 84):** Mandatory for all devices; describes how the manufacturer will collect, analyze, and act on post-market data.
- **PMS Report (Class I):** Prepared when there is no Notified Body; updated when needed.
- **PSUR — Periodic Safety Update Report (Class IIa, IIb, III):**
  - **Class IIa:** Updated every 2 years.
  - **Class IIb:** Updated annually.
  - **Class III:** Updated **annually**, and submitted to the Notified Body for assessment.
- **Vigilance Reporting (Article 87–92):** Serious incidents and Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCAs) must be reported to the **competent authority** of the member state where the incident occurred, within:
  - **15 days** for serious incidents.
  - **10 days** for serious public health threats.
- **EUDAMED Database:** Publicly accessible database for registration, UDI, certificates, clinical investigations, vigilance, and PMS.

**Indian PMS Framework (MDR 2017 + MvPI):**

$$
\text{India PMS} = \text{MvPI Reporting} + \text{PMS Report (Rule 36)}
$$

- **Materiovigilance Programme of India (MvPI):** Launched in 2015 by **Indian Pharmacopoeia Commission (IPC)** under the **Ministry of Health**. Medical device adverse events are reported by healthcare professionals, manufacturers, and patients through a **VigiFlow** system.
- **Rule 36 — PMS Report:** Manufacturers of **Class C and D** devices must submit a PMS report to CDSCO.
- **MD-37 Form:** Used for reporting adverse events related to medical devices in India.
- **Reporting Timeline:** As per MvPI guidelines — serious adverse events within **15 calendar days**.

**Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | US-FDA | EU MDR 2017 | India (MDR 2017 + MvPI) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|-------------------------|
| Reporting Timeline (Serious) | 30 days (MDR) | 15 days (Vigilance) | 15 days (MvPI) |
| Public Database | MAUDE | EUDAMED | MvPI database |
| Periodic Report | 522 Studies | PSUR (Class IIa/IIb/III) | PMS Report (Class C/D) |
| Person Responsible | US Agent | PRRC (Art. 15) | Authorised Indian Agent |

**Conclusion:** All three PMS systems emphasize **timely reporting of adverse events, manufacturer accountability, and public transparency**, but differ in their **legal formality, reporting timelines, and database integration**. (1 Mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing 510(k) with PMA:** Many students write 510(k) for Class III devices. **510(k) is for Class II (most cases); PMA is mandatory for Class III.**
> 2. **Mixing up EU Class I, IIa, IIb, III:** Roman numerals + lowercase letters. **Class I (low) → IIa (low-mod) → IIb (mod-high) → III (high).**
> 3. **Confusing India MD Forms:** MD-5 is for *manufacture* of Class C/D; MD-16 is for *import* of Class C/D. Many students interchange them.
> 4. **Forgetting ISO 13485:2016:** All three frameworks have converged on this QMS standard — examiners expect explicit mention.
> 5. **Omitting Post-Market Surveillance:** Many students write only about pre-market steps. PMS carries **30–40% weight** in any comparison question.
> 6. **Not giving examples:** A comparison table without examples (e.g., "heart valve = Class III/D") loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Three regulatory authorities:** **US-FDA (CDRH)**, **European Commission + NCAs + Notified Bodies**, **CDSCO + State Licensing Authorities (India)**.
- **Three governing laws:** **FD&C Act 1938 / 21 CFR**, **EU MDR 2017/745**, **Medical Devices Rules, 2017 (under D&C Act 1940)**.
- **Three classification systems:** **I/II/III (US)**, **I/IIa/IIb/III (EU)**, **A/B/C/D (India)**.
- **Key US-FDA pathways:** **510(k), PMA, De Novo, HDE**.
- **Key EU conformity routes:** **Annexes IX, X, XI of EU MDR 2017/745**.
- **Key India Forms:** **MD-3, MD-5 (manufacture C/D)**, **MD-15, MD-16 (import C/D)**, **MD-9, MD-10 (import A)**, **MD-1, MD-2 (manufacture A)**.
- **Common QMS Standard:** **ISO 13485:2016** (mandatory in EU and India; transitioning in US).
- **Post-Market Surveillance:** US = **MDR (21 CFR 803) + MAUDE**, EU = **PSUR + EUDAMED + Vigilance**, India = **MvPI + Rule 36 PMS Report**.
- **Reporting Timelines:** US = 30 days, EU = 15 days, India = 15 days (serious events).
- **UDI System:** US (21 CFR 830), EU (Article 27), India (in pipeline, IMDRF-aligned).
- **Notified Body:** Required in EU for Class IIa/IIb/III; in India for Class C and D; **NOT** required by US-FDA (FDA itself audits).
- **Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC):** New concept under **EU MDR Article 15** — no direct US/India equivalent, but **US Agent** and **Authorised Indian Agent** serve similar roles.
- **Global Convergence:** All three frameworks are converging through **IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) and **GHTF** legacy principles.
- **Example Mapping (Heart Valve):** US Class III (PMA) | EU Class III (Annex IX + Clinical) | India Class D (CDSCO NB + Clinical Investigation).
- **Example Mapping (Surgical Gloves):** US Class I (Exempt) | EU Class I (Self-certify) | India Class A (SLA License).
- **Highest cost & time burden:** US-FDA PMA (3+ years, >$300K).
- **Newest framework:** India MDR 2017 + EU MDR 2017/745 (both 2017-era, showing global alignment trend).

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** A 14-mark question on this topic **always expects a comparative table**. Draw at least **3 tables** (Classification, Premarket Pathway, PMS) and **give one example device** for each risk class to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device risk classification, registration, clinical trials, premarket notification, pre-market approval (PMA), investigational device exemption (IDE) and in-vitro diagnostics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Device risk classification, registration, clinical trials, premarket notification, pre-market approval (PMA), investigational device exemption (IDE) and in-vitro diagnostics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Medical Device Risk Classification (FDA Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (21 CFR Part 860):** The U.S. FDA classifies medical devices into **three regulatory classes (Class I, II, III)** based on the **level of risk** they pose to the patient and the user, the **intended use**, and the **indications for use**. The classification determines the type of regulatory submission required prior to marketing.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Light" Model
Imagine medical devices as vehicles on a road. **Class I** is like a **bicycle** — low risk, simple design, no license plate needed (just basic registration). **Class II** is like a **car** — moderate risk, requires a driver's test and registration (analogous to **510(k) Premarket Notification**). **Class III** is like a **commercial aircraft** — high risk, requires exhaustive engineering certification, flight testing, and federal aviation approval (analogous to **Premarket Approval / PMA**). The more harm the device can inflict, the stricter the regulatory gauntlet.

| Class | Risk Level | Analogy | Example Devices |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Class I** | **Low Risk** | Bicycle (basic registration) | Tongue depressors, elastic bandages, stethoscopes |
| **Class II** | **Moderate Risk** | Car (needs driver's test) | Blood pressure cuffs, infusion pumps, surgical gloves |
| **Class III** | **High Risk** | Aircraft (full certification) | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, coronary stents |

> [!NOTE]
> Approximately **47%** of all medical devices fall under **Class I**, **43%** under **Class II**, and only **10%** under **Class III**, reflecting the inverse relationship between risk and device prevalence.

---

## 1.2 Establishment Registration & Device Listing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Under **21 CFR Part 807**, every manufacturer, contract manufacturer, specification developer, contract sterilizer, repackager, and relabeler of medical devices marketed in the U.S. must **annually register** their establishment with the FDA and **list** the devices being marketed. This is a *prerequisite* but does **NOT** constitute marketing approval.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of this as the device's **"birth certificate and annual address update"** with the government. Before you sell a product, the FDA needs to know *who you are*, *where you are*, and *what exactly you are selling*. It does **not** mean the product is approved — just that the seller is on record.

---

## 1.3 Clinical Trials & Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (21 CFR Part 812):** An **Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)** is a regulatory submission that allows an unapproved medical device to be used in a **clinical investigation** (clinical trial) on human subjects to collect **safety and effectiveness data** in support of a future **PMA** or **510(k)** submission.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a new aircraft prototype. Before commercial passenger flights, it must undergo **test flights** with specialized crews. The **IDE** is the FAA's permission slip to conduct those test flights. Patients enrolled in the trial are like the test pilots — informed, monitored, and protected by **Informed Consent** and **IRB (Institutional Review Board)** oversight.

Two IDE pathways exist:
- **Significant Risk (SR) Device** → Full IDE application to FDA
- **Non-Significant Risk (NSR) Device** → Abbreviated IDE (IRB approval only)

---

## 1.4 Premarket Notification — 510(k)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (21 CFR Part 807 Subpart E):** A **510(k)** is a premarket submission to the FDA demonstrating that the device to be marketed is **at least as safe and effective** (i.e., **substantially equivalent**) to a legally marketed device that is **not subject to PMA** (the "predicate device").

### Conceptual Analogy
The 510(k) is like a **"me-too" patent application**. Instead of proving your car is revolutionary, you just prove it has the **same critical features and safety profile** as an existing model. The original model is the "predicate." No new clinical trial is required *if* equivalence is established — only engineering and bench testing.

> [!CAUTION]
> The 510(k) pathway does **NOT** apply to **Class III** devices, and it requires no original clinical evidence in many cases — a fact that has been heavily criticized in high-profile device failures.

---

## 1.5 Premarket Approval (PMA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (21 CFR Part 814):** **Premarket Approval (PMA)** is the FDA's most stringent device marketing authorization process. It requires **independent demonstration** of the device's **safety and effectiveness** through valid **scientific evidence**, typically including **clinical trial data**, manufacturing information, and labeling.

### Conceptual Analogy
Unlike 510(k), PMA is like a **"ground-up" design and crash-test certification**. You must independently prove the aircraft can fly safely, carry passengers, and withstand emergencies — no "similar aircraft" shortcuts. The FDA review takes **180 days minimum** and the approval rate is historically around **80%** for those that reach a final decision.

---

## 1.6 In-Vitro Diagnostics (IVD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (21 CFR Part 809):** An **In-Vitro Diagnostic (IVD)** product is a reagent, instrument, or system intended for use in the **diagnosis of disease or other conditions**, including the determination of the state of health, in order to **cure, mitigate, treat, or prevent disease**. These products are reagents, instruments, and systems intended for use in the *diagnosis of disease or other conditions, including a determination of the state of health, in order to cure, mitigate, treat, or prevent disease or its sequelae*.

### Conceptual Analogy
An IVD is the device's **"detective sidekick."** It doesn't treat the patient — it *investigates* the sample (blood, urine, tissue) to give the doctor a clue. Think of a **pregnancy test strip**, a **COVID-19 PCR test**, or a **blood glucose meter** — all IVDs.

> [!NOTE]
> IVDs are classified under a **separate risk-based framework (21 CFR 866)** with categories such as **Class I (low), Class II (moderate), Class III (high)**, plus a special **LDT (Laboratory Developed Test)** category historically regulated under **CLIA** rather than FDA pre-market review.

---

## 1.7 Relationship Map: The Five Pathways at a Glance

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "R-C-PI²"** → **R**egistration → **C**linical Trials (IDE) → **P**remarket **N**otification (510k) → **P**remarket **A**pproval (PMA) → **I**n-**V**itro **D**iagnostics.
> This is the lifecycle order from initial establishment through final market clearance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Risk Classification Criteria (21 CFR Part 860)

The FDA assigns a device to Class I, II, or III based on **seven statutory panels** and the following eight criteria:

1. **Intended use** of the device
2. **Indications for use** (specific disease/condition)
3. **Duration of contact** with the body (transient ≤ 60 min, short term ≤ 30 days, long term > 30 days)
4. **Degree of invasiveness** (non-invasive, invasive, implantable)
5. **Body system affected** (cardiovascular, neurological, etc.)
6. **Energy delivered** to the patient (electrical, thermal, radiation, mechanical)
7. **Whether the device is life-sustaining or life-supporting**
8. **Whether the device is critical for preventing impairment of health**

### Classification Logic Flow

- **Class I** — Devices for which *general controls* (registration, listing, GMP, labeling, MDR reporting) are *sufficient* to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness.
- **Class II** — Devices for which *general controls alone* are *insufficient* but **Special Controls** (performance standards, post-market surveillance, guidance documents, special labeling) provide reasonable assurance.
- **Class III** — Devices that *support or sustain human life*, are *of substantial importance in preventing impairment of health*, or which *present a potential, unreasonable risk of illness or injury* → require **PMA**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — Regulatory Pathways at a Glance

| Parameter | 510(k) | PMA | IDE |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Statutory Basis** | FD&C Act §510(k) | FD&C Act §515 | 21 CFR Part 812 |
| **Class Eligibility** | Class I, II (most) | Class III (most) | All classes (if investigational) |
| **Clinical Data Required** | Usually NO (predicate-based) | YES — mandatory clinical study | Required for SR devices |
| **Review Time (FDA)** | 90 days (statutory) | 180 days (statutory) | 30 days (statutory) |
| **Approval Mechanism** | Substantial Equivalence | Safety + Effectiveness | Permission to investigate |
| **User Fee (FY2024 est.)** | $54,517 (standard) | $483,560 (standard) | $0 (no fee) |
| **Outcome** | "Cleared" | "Approved" | "IDE authorized" |
| **Predicate Requirement** | YES (legally marketed) | NO | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verbs Matter in KTU Exams:** FDA "**clears**" a 510(k), "**approves**" a PMA, and "**authorizes**" an IDE. Using the wrong verb in your exam answer will cost you marks.

---

## 2.3 Substantial Equivalence Decision Tree (510(k))

The 510(k) reviewer applies a **three-step test**:

$$\text{SE Test} = \begin{cases} \text{Pass} & \text{if Predicate Exists} \wedge \text{Same Intended Use} \wedge \text{Same Technological Characteristics} \\ \text{Pass with Limits} & \text{if New Tech} \wedge \text{No New Safety Questions Raised} \\ \text{Fail (Not SE)} & \text{if Predicate Missing} \vee \text{New Intended Use} \vee \text{Unacceptable Risk} \end{cases}$$

If the device is **NOT substantially equivalent (NSE)**, the manufacturer has two options:
1. Submit a **De Novo Classification Request** (down-classify to Class I/II with no predicate)
2. File a **PMA** if Class III status is unavoidable

---

## 2.4 IDE Risk Determination — Significant Risk vs. Non-Significant Risk

| Criterion | Significant Risk (SR) | Non-Significant Risk (NSR) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Sponsor Responsibility** | Full IDE application to FDA | IRB approval only |
| **FDA Approval** | Required **before** study begins | **NOT** required |
| **Informed Consent** | Required (21 CFR Part 50) | Required (21 CFR Part 50) |
| **IRB Approval** | Required | Required |
| **Examples** | Implantable pacemakers, heart valves | Contact lens cleaning solution trials, certain dental devices |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse "Significant Risk" with "Class III." These are *independent* concepts. A Class II device can be a Significant Risk device if the protocol is invasive (e.g., a surgical laser study).

---

## 2.5 IVD Risk Classification Framework

IVDs follow a **risk-based classification** under **21 CFR 809.30** and the **FDA's IVD Classification Panel (21 CFR 866)**:

| IVD Class | Risk | Example | Regulatory Pathway |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Class I** | Low | Blood collection tubes, general culture media | 510(k) exempt (most) |
| **Class II** | Moderate | HbA1c test kits, pregnancy tests (OTC) | 510(k) required |
| **Class III** | High | HIV diagnostic kits, cancer biomarkers, COVID-19 PCR | **PMA** or **De Novo** |
| **LDT** | Variable | In-house hospital lab tests | CLIA-regulated (historically) |

> [!NOTE]
> Since the **VALID Act (Verifying Accurate, Leading-edge IVCT Development Act)** proposal and the **FDA's 2024 LDT Final Rule**, Laboratory Developed Tests are being brought under the full FDA medical device framework, ending the historical CLIA-only oversight era.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A **biomedical engineer** designing a new surgical robot must decide *before prototyping* whether the device will require 510(k) (if a predicate exists, e.g., the Da Vinci system) or PMA (if novel AI-driven control algorithms create new risk profiles).
- A **clinical research coordinator** must know IDE requirements to lawfully enroll patients in investigational studies.
- A **regulatory affairs specialist** uses the classification framework to estimate **time-to-market** (510(k) ≈ 6–12 months; PMA ≈ 18–36 months) and **development cost** (PMA clinical trial: $20M–$100M+).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Process Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrices

## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Bringing a New Medical Device to U.S. Market

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question framework**. Memorize these steps.

### Step 1: Device Classification
- Identify the **device panel** (e.g., Cardiovascular, Orthopedic, IVD).
- Match against **21 CFR Parts 862–892** to determine the correct **Class (I, II, or III)**.

### Step 2: Choose the Regulatory Pathway
Apply the decision logic:

$$\text{Pathway} = \begin{cases} \text{510(k)} & \text{if Class I/II} \wedge \text{Predicate Available} \\ \text{De Novo} & \text{if Class I/II} \wedge \text{No Predicate} \wedge \text{Low-Moderate Risk} \\ \text{PMA} & \text{if Class III} \vee \text{No Predicate} \wedge \text{High Risk} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Pre-Submission (Pre-Sub) Meeting (Optional but recommended)
- Submit a **Q-Submission (Pre-Sub)** to FDA to obtain **non-binding feedback** on testing strategy and regulatory pathway.

### Step 4: Compliance with Quality System Regulation (QSR) / 21 CFR Part 820
- Establish **Design Controls**, **Document Controls**, **CAPA**, **MDR reporting** — applies to **all classes** except certain Class I exemptions.

### Step 5: Bench Testing & Biocompatibility (ISO 10993)
- Conduct **material characterization**, **sterility testing (ISO 11137)**, **shelf life**, and **packaging validation**.

### Step 6: Clinical Evaluation
- For **PMA devices**: Conduct a full **clinical investigation under an IDE**.
- For **510(k) devices**: Generally not required, but may be needed if the predicate has known safety concerns or if new technological features raise new questions.

### Step 7: Submit the Marketing Application
- 510(k) → FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH) review → **Substantial Equivalence determination** → **510(k) Clearance Letter**.

### Step 8: Post-Market Obligations
- **Medical Device Reporting (MDR)** under 21 CFR Part 803
- **Post-Approval Studies (PAS)** for PMA devices
- **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** under 21 CFR Part 830
- **Adverse Event Tracking** and **Recall procedures**

---

## 3.2 Detailed PMA Approval Process — The Five-Phase Flow

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 1: Pre-Clinical} &\rightarrow \text{Bench, Animal, Biocompatibility Testing} \\
\text{Phase 2: IDE Authorized} &\rightarrow \text{FDA issues IDE approval letter (30-day cycle)} \\
\text{Phase 3: Pivotal Trial Conducted} &\rightarrow \text{Enroll, treat, follow subjects, collect data} \\
\text{Phase 4: PMA Submission} &\rightarrow \text{Original PMA or Modular PMA filed} \\
\text{Phase 5: FDA Review} &\rightarrow \text{180-day clock} \rightarrow \text{Approval or Denial}
\end{aligned}$$

### PMA Submission Document Contents (21 CFR 814.20)

1. **Cover letter** and **table of contents**
2. **Indications for use** statement
3. **Device description** and **materials**
4. **Manufacturing information** and **sterilization protocols**
5. **Performance testing** (bench, animal, biocompatibility)
6. **Software documentation** (if applicable, IEC 62304)
7. **Clinical study reports** (statistical analysis, primary endpoint)
8. **Risk analysis** (ISO 14971)
9. **Proposed labeling** (IFU, warnings, contraindications)
10. **Financial certification** and **environmental assessment**
11. **User fee cover sheet** (Form FDA 3601A)

---

## 3.3 IDE Process — Detailed Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Sponsor Determines Risk Classification (SR vs. NSR)

The sponsor must first ask: *"Does this device present a potential for serious risk to the health, safety, or welfare of a subject?"*

- **YES → Significant Risk → Full IDE application required**
- **NO → Non-Significant Risk → IRB approval only**

> [!NOTE]
> The **IRB** also makes an independent SR/NSR determination. If the IRB disagrees with the sponsor and decides the device is SR, then a full IDE must be filed.

### Step 2: Prepare the IDE Application (21 CFR 812.20)

The IDE submission includes:
- **Report of prior investigations** (bench, animal, foreign clinical)
- **Investigational plan** (protocol, risk analysis, monitoring procedures)
- **Manufacturing information** (sterilization, packaging, expiration)
- **Investigator's Brochure**
- **IRB approval letter** (or pending status)
- **Informed consent form** (per 21 CFR Part 50)
- **Labeling** (must clearly state "CAUTION — Investigational Device. Limited by Federal (or U.S.) law to investigational use.")

### Step 3: FDA Review (30-day Statutory Period)
$$\text{FDA Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Approved} & \text{No issues within 30 days} \\ \text{Disapproved} & \text{FDA sends disapproval letter; clinical study cannot begin} \\ \text{Approved with Conditions} & \text{Study may proceed under specified conditions} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4: Conduct the Clinical Study
- Enroll subjects under **IRB oversight**
- **Monitor adverse events** within 10 working days for **unanticipated adverse device effects (UADEs)**
- Maintain **device accountability** records (every device must be tracked from receipt to disposition)
- **Annual progress reports** to FDA and IRB

### Step 5: Study Completion
- **Final report** filed with FDA within 6 months of study completion
- If PMA is planned, IDE data is incorporated into the **PMA submission**

---

## 3.4 The 510(k) Substantial Equivalence Test — Worked Example

**Scenario:** A company designs a new **automated external defibrillator (AED)**. They want to market it as a Class III device via 510(k). Is this possible?

### Analysis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Class Identification} &\rightarrow \text{AEDs are Class III per 21 CFR 870.5310} \\
\text{Step 2: Pathway Eligibility} &\rightarrow \text{Class III cannot use 510(k) (statutory bar)} \\
\text{Step 3: Required Pathway} &\rightarrow \text{PMA} \\
\text{Conclusion} &\rightarrow \text{The 510(k) pathway is NOT available}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Trap Question:** Students often think "all devices can use 510(k) if they have a predicate." This is FALSE. Most **Class III** devices are **pre-amendment devices** subject to PMA regardless of predicate availability (per FD&C Act §515(b)(1)).

---

## 3.5 IVD Regulatory Decision Matrix

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{IVD Risk} &\rightarrow \text{Determine if device is for:} \\
&\quad \text{(a) Critical disease (cancer, HIV) → Class III} \\
&\quad \text{(b) Routine diagnosis (glucose, pregnancy) → Class II} \\
&\quad \text{(c) General lab use (buffers, controls) → Class I} \\
\text{Pathway} &\rightarrow \text{Apply 510(k) / PMA / De Novo logic as above} \\
\text{LDT Status} &\rightarrow \text{Verify if in-house test → 2024 FDA LDT rule applies}
\end{aligned}$$

### Worked Example: COVID-19 PCR Test
- **Intended Use:** Detection of SARS-CoV-2 viral RNA in nasal swabs
- **Risk:** High — drives critical treatment decisions for a life-threatening disease
- **Classification:** **Class III**
- **Pathway:** **PMA** or **De Novo** (during public health emergency, FDA issued **Emergency Use Authorizations (EUAs)** as a temporary alternative pathway)

---

## 3.6 Comparative Cost & Timeline Matrix for KTU Exam Readiness

| Pathway | Avg. Cost (USD) | Timeline | Clinical Data | Success Rate |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Class I Exempt** | $5K – $50K | 1–3 months | No | ~99% |
| **510(k)** | $30K – $500K | 6–12 months | Usually No | ~85% |
| **De Novo** | $1M – $5M | 12–24 months | Sometimes | ~70% |
| **PMA** | $20M – $100M+ | 18–60 months | Yes (mandatory) | ~80% |

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix in **Part B 14-mark questions** when asked *"Compare the regulatory pathways in terms of cost, time, and clinical evidence."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FDA Risk Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Medical Device] --> B{Is it life-sustaining,<br/>life-supporting, or<br/>prevents health impairment?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Class III<br/>High Risk]
    B -- No --> D{Can general controls<br/>alone ensure safety?}
    D -- No --> E[Class II<br/>Moderate Risk]
    D -- Yes --> F[Class I<br/>Low Risk]
    C --> G[PMA Required]
    E --> H[510k Required]
    F --> I[510k Exempt or<br/>Minimal Controls]
    G --> J[Clinical Data<br/>Mandatory]
    H --> K[Substantial<br/>Equivalence Test]
    I --> L[Registration and Listing<br/>Only]
```

## 4.2 510(k) vs PMA Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Determine Device Class] --> B{Is device Class III?}
    B -- Yes --> C[PMA Pathway]
    B -- No --> D{Is a legally marketed<br/>predicate available?}
    D -- Yes --> E[510k Pathway]
    D -- No --> F{Risk profile}
    F -- Low to Moderate --> G[De Novo Request]
    F -- High --> H[PMA Pathway]
    E --> I[Submit 510k<br/>90-day review]
    G --> J[Submit De Novo<br/>120-day review]
    C --> K[Submit PMA<br/>180-day review]
    I --> L{Cleared?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Market Device]
    L -- No --> N[File De Novo or PMA]
    J --> O{Granted?}
    O -- Yes --> M
    O -- No --> P[Reclassify or PMA]
    K --> Q{Approved?}
    Q -- Yes --> M
    Q -- No --> R[Address Deficiencies]
```

## 4.3 IDE Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sponsor Identifies<br/>Investigational Device] --> B{Is device<br/>Significant Risk?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Prepare Full IDE Application]
    B -- No --> D[Abbreviated IDE<br/>IRB Review Only]
    C --> E[Submit to FDA<br/>21 CFR 812.20]
    E --> F{30-day FDA Review}
    F -- Approved --> G[Begin Clinical Study]
    F -- Disapproved --> H[Study Cannot Begin]
    G --> I[Conduct Pivotal Trial]
    I --> J[Monitor Adverse Events<br/>UADE in 10 days]
    J --> K[File Final Report<br/>6 months post-completion]
    K --> L[Incorporate Data into<br/>510k or PMA]
    D --> M[IRB Reviews Protocol]
    M --> N{IRB Approval?}
    N -- Yes --> G
    N -- No --> H
```

## 4.4 IVD Classification & Pathway Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IVD Product] --> B{Determines<br/>Critical Care Decision?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Class III<br/>High Risk]
    B -- No --> D{Patient vs.<br/>Provider Use?}
    D -- Patient OTC --> E[Class II<br/>Most Likely]
    D -- Lab Professional --> F{Routine or<br/>Specialty Test?}
    F -- Routine --> E
    F -- Specialty --> C
    E --> G[510k Pathway]
    C --> H[PMA Pathway]
    G --> I[Market as Cleared]
    H --> J[Market as Approved]
    C --> K[Alternative: LDT<br/>under CLIA]
    K --> L[FDA 2024 Final Rule<br/>Brings LDT under FDA]
```

## 4.5 Complete Device Lifecycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Concept and Design] --> A2[Risk Classification]
    A2 --> A3[Pathway Selection]
    A3 --> A4{QSR Compliance<br/>21 CFR 820}
    A4 --> A5[Bench and Animal Testing]
    A5 --> A6{IDE Required?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Clinical Trial]
    A6 -- No --> A8[Direct Submission]
    A7 --> A9[510k or PMA Submission]
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10{FDA Decision}
    A10 -- Cleared --> A11[Market Launch]
    A10 -- Approved --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Post-Market Surveillance<br/>MDR and UDI]
    A12 --> A13[Annual Registration Renewal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between Premarket Notification (510(k)) and Premarket Approval (PMA) with respect to the device classes, clinical data requirement, and FDA review timeline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 510(k) | PMA |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Class Eligibility** | Class I and II (mostly) | Class III (mostly) |
| **Clinical Data** | Usually NOT required | Mandatory |
| **Review Time** | 90 days (statutory) | 180 days (statutory) |
| **Outcome** | Cleared | Approved |

510(k) is a *substantial equivalence* pathway demonstrating that the new device is as safe and effective as a legally marketed predicate device. PMA is the most stringent pathway requiring independent demonstration of safety and effectiveness through valid scientific evidence, including clinical trials, manufacturing data, and labeling review. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is an Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)? When is it required?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **IDE** is a regulatory submission under **21 CFR Part 812** that permits an *unapproved* medical device to be used in a **clinical investigation** on human subjects to collect **safety and effectiveness data** in support of a future marketing application (510(k) or PMA).

**When required:**
- A significant-risk (SR) device being studied to determine safety/effectiveness must obtain FDA IDE authorization **before** initiating the clinical study.
- Non-significant-risk (NSR) devices only require IRB approval, not FDA submission.
- Devices that are *already legally marketed* and being studied for a **new indication** may also need an IDE if the new use introduces significant risk. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **FDA medical device classification system** in detail. Discuss the criteria used, the level of regulatory control for each class, and provide **two examples** of devices in each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Premarket Notification [510(k)] process** in detail. Explain the concept of **substantial equivalence**, the predicate device requirement, the contents of a 510(k) submission, and the FDA decision outcomes. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — FDA Medical Device Classification (7 marks)

The FDA classifies medical devices into **three regulatory classes** based on the level of risk and the controls necessary to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness.

**Classification Criteria (21 CFR Part 860):**
1. **Intended use** and **indications for use**
2. **Duration of contact** with the body (transient, short-term, long-term)
3. **Degree of invasiveness** (non-invasive, invasive, implantable)
4. **Energy delivered** to the patient
5. **Whether life-sustaining or life-supporting**
6. **Body system affected**

**Class I — Low Risk (1 mark)**
- General controls are sufficient (registration, listing, GMP, MDR, labeling).
- **Examples:** Tongue depressors, elastic bandages, stethoscopes, examination gloves.

**Class II — Moderate Risk (1 mark)**
- General controls + **Special Controls** (performance standards, post-market surveillance, special labeling, guidance documents).
- **Examples:** Blood pressure cuffs, infusion pumps, surgical drapes, powered wheelchairs.

**Class III — High Risk (1 mark)**
- General controls + Special Controls are insufficient; **Premarket Approval (PMA)** is required.
- **Examples:** Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, HIV diagnostic tests, coronary stents.

The classification framework ensures that the **regulatory burden is proportional to the risk** posed by the device, balancing patient safety with innovation. **Conclusion (1 mark)**

#### Part (b) — Premarket Notification 510(k) (7 marks)

The **510(k)** is regulated under **21 CFR Part 807 Subpart E**.

**Substantial Equivalence (3 marks):**
A device is "substantially equivalent" (SE) to a predicate when it has:
- The **same intended use** as the predicate, AND
- The **same technological characteristics**, OR
- Different technological characteristics that do **not raise new questions of safety and effectiveness**, and the sponsor demonstrates that the device is at least as safe and effective as the predicate.

**Predicate Device Requirement (1 mark):**
A legally marketed device that was either (a) cleared via 510(k), (b) legally marketed before May 28, 1976, or (c) reclassified from Class III to Class II/I. The predicate serves as the **comparator benchmark**.

**510(k) Submission Contents (2 marks):**
- Cover letter and indications for use statement
- Device description and comparison to predicate
- Performance testing data (bench, biocompatibility, sterilization)
- Software documentation (if applicable)
- Proposed labeling
- 510(k) cover sheet (Form FDA 3514)
- User fee cover sheet (Form FDA 3601)

**FDA Decision Outcomes (1 mark):**
- **Substantially Equivalent (SE):** Device is cleared for marketing
- **Not Substantially Equivalent (NSE):** Sponsor may file De Novo or PMA

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **Investigational Device Exemption (IDE)** process. Explain the difference between **Significant Risk (SR)** and **Non-Significant Risk (NSR)** devices, and outline the steps involved in obtaining IDE authorization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Premarket Approval (PMA)** pathway. Describe the PMA submission contents, FDA review timeline, and the role of clinical data. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — IDE Process (7 marks)

An **IDE** is a regulatory submission under **21 CFR Part 812** that authorizes the use of an unapproved medical device in a clinical investigation to collect safety and effectiveness data.

**SR vs. NSR Devices (3 marks):**

| Feature | Significant Risk (SR) | Non-Significant Risk (NSR) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Definition | Potential for serious risk | Does not pose significant risk |
| FDA Submission | Required (full IDE) | Not required |
| IRB Approval | Required | Required |
| Examples | Implantable pacemakers, heart valves | Contact lens solutions, dental devices |

**Steps to Obtain IDE Authorization (4 marks):**
1. **Sponsor classifies** the device as SR or NSR.
2. **Pre-Submission (Pre-Sub)** meeting with FDA (optional but recommended).
3. **Prepare IDE application** (per 21 CFR 812.20): includes investigational plan, risk analysis, prior investigations report, manufacturing info, informed consent, IRB approval, labeling with "CAUTION — Investigational Device" statement.
4. **Submit to FDA** — 30-day statutory review period.
5. **FDA decision** → Approval (study may begin), Disapproval (study halted), or Conditional Approval.
6. **Conduct study** under IRB oversight, monitor UADEs within 10 working days, file annual progress reports.
7. **Final report** within 6 months of study completion.

#### Part (b) — Premarket Approval (PMA) (7 marks)

**PMA** is the FDA's most stringent marketing authorization process, regulated under **21 CFR Part 814**.

**When PMA is Required (1 mark):**
- Most **Class III** devices
- Devices where general and special controls are insufficient to assure safety and effectiveness

**PMA Submission Contents (3 marks):**
1. **Administrative documents** (cover letter, financial certification, user fee)
2. **Device description** (materials, components, principles of operation)
3. **Manufacturing information** (sterilization, packaging, shelf life)
4. **Performance testing** (bench, animal, biocompatibility — ISO 10993)
5. **Software documentation** (IEC 62304)
6. **Clinical study reports** (statistical analysis, primary and secondary endpoints)
7. **Risk analysis** (ISO 14971)
8. **Proposed labeling** (IFU, warnings, contraindications)
9. **Environmental assessment**

**FDA Review Timeline (2 marks):**
- **180-day statutory review period** by CDRH
- May issue **Additional Information (AI) requests** that pause the clock
- Outcomes: **Approval**, **Denial**, or **Withdrawal**

**Role of Clinical Data (1 mark):**
Valid scientific evidence from **well-controlled clinical investigations** is the cornerstone of PMA. Clinical data must demonstrate **reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness** for the proposed indications for use. Most PMA studies are **pivotal trials** with clearly defined endpoints, statistical hypotheses, and study populations.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **Verb Confusion:** Using "approved" for 510(k) — FDA **clears** 510(k) and **approves** PMA. Loss of **0.5 to 1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Class III Trap:** Assuming all devices can use 510(k). Class III devices are barred from 510(k) in most cases. Cost: **1 to 2 marks**.
> 3. **IDE vs. IND Confusion:** IDE is for devices; **IND (Investigational New Drug)** is for drugs under 21 CFR Part 312. Do NOT mix them.
> 4. **LDT Oversight:** Do not write that IVDs are *only* regulated under CLIA — since the **2024 FDA Final Rule**, LDTs are now under FDA jurisdiction. Cost: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Predicate vs. Generic:** A 510(k) predicate is **legally marketed**, not "similar" or "competitor's." Use the exact term.
> 6. **Statutory Timeframes:** 90 days for 510(k), 180 days for PMA, 30 days for IDE — memorize all three. Cost: **1 mark** per error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ FDA classifies devices into **3 classes** based on **risk** and **intended use**.
- ✅ **Class I = General Controls** | **Class II = General + Special Controls** | **Class III = PMA**.
- ✅ **510(k)** → Cleared, predicate-based, 90 days, mostly Class I/II.
- ✅ **PMA** → Approved, independent evidence required, 180 days, mostly Class III.
- ✅ **IDE** → 21 CFR Part 812, 30-day FDA review, required for **Significant Risk** device studies.
- ✅ **Significant Risk** requires full IDE + FDA approval; **Non-Significant Risk** only requires IRB.
- ✅ **IVDs** are Class I, II, or III, regulated under **21 CFR 809**; LDTs now under FDA per 2024 final rule.
- ✅ FDA "**clears**" 510(k), "**approves**" PMA, "**authorizes**" IDE, "**grants**" De Novo, "**authorizes**" EUA.
- ✅ Quality System Regulation (**21 CFR Part 820**) applies to almost all classes.
- ✅ Post-market requirements: **MDR** (21 CFR 803), **UDI** (21 CFR 830), **Adverse event reporting** within 10 working days for UADEs.
- ✅ Always cite the **21 CFR Part number** in exam answers for full credit.
- ✅ Use the memory mnemonic **"R-C-PI²"** → Registration → Clinical Trials → Premarket Notification → PMA → In-Vitro Diagnostics.
- ✅ Statutory timeframes: **90 / 180 / 30** days for 510(k) / PMA / IDE respectively.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality management of medical devices - international organization for standardization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality management of medical devices - international organization for standardization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quality Management System (QMS) for Medical Devices — Formal Definition

A **Quality Management System (QMS)** for medical devices is a formalized, documented system of policies, processes, procedures, and records that an organization establishes and maintains to ensure that medical devices consistently meet customer requirements, applicable regulatory requirements, and the highest standards of safety and performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim)**: *Quality management of medical devices* refers to the systematic approach adopted by manufacturers to design, produce, control, and continuously improve medical devices so that they conform to international, regional, and national regulations, ultimately safeguarding patient and user safety.

## 1.2 International Organization for Standardization (ISO)

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** is an independent, non-governmental international body that develops and publishes voluntary international standards to ensure the quality, safety, efficiency, and interoperability of products, services, and systems across industries.

- **Headquarters**: Geneva, Switzerland
- **Founded**: 23 February 1947
- **Members**: 169 national standards bodies
- **Official Languages**: English, French, Russian

> [!NOTE]  
> The acronym "ISO" is derived from the Greek word *isos* (ἴσος), meaning **"equal"**. It is *not* an acronym for "International Organization for Standardization" — this naming convention is deliberate to make the term universal across all languages.

## 1.3 Key Standards Relevant to Medical Devices

| ISO Standard | Full Title | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 13485:2016** | Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes | The globally recognized QMS standard for medical device manufacturers |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices | Risk management process for the entire medical device lifecycle |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | Quality management systems — Requirements | Generic QMS standard (foundation for ISO 13485) |
| **ISO 14708 / IEC 60601** | Various | Specific product safety standards for active medical devices |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Kitchen" Analogy

Imagine a hospital kitchen that prepares meals for patients with specific dietary needs:

- **The Recipe Book** = Quality Manual (documents the *what* and *why* of every process)
- **The Cooking Steps** = Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) (documents the *how*)
- **The Ingredients** = Incoming raw materials (subject to inspection and traceability)
- **The Chef** = The process owner (responsible for execution)
- **The Taste Test** = Inspection, testing, and validation
- **Customer Feedback** = Post-market surveillance and CAPA

If a patient is harmed by a contaminated meal, regulators (the FDA, EU Notified Bodies) will demand evidence that the kitchen followed a recognized standard. **That standard is ISO 13485** for medical devices. Just as a hospital kitchen cannot "wing it" with patient meals, a medical device manufacturer cannot rely on informal quality control — they must follow a documented, auditable, internationally harmonized QMS.

## 1.5 Why ISO 13485 Matters for Medical Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Regulatory Acceptance**: ISO 13485:2016 is the **de facto** regulatory requirement in over 190+ countries. In the EU (MDR 2017/745), in Canada (Health Canada CMDCAS), in the USA (FDA QSR alignment), and across most of Asia, ISO 13485 certification is either **mandatory or strongly preferred** for placing medical devices on the market.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of ISO 13485 — Timeline

| Year | Version | Key Change |
|---|---|---|
| 1996 | ISO 13485 (1st Edition) | First publication as a standalone medical device QMS standard |
| 2003 | ISO 13485:2003 | Alignment with ISO 9001:2000; emphasis on regulatory purposes |
| 2016 | **ISO 13485:2016** | **Current version**; strengthened focus on risk management, validation, and the entire supply chain |

## 2.2 The Eight-Clause Structure of ISO 13485:2016

The standard is organized into **8 clauses**. Clauses 1–3 are introductory; **Clauses 4–8 are auditable (mandatory)**.

> [!NOTE]  
> This is the **single most important structural fact** about ISO 13485 for KTU exams. Memorize the eight-clause layout.

| Clause | Title | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Scope | Defines applicability of the standard |
| **2** | Normative references | Lists referenced standards |
| **3** | Terms and definitions | Provides vocabulary |
| **4** | Quality management system | Documentation requirements, control of documents and records, medical device file |
| **5** | Management responsibility | Management commitment, customer focus, quality policy, quality objectives, management review, responsibility and authority |
| **6** | Resource management | Human resources, infrastructure, work environment, contamination control |
| **7** | Product realization | Planning, customer-related processes, design and development, purchasing, production and service provision, control of monitoring and measuring devices |
| **8** | Measurement, analysis, and improvement | Monitoring, measurement, control of nonconforming product, analysis of data, CAPA, internal audit |

## 2.3 Core Principles of ISO 13485

1. **Customer Focus** — Patient and user safety is paramount.
2. **Leadership** — Top management must demonstrate commitment to the QMS.
3. **Engagement of People** — Competence, awareness, and communication.
4. **Process Approach** — Activities managed as interconnected processes.
5. **Improvement** — Continual improvement of the QMS.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Decisions based on data and analysis.
7. **Relationship Management** — Managing relationships with suppliers, regulators, and other interested parties.

## 2.4 The Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) Cycle

The PDCA cycle is the **operational backbone** of any ISO 13485 QMS.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{PLAN} & : \text{Establish objectives and processes} \\
\textbf{DO} & : \text{Implement the planned processes} \\
\textbf{CHECK} & : \text{Monitor and measure performance against objectives} \\
\textbf{ACT} & : \text{Take corrective actions to continually improve}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 Risk-Based Approach (ISO 13485:2016 Highlight)

Unlike ISO 9001, ISO 13485:2016 mandates that **risk-based thinking** be applied throughout the QMS, even outside of formal product risk management (ISO 14971). The risk-based approach covers:

- Outsourced processes (clause 4.1.5)
- Design and development (clause 7.3)
- Purchasing and supplier control (clause 7.4)
- CAPA (clause 8.5.2)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Term | Definition / Formula-like Statement |
|---|---|
| **QMS** | Formalized system of policies, processes, and records ensuring product quality and regulatory compliance |
| **ISO** | Independent, non-governmental international standards body headquartered in Geneva |
| **ISO 13485** | International QMS standard specifically for medical device manufacturers |
| **PDCA** | Plan–Do–Check–Act continuous improvement cycle |
| **CAPA** | Corrective Action and Preventive Action — systematic process to eliminate causes of nonconformities |
| **MDR** | Medical Device Regulation (EU 2017/745) — references ISO 13485 |
| **Notified Body** | Accredited organization that audits QMS for CE marking |
| **MDSAP** | Medical Device Single Audit Program — allows a single audit to satisfy multiple regulatory jurisdictions |
| **Risk-Based Approach** | Method requiring organizations to identify, assess, and control risks in QMS processes |
| **Medical Device File (MDF)** | Documented file for each medical device type that demonstrates conformity to QMS |
| **DHF / DMR / DHR** | Design History File / Device Master Record / Device History Record |
| **8 Clauses** | The mandatory auditable structure of ISO 13485:2016 (clauses 4 through 8) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, an ISO 13485-certified QMS is the **entry ticket** for a manufacturer to:

- Sell in the European Union (MDR/IVDR compliance)
- Apply for FDA 510(k) or PMA in the USA
- Register products in Canada, Japan, Australia, and Brazil
- Demonstrate due diligence in product liability litigation
- Gain procurement preference from hospitals and government agencies

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Building an ISO 13485-Compliant QMS (Step-by-Step)

This is the **process derivation** for establishing a QMS. Every step must be documented as evidence during regulatory audits.

### Step 1: Define the Scope of the QMS (Clause 4.1)

The organization must document the **scope** of its QMS, including the type of medical devices, the activities covered, and any applicable exclusions (with justification).

$$
\text{QMS Scope} = \{ \text{Device Types} \} \cup \{ \text{Activities: Design, Production, Distribution, Service} \} \cup \{ \text{Site Locations} \}
$$

### Step 2: Establish the Documentation Pyramid (Clause 4.2)

The documentation hierarchy is structured in **four levels**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Level 1} & : \text{Quality Manual (high-level policy and scope)} \\
\textbf{Level 2} & : \text{Documented Procedures (mandatory in ISO 13485:2016)} \\
\textbf{Level 3} & : \text{Work Instructions, Forms, Templates} \\
\textbf{Level 4} & : \text{Records (evidence of performance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> ISO 13485:2016 requires **6 mandatory documented procedures**: (1) Control of Documents, (2) Control of Records, (3) Internal Audit, (4) Control of Nonconforming Product, (5) Corrective Action, (6) Preventive Action.

### Step 3: Identify Processes and the Sequence of Interaction (Clause 4.1)

A **process map** must be created showing the interaction of all QMS processes. Each process must have:

- An **owner** (person accountable)
- **Inputs** (what enters the process)
- **Outputs** (what leaves the process)
- **Performance indicators** (KPIs to monitor)
- **Risk and opportunity analysis**

### Step 4: Implement Management Responsibility (Clause 5)

Top management must:

- Communicate the importance of meeting customer and regulatory requirements
- Establish a **quality policy** and **quality objectives**
- Conduct **management reviews** at planned intervals (typically every 6–12 months)
- Ensure adequate **resource provision** (people, infrastructure, environment)

### Step 5: Resource Provision (Clause 6)

Resources include:

- **Human resources** (competent personnel with documented training records)
- **Infrastructure** (buildings, equipment, IT, transportation)
- **Work environment** (lighting, humidity, temperature, contamination control for sterile devices)

### Step 6: Product Realization (Clause 7)

This is the **largest clause** in ISO 13485. It covers:

- **7.1** Planning of product realization
- **7.2** Customer-related processes (determining requirements, review)
- **7.3** Design and development (with design controls, verification, validation, transfer, and change control)
- **7.4** Purchasing (supplier evaluation, purchasing information, verification of purchased product)
- **7.5** Production and service provision (installation, servicing, sterilization, traceability, preservation, validation of processes)
- **7.6** Control of monitoring and measuring devices (calibration)

### Step 7: Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement (Clause 8)

Key activities include:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monitoring and Measurement} & \rightarrow \text{Product conformity, QMS effectiveness, customer satisfaction} \\
\text{Internal Audit} & \rightarrow \text{Conducted at planned intervals} \\
\text{CAPA} & \rightarrow \text{Investigate root cause, eliminate, verify effectiveness} \\
\text{Analysis of Data} & \rightarrow \text{Statistical analysis where appropriate}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing ISO 13485 vs. ISO 9001

| Parameter | ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|
| Primary focus | Regulatory compliance & safety | Customer satisfaction & continual improvement |
| Risk approach | Risk-based throughout QMS | Risk-based thinking (less prescriptive) |
| Mandatory procedures | 6 documented procedures required | No mandatory procedures |
| Continual improvement | Not explicitly required | Explicit requirement |
| Outsource control | Explicitly addressed (clause 4.1.5) | General control |
| Design controls | Detailed and prescriptive (clause 7.3) | Generic |
| Post-market surveillance | Required as part of QMS | Not explicitly required |
| Applicable to | Medical device manufacturers | All industries |

## 3.3 Worked Example — The CAPA Process Flow

**Scenario**: A manufacturer receives a complaint that a batch of blood glucose meters is giving false low readings.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step A: Problem Detection} & \rightarrow \text{Complaint received, logged in CAPA system} \\
\textbf{Step B: Containment} & \rightarrow \text{Quarantine affected batch, notify regulators if required} \\
\textbf{Step C: Investigation} & \rightarrow \text{Root cause analysis (e.g., 5-Why, Fishbone/Ishikawa)} \\
\textbf{Step D: Corrective Action} & \rightarrow \text{Implement fix (e.g., recalibrate sensor, retrain operators)} \\
\textbf{Step E: Verification} & \rightarrow \text{Confirm effectiveness of corrective action} \\
\textbf{Step F: Preventive Action} & \rightarrow \text{Apply lessons learned to similar products/processes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Documentation of **every step** of the CAPA is mandatory under ISO 13485 clause 8.5.2 and is one of the most heavily audited areas during certification audits.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Eight-Clause Architecture of ISO 13485:2016

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ISO 13485:2016\nMedical Device QMS"] --> B["Clause 1: Scope"]
    A --> C["Clause 2: Normative References"]
    A --> D["Clause 3: Terms and Definitions"]
    A --> E["Clause 4: QMS Requirements"]
    A --> F["Clause 5: Management Responsibility"]
    A --> G["Clause 6: Resource Management"]
    A --> H["Clause 7: Product Realization"]
    A --> I["Clause 8: Measurement, Analysis and Improvement"]
    E --> E1["4.1 QMS and Documentation"]
    E --> E2["4.2 Medical Device File"]
    E --> E3["4.3 Control of Documents"]
    E --> E4["4.4 Control of Records"]
    F --> F1["5.1 Management Commitment"]
    F --> F2["5.2 Customer Focus"]
    F --> F3["5.3 Quality Policy"]
    F --> F4["5.4 Quality Objectives"]
    F --> F5["5.5 Responsibility and Authority"]
    F --> F6["5.6 Management Review"]
    G --> G1["6.1 Human Resources"]
    G --> G2["6.2 Infrastructure"]
    G --> G3["6.3 Work Environment"]
    H --> H1["7.1 Planning of Realization"]
    H --> H2["7.2 Customer-related Processes"]
    H --> H3["7.3 Design and Development"]
    H --> H4["7.4 Purchasing"]
    H --> H5["7.5 Production and Service"]
    H --> H6["7.6 Control of Measuring Devices"]
    I --> I1["8.1 Monitoring and Measurement"]
    I --> I2["8.2 Internal Audit"]
    I --> I3["8.3 Control of Nonconforming Product"]
    I --> I4["8.4 Analysis of Data"]
    I --> I5["8.5 CAPA Improvement"]
```

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle Applied to ISO 13485 QMS

```mermaid
graph LR
    P["PLAN\nEstablish QMS, Objectives, Processes"] --> D["DO\nImplement Processes, Train Personnel"]
    D --> C["CHECK\nMonitor, Measure, Audit, Analyze Data"]
    C --> A["ACT\nCorrective and Preventive Actions, Improve"]
    A --> P
    C -.Feedback.-> P
```

## 4.3 Process Interaction Model for a Medical Device Manufacturer

```mermaid
graph TD
    CUST["Customer and Regulatory Requirements"] --> P1["Process 1: Design and Development"]
    P1 --> P2["Process 2: Purchasing and Supplier Control"]
    P2 --> P3["Process 3: Production and Assembly"]
    P3 --> P4["Process 4: Sterilization and Packaging"]
    P4 --> P5["Process 5: Distribution"]
    P5 --> CUST
    P1 --> MR["Management Review"]
    P2 --> MR
    P3 --> MR
    P4 --> MR
    P5 --> MR
    MR --> P1
    MR --> P2
    MR --> P3
    MR --> P4
    MR --> P5
    CAPA["CAPA System"] --> P1
    CAPA --> P2
    CAPA --> P3
    CAPA --> P4
    CAPA --> P5
```

## 4.4 The CAPA Workflow (Corrective and Preventive Action)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Problem Detection\nComplaint, Audit Finding, Deviation"] --> S2["Step 2: Containment\nQuarantine, Notification"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Root Cause Analysis\n5-Why, Fishbone, Fault Tree"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Corrective Action Plan\nDefine Action, Owner, Timeline"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Implementation of Action"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Verification of Effectiveness"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Preventive Action\nApply to Similar Processes"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Closure and Documentation"]
```
