# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: Define ISO 13485:2016. Why is it important for medical device manufacturers?**

**Model Answer:**

ISO 13485:2016 is an internationally recognized standard titled "Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes." It specifies the requirements for a QMS where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide medical devices and related services that consistently meet customer and applicable regulatory requirements.

**Importance for medical device manufacturers:**

1. **Regulatory Acceptance**: ISO 13485 certification is required or strongly preferred in the EU, Canada, Japan, Australia, and most Asian markets.
2. **Patient Safety**: It mandates a systematic approach to risk management, design control, and post-market surveillance, ensuring devices are safe and effective.
3. **Market Access**: Acts as a single global benchmark, reducing the need for multiple regional audits.
4. **Legal Protection**: Demonstrates due diligence in case of product liability claims.
5. **Customer Confidence**: Hospitals, procurement agencies, and healthcare providers prefer ISO 13485-certified suppliers.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: List any three mandatory documented procedures required by ISO 13485:2016.**

**Model Answer:**

ISO 13485:2016 requires the following **six mandatory documented procedures**:

1. **Control of Documents** (Clause 4.2.4) — defines how documents are approved, reviewed, distributed, and revised.
2. **Control of Records** (Clause 4.2.5) — defines how records are stored, protected, retrieved, and retained.
3. **Internal Audit** (Clause 8.2.4) — defines the frequency, methodology, and documentation of internal QMS audits.
4. **Control of Nonconforming Product** (Clause 8.3) — defines how products that do not conform are identified, segregated, and disposed of.
5. **Corrective Action** (Clause 8.5.2) — defines how the root causes of nonconformities are eliminated.
6. **Preventive Action** (Clause 8.5.3) — defines how potential nonconformities are identified and prevented.

*(Any three of the above for 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Students frequently lose marks by (1) confusing ISO 13485 with ISO 9001, (2) omitting the risk-based approach in their answers, (3) failing to mention the specific clause number being addressed, and (4) writing vague, generic quality statements without linking them to **medical device** regulatory requirements. Always cite the **clause number** and use medical-device-specific examples.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Choice 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the eight-clause structure of ISO 13485:2016 in detail. Highlight the key requirements under each clause. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of ISO 13485 in the European Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745) and the US FDA Quality System Regulation (21 CFR 820). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Eight-Clause Structure of ISO 13485:2016 (7 Marks)**

The standard is organized as follows:

| Clause | Title | Key Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Clause 1** | Scope | Applies to organizations involved in one or more lifecycle stages of a medical device, including design, production, storage, distribution, installation, servicing, and decommissioning. |
| **Clause 2** | Normative References | References to other standards (e.g., ISO 14971 for risk management) that are indispensable for application. |
| **Clause 3** | Terms and Definitions | Provides the glossary of terms used in the standard. |
| **Clause 4** | Quality Management System | Requires the organization to establish, document, implement, and maintain a QMS; maintain a Medical Device File (MDF); and control documents and records. |
| **Clause 5** | Management Responsibility | Top management must demonstrate commitment, establish a quality policy, define quality objectives, plan the QMS, define responsibilities, and conduct periodic management reviews. |
| **Clause 6** | Resource Management | Provisions for competent human resources, adequate infrastructure, and a controlled work environment (with specific attention to contamination control for sterile devices). |
| **Clause 7** | Product Realization | Planning of product realization; customer-related processes; design and development with stage gates; purchasing and supplier control; production, installation, and servicing with validation of processes; and control of monitoring and measuring devices (calibration). |
| **Clause 8** | Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement | Monitoring and measurement of product and QMS; internal audits; control of nonconforming product; analysis of data; corrective and preventive action; and improvement activities. |

**Awarding Marks**: 1 mark for correctly listing the eight clauses; remaining 6 marks for describing the key requirements under each major clause (1 mark each for clauses 4, 5, 6, 7, 8; 0.5 mark each for clauses 1, 2, 3).

**(b) Role of ISO 13485 in EU MDR 2017/745 and US FDA 21 CFR 820 (7 Marks)**

**In the European Union (MDR 2017/745):**

- ISO 13485:2016 is **harmonized** under the MDR, meaning compliance with ISO 13485 provides **presumption of conformity** with the QMS-related requirements of the MDR.
- **Annex IX** of the MDR requires manufacturers to implement and maintain a QMS, and ISO 13485 certification is the **default pathway** for demonstrating compliance during conformity assessment by Notified Bodies.
- ISO 13485 supports the **General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR)** of Annex I, especially those related to risk management, design controls, post-market surveillance, and traceability.
- However, ISO 13485 alone is **not sufficient** for CE marking — manufacturers must also comply with **clinical evaluation, post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF), and unique device identification (UDI)** requirements of the MDR.

**In the US (FDA 21 CFR 820 — Quality System Regulation):**

- The FDA's QSR (21 CFR Part 820) is **substantively equivalent** to ISO 13485:2003, and the FDA has been working on harmonization.
- In 2024, the FDA finalized the **Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR)** which becomes effective in 2026, and the new QMSR incorporates **ISO 13485:2016 by reference**, with limited additions.
- ISO 13485 certification **simplifies FDA inspections** because it demonstrates the manufacturer already follows a globally recognized QMS framework.
- ISO 13485:2016's emphasis on **risk-based approach** aligns with FDA's expectations under 21 CFR 820, particularly for design controls (820.30) and CAPA (820.100).

**Conclusion**: ISO 13485:2016 acts as the **international lingua franca** of medical device QMS, accepted across major regulatory jurisdictions.

**Awarding Marks**: 3 marks for EU/MDR discussion; 3 marks for US/FDA discussion; 1 mark for conclusion/comparison.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Choice 2]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle and describe how each phase is implemented in a medical device QMS under ISO 13485:2016. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the risk-based approach mandated by ISO 13485:2016. How does it differ from the product-level risk management of ISO 14971? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) The PDCA Cycle in a Medical Device QMS (7 Marks)**

The Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle is a four-stage iterative method for continual improvement, which forms the foundation of any ISO 13485 QMS.

**PLAN Phase (Clause 4.1, 5.4, 7.1):**
- Identify the scope of the QMS and applicable regulatory requirements.
- Establish **quality objectives** (e.g., reduce product defects by 20% in the next year).
- Plan the **quality management system processes** and their sequence of interaction.
- Plan **product realization** activities, including design, production, and post-market processes.
- Identify and plan for **risks and opportunities** (risk-based thinking).

**DO Phase (Clauses 6, 7):**
- **Resource provision**: deploy competent personnel, infrastructure, and work environment.
- Implement **product realization**: design controls, purchasing controls, production, sterilization, packaging, and distribution.
- Execute training programs and ensure competence of personnel.
- Implement process validation (e.g., validation of sterilization processes per ISO 11137 or ISO 11135).

**CHECK Phase (Clauses 8.1, 8.2, 8.4):**
- **Monitor and measure** product conformity, QMS performance, and customer satisfaction.
- Conduct **internal audits** at planned intervals (typically every 12 months).
- Conduct **management reviews** of QMS effectiveness.
- Perform **analysis of data** to identify trends (e.g., complaint trends, defect rates).

**ACT Phase (Clauses 8.3, 8.5):**
- Initiate **Corrective Actions (CA)** to eliminate causes of detected nonconformities.
- Initiate **Preventive Actions (PA)** to eliminate potential nonconformities.
- Update the QMS based on findings, leading to continual improvement (although this is **not explicitly mandated** in ISO 13485 as it is in ISO 9001).

**Awarding Marks**: 1.5 marks for explaining PDCA conceptually; 1.5 marks each for the four phases with KTU-relevant examples (4 × 1.5 = 6); 0.5 mark for conclusion.

**(b) Risk-Based Approach vs. ISO 14971 Product Risk Management (7 Marks)**

**Risk-Based Approach in ISO 13485:2016:**

ISO 13485:2016 requires the organization to apply a **risk-based approach** to the control of all QMS processes, not just product design. This is one of the **major changes** introduced in the 2016 revision. The risk-based approach is required in:

- **Clause 4.1.5** — Risk-based control of outsourced processes.
- **Clause 4.1.6** — Validation of software used in the QMS.
- **Clause 7.1** — Risk identification during product realization planning.
- **Clause 7.3.3** — Design and development inputs and outputs.
- **Clause 7.4.1** — Supplier evaluation and selection.
- **Clause 8.2.1** — Feedback and complaint handling.
- **Clause 8.5.2** — CAPA effectiveness verification.

The risk-based approach in ISO 13485 is **process-oriented** and focuses on whether each QMS process is capable of producing conforming product and meeting regulatory requirements.

**ISO 14971 Product Risk Management:**

ISO 14971:2019, "Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices," specifies a **product-level risk management process** for the entire medical device lifecycle. It follows a structured methodology:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1} & : \text{Risk Analysis (identify hazards, estimate risks)} \\
\textbf{Step 2} & : \text{Risk Evaluation (compare to acceptability criteria)} \\
\textbf{Step 3} & : \text{Risk Control (implement measures to reduce risk)} \\
\textbf{Step 4} & : \text{Residual Risk Evaluation} \\
\textbf{Step 5} & : \text{Risk Management Review} \\
\textbf{Step 6} & : \text{Production and Post-Production Information}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Differences between the two:**

| Aspect | ISO 13485 Risk-Based Approach | ISO 14971 Product Risk Management |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | QMS process-level risks | Product-level safety risks |
| Focus | Ensuring QMS processes are effective and controlled | Ensuring the medical device is safe for patients/users |
| Documented output | Process controls, validation, monitoring | Risk Management File, Risk Management Report |
| Lifecycle stage | All stages | All stages but with a product focus |
| Outcome | Effective QMS | Safe and effective medical device |

**Conclusion**: The ISO 13485 risk-based approach and ISO 14971 are **complementary**. ISO 14971 is the *substance* (what risks the device poses and how they are controlled), while ISO 13485's risk-based approach is the *framework* (how the QMS manages those risks systematically).

**Awarding Marks**: 2 marks for explaining risk-based approach in ISO 13485 with examples; 2 marks for explaining ISO 14971; 2 marks for clear differentiation table; 1 mark for conclusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Frequency KTU Recap Checklist**

- **ISO** stands for the **International Organization for Standardization**, headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland** (founded 1947).
- The **current** version of ISO 13485 is **ISO 13485:2016** (published March 2016).
- ISO 13485 has **8 clauses** — only **clauses 4 to 8 are auditable** (mandatory).
- ISO 13485:2016 mandates **6 documented procedures**: Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Control of Nonconforming Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action.
- The **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle is the backbone of the QMS.
- ISO 13485 emphasizes a **risk-based approach** throughout the QMS (a key change from the 2003 version).
- ISO 13485 differs from ISO 9001 in that it does **not** explicitly require continual improvement but **does** require regulatory compliance throughout.
- **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action) is the most heavily audited area of ISO 13485.
- The **Medical Device File (MDF)** is mandatory for each device type or family.
- ISO 13485:2016 is **harmonized** under EU MDR 2017/745, providing **presumption of conformity** with QMS-related requirements.
- The FDA's new **QMSR (effective 2026)** incorporates **ISO 13485:2016 by reference** with limited additions.
- **MDSAP** (Medical Device Single Audit Program) allows a single audit to satisfy five regulatory jurisdictions: USA, Canada, Japan, Australia, Brazil.
- ISO 14971 is the **product-level risk management** standard; it works **with** ISO 13485, not in place of it.
- A common **14-mark question** on this topic asks for: (a) ISO 13485 structure / PDCA, (b) risk-based approach or international regulatory comparison.
- Always cite the **clause number** in your exam answers (e.g., "Clause 7.3 — Design and Development").
- Use **medical-device-specific examples** (e.g., sterility, biocompatibility, traceability via UDI) rather than generic manufacturing examples.

topics.title: ISO 9001 - QMP overview and process approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 9001 - QMP overview and process approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — ISO 9001

**ISO 9001** is an internationally recognized standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** that specifies the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. The current version, **ISO 9001:2015**, is built upon seven **Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** and emphasizes the **process approach** combined with **risk-based thinking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> ISO 9001:2015 is the *generic* QMS standard. For medical devices, **ISO 13485:2016** is the sector-specific adaptation. Understanding ISO 9001 forms the foundation for comprehending ISO 13485.

### 1.2 The Seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs)

The seven QMPs are the foundational beliefs that guide an organization's quality management:

| # | Principle | Core Idea |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Primary focus is meeting customer needs and exceeding expectations |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered people enhance organizational capability |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Consistent and predictable results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as interconnected processes |
| 5 | **Improvement** | Successful organizations focus on continual improvement |
| 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on analysis and evaluation of data are more likely to produce desired results |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | Managing relationships with interested parties optimizes performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Process Approach (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.4):**
> "The application of a system of processes within an organization, together with the identification and interactions of these processes, and their management to produce the desired outcome, is referred to as the 'process approach'."

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** preparing meals (the final product):

- **Input** = raw ingredients, orders, recipes
- **Process** = chopping, cooking, plating
- **Output** = served meal
- **Customer** = the diner
- **Feedback** = customer reviews, return rates
- **Monitoring** = chef tasting food, checking temperature

If the chef treats each step (receiving ingredients → storing → prepping → cooking → serving) as a **linked process** with clear owners, measurements, and feedback loops, quality becomes **predictable and repeatable**. ISO 9001's process approach applies the same logic to manufacturing medical devices.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **QMS Effectiveness**: measured by **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)**, **First Pass Yield (FPY)**, and **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**
- **Process Capability**: **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the industry benchmark
- **Conformity Rate**: **> 95%** of audit findings closed on time
- **Continual Improvement**: typically **≥ 5%** annual reduction in NCRs (Non-Conformance Reports)
- **Risk**: quantified using **Severity × Occurrence × Detection** scoring (FMEA-style)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Process Approach — Theoretical Foundation

The process approach is built on the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** and the concept that every activity uses **inputs** and produces **outputs**.

#### 2.1.1 Core Elements of a Process

Every process in ISO 9001 has the following elements:

1. **Inputs** — Resources, data, materials required
2. **Controls** — Standards, procedures, regulations (e.g., design inputs, SOPs)
3. **Activities** — Value-adding transformation steps
4. **Outputs** — Products, services, decisions
5. **Customers (Internal/External)** — Recipients of the output
6. **Feedback** — Monitoring and measurement of effectiveness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The *Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)* cycle is applied to:
> - **Process-level**: each individual process
> - **QMS-level**: the overall management system
> - **Clause-level**: each ISO 9001 clause (e.g., Clause 8 — Operation)

### 2.2 PDCA Cycle Application to QMS

The **PDCA cycle** provides a structured framework for the process approach:

| Stage | Action | Example in Medical Device QMS |
|-------|--------|-------------------------------|
| **P — Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, resources | Define QMS scope, quality objectives, risk register |
| **D — Do** | Execute the planned processes | Train staff, perform design and development, manufacture |
| **C — Check** | Monitor and measure performance | Internal audits, CAPA, customer feedback analysis |
| **A — Act** | Take actions to improve performance | Management review, update procedures, preventive actions |

### 2.3 Process Approach Map — Clause 4.4 Requirements

ISO 9001:2015 Clause 4.4 mandates the organization shall:

- **a)** Determine the processes needed for the QMS and their application
- **b)** Determine criteria for monitoring these processes
- **c)** Determine criteria for acceptance of outputs
- **d)** Determine risks and opportunities (Clause 6.1)
- **e)** Evaluate these processes and implement changes to ensure intended results
- **f)** Improve the processes and the QMS

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is primarily conceptual, but key metrics are summarized below for exam reference.

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Output Meeting Specification}}{\text{Total Output Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{First Pass Yield (FPY)} = \frac{\text{Units Passing Inspection First Time}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Priority Number (RPN)} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1 to 10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1 to 10)
- $D$ = Detection (1 to 10)

**Benchmark Values (KTU expected answers):**
- **Cpk** $\geq$ **1.33** indicates a capable process
- **RPN** $\geq$ **100** typically triggers a CAPA
- **COPQ** target: **3% to 5%** of total revenue in regulated industries

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The process approach is fundamental to:
- **Medical Device Manufacturing** — ensuring every device meets design specifications
- **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** — managing the SDLC as interconnected processes
- **Regulatory Submissions** — auditors (FDA, Notified Bodies) trace process interactions
- **Supplier Management** — extending the process map to the supply chain (Clause 8.4)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Process Effectiveness Calculation

Let us derive a numerical example often asked in KTU exams.

**Problem Statement:** A stent manufacturing line produces 1,000 stents in a shift. During final inspection, 920 stents pass on the first attempt, 60 are reworked, and 20 are scrapped. Calculate the **Process Effectiveness**, **First Pass Yield**, and the **COPQ** if failure costs are ₹50,000 for rework and ₹2,00,000 for scrap.

**Step 1 — Identify the Input and Output**

$$
\text{Total Output} = 1000 \text{ stents}
$$

$$
\text{First Pass Output} = 920 \text{ stents}
$$

$$
\text{Reworked} = 60 \text{ stents}
$$

$$
\text{Scrapped} = 20 \text{ stents}
$$

*Logic:* The first line identifies the total production volume. The subsequent lines partition the output based on inspection outcome. This partitioning is essential because each category represents a different cost implication in the QMS.

**Step 2 — Calculate First Pass Yield (FPY)**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Units Passing First Time}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{920}{1000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{FPY} = 92\%
$$

*Logic:* FPY measures process capability at the source. A 92% FPY indicates that 8% of production requires additional resources, which directly affects the QMS efficiency and is a key metric for the "Check" stage of PDCA.

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Rework + Scrap (Failure Output)**

$$
\text{Failure Output} = 60 + 20 = 80 \text{ stents}
$$

*Logic:* This represents the portion of output that did not meet specification on first attempt. This is the population that contributes to the Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ).

**Step 4 — Calculate Process Effectiveness**

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Specification-Meeting Output}}{\text{Total Output}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{1000 - 20}{1000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{980}{1000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = 98\%
$$

*Logic:* Note the distinction — Process Effectiveness includes *reworked* units (since they eventually meet spec), while FPY does not. This distinction is critical for KTU short-answer questions.

**Step 5 — Calculate Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**

$$
\text{COPQ} = (\text{Rework Cost} \times \text{Rework Units}) + (\text{Scrap Cost} \times \text{Scrap Units})
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = (50{,}000 \times 60) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 20)
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = 70{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
$$

*Logic:* Rework cost is internal failure cost (rectifiable), while scrap cost is the highest form of failure cost (non-recoverable). For KTU exams, the full COPQ formula includes Prevention + Appraisal costs as well.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Approach vs. Element-Based Approach

| Aspect | Element-Based Approach (Pre-2000) | Process Approach (ISO 9001:2015) |
|--------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Focus** | Departmental functions | Cross-functional processes |
| **Customer** | Implicit | Explicit (Clause 4.4 + 9.1.3) |
| **Management** | Functional silos | System of interrelated processes |
| **Improvement** | Reactive | Continual (PDCA) |
| **Risk** | Not addressed | Clause 6.1 mandatory |
| **Documentation** | Heavy (manual) | Risk-based, flexible |
| **Audit** | Compliance-based | Performance-based |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> The shift from ISO 9001:2008 to ISO 9001:2015 introduced **risk-based thinking** explicitly. The **High-Level Structure (HLS)** also aligned ISO 9001 with ISO 14001 and ISO 45001.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Approach — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Customer Requirements Input] --> B1[Clause 4 Context of Organization]
    B1 --> C1[Clause 6 Quality Planning and Risks]
    C1 --> D1[Clause 7 Support Resources]
    D1 --> E1[Clause 8 Operation Core Processes]
    E1 --> F1[Product and Service Output]
    F1 --> G1[Clause 9 Performance Evaluation]
    G1 --> H1[Clause 10 Improvement]
    H1 --> E1
    G1 --> I1[Clause 5 Leadership Review]
    I1 --> C1
    A1 --> G1
    E1 --> A1
```

*Explanation:* This architecture flow demonstrates the **closed-loop nature** of the ISO 9001:2015 process approach. Customer requirements feed into the QMS (Clause 4), are planned with risk consideration (Clause 6), supported by resources (Clause 7), executed as operations (Clause 8), evaluated (Clause 9), and improved (Clause 10). The feedback loops ensure continual improvement.

### 4.2 PDCA Cycle Mapped to ISO 9001 Clauses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plan: Clause 4 Context] --> P2[Plan: Clause 6 Quality Objectives and Risks]
    P2 --> P3[Plan: Clause 7 Resource Planning]
    P3 --> D1[Do: Clause 8 Operational Planning and Control]
    D1 --> D2[Do: Clause 8 Design and Development]
    D2 --> D3[Do: Clause 8 Production and Service Provision]
    D3 --> C1[Check: Clause 9 Monitoring and Measurement]
    C1 --> C2[Check: Clause 9 Internal Audit]
    C2 --> C3[Check: Clause 9 Management Review]
    C3 --> A1[Act: Clause 10 Nonconformity and Corrective Action]
    A1 --> A2[Act: Clause 10 Continual Improvement]
    A2 --> P1
```

*Explanation:* Each ISO 9001 clause is mapped to a PDCA phase, satisfying the **Clause 4.4 requirement** that "the organization shall apply the PDCA cycle at the process level and the QMS level."

### 4.3 Seven QMPs — Hierarchical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QMS[ISO 9001:2015 QMS Foundation]
    QMS --> P1[1. Customer Focus]
    QMS --> P2[2. Leadership]
    QMS --> P3[3. Engagement of People]
    QMS --> P4[4. Process Approach]
    QMS --> P5[5. Improvement]
    QMS --> P6[6. Evidence Based Decision Making]
    QMS --> P7[7. Relationship Management]
    P1 --> OUT[Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT
    P7 --> OUT
```

*Explanation:* The seven QMPs are the **foundational beliefs** of ISO 9001. The Process Approach (Principle 4) is closely linked to the Plan-Do-Check-Act methodology and is the operational engine of the QMS.

### 4.4 Risk-Based Thinking Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clause 6.1 Actions to Address Risks] --> B[Identify Risks at Process Level]
    B --> C[Analyze and Evaluate RPN]
    C --> D{RPN greater than 100?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Implement Controls or CAPA]
    D -->|No| F[Accept and Monitor]
    E --> G[Update Process Documentation]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Internal Audit Verification]
    H --> I[Management Review Input]
```

*Explanation:* Risk-based thinking is **embedded within the process approach** (Clause 6.1). This is a major evolution from ISO 9001:2008 and is a frequent KTU exam topic.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the "Process Approach" as per ISO 9001:2015. List any four elements of a process.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Process Approach** (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 4.4) is defined as *"the application of a system of processes within an organization, together with the identification and interactions of these processes, and their management, to produce the desired outcome."*

**Four Elements of a Process:**
1. **Inputs** — Resources and data required
2. **Activities / Controls** — Value-adding transformation steps
3. **Outputs** — Products, services, or decisions
4. **Feedback** — Monitoring, measurement, and customer satisfaction data

**[3 Marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for elements]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the PDCA cycle in the context of the ISO 9001 process approach.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** is a four-stage management method applied within the ISO 9001 process approach:

- **Plan (Clauses 4, 6, 7):** Establish QMS scope, quality objectives, risks, and resources.
- **Do (Clause 8):** Execute operational planning, design, production, and service provision.
- **Check (Clause 9):** Monitor, measure, audit, and conduct management review.
- **Act (Clause 10):** Address nonconformities, implement CAPA, and drive continual improvement.

The PDCA cycle is applied at **process level** and **QMS level**, ensuring closed-loop feedback.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark overview + 2 marks for PDCA mapping]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **seven Quality Management Principles (QMPs)** of ISO 9001:2015 with one-line descriptions of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **process approach** as per Clause 4.4 of ISO 9001:2015. How does it differ from the element-based approach used in earlier versions of the standard? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — The Seven QMPs (7 Marks)**

| # | Principle | Key Benefit |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Customer Focus | Increased customer value and satisfaction |
| 2 | Leadership | Unity of purpose and direction |
| 3 | Engagement of People | Improved motivation and performance |
| 4 | Process Approach | Consistent and predictable results |
| 5 | Improvement | Enhanced organizational performance |
| 6 | Evidence-Based Decision Making | Improved decision quality |
| 7 | Relationship Management | Optimization of stakeholder value |

Each principle explained with one line = **1 mark each × 7 = 7 marks**

**Part (b) — Process Approach (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The process approach (Clause 4.4) requires the organization to:
- Determine the processes needed for the QMS
- Determine sequence and interaction of these processes
- Determine criteria and methods for monitoring
- Address risks and opportunities (Clause 6.1)
- Evaluate processes and implement continual improvement

**Differences from Element-Based Approach (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Process Approach (2015) | Element Approach (Pre-2000) |
|--------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
| Orientation | Process outputs and customers | Departmental functions |
| Risk | Explicit (Clause 6.1) | Not addressed |
| Documentation | Flexible, risk-based | Heavy, mandatory procedures |
| Improvement | Continual via PDCA | Reactive |
| Leadership | Clause 5 (top management) | Management representative |
| HLS alignment | Yes (Annex SL) | No |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]**
**(a)** A medical device company produces 5,000 syringes per day. In a quality audit, 4,500 pass on first inspection, 350 are reworked, and 150 are scrapped. Calculate the **First Pass Yield (FPY)** and **Process Effectiveness**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **risk-based thinking** in the process approach. How is it integrated with the **PDCA cycle**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)**

Given:
- Total Output = 5,000 syringes
- First Pass = 4,500
- Reworked = 350
- Scrapped = 150

**Step 1: First Pass Yield (3 marks)**

$$
\text{FPY} = \frac{4500}{5000} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

*Logic:* FPY only counts units that pass on the very first inspection.

**Step 2: Process Effectiveness (4 marks)**

$$
\text{Process Effectiveness} = \frac{5000 - 150}{5000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{4850}{5000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= 97\%
$$

*Logic:* Reworked units are eventually conforming, so they are included in the "meeting specification" count. Scrapped units are not.

**Part (b) — Risk-Based Thinking and PDCA Integration (7 Marks)**

**Risk-Based Thinking (3 marks):**
Introduced in ISO 9001:2015 as Clause 6.1, it requires the organization to:
- Identify risks and opportunities
- Plan actions to address them
- Integrate these actions into QMS processes

**PDCA Integration (4 marks):**
- **Plan:** Identify risks during QMS planning (Clause 6)
- **Do:** Implement controls within operational processes (Clause 8)
- **Check:** Monitor risks through audits and KPIs (Clause 9)
- **Act:** Update risk register and controls in management review (Clause 10)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing FPY with Process Effectiveness** — FPY excludes rework, Process Effectiveness includes it. Mixing these will cost 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting Clause numbers** — Always cite the specific ISO 9001 clause (e.g., Clause 4.4, 6.1) for full marks.
> 3. **Listing QMPs without explaining their benefits** — KTU awards 1 mark per principle with benefit, not just listing.
> 4. **Not mentioning PDCA** — Any question on process approach requires PDCA discussion. Omission = 2-3 mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping risk-based thinking** — Mandatory in ISO 9001:2015 explanations. Do not ignore Clause 6.1.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ISO 9001 QMP and Process Approach**

- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015** is the current QMS standard; **ISO 13485:2016** is the medical device-specific derivative.
- ✅ The **7 QMPs** form the foundational beliefs of every QMS.
- ✅ The **Process Approach** (Clause 4.4) requires identification, interaction, control, and improvement of processes.
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** must be applied at both **process level** and **QMS level**.
- ✅ **Risk-based thinking** (Clause 6.1) is the major evolution from ISO 9001:2008.
- ✅ **High-Level Structure (HLS)** aligns ISO 9001 with ISO 14001 and ISO 45001.
- ✅ Key metrics: **FPY, Process Effectiveness, COPQ, RPN, Cpk**.
- ✅ **First Pass Yield** excludes rework; **Process Effectiveness** includes rework but excludes scrap.
- ✅ **COPQ** includes prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.
- ✅ **RPN = S × O × D**; RPN ≥ 100 typically triggers CAPA.
- ✅ **Leadership (Clause 5)** is a major change — top management must demonstrate commitment.
- ✅ **Documented Information** (Clause 7.5) replaced the older "documents and records" terminology.
- ✅ **Context of the Organization** (Clause 4) is a new requirement identifying internal/external issues.
- ✅ **Customer focus** requires measuring and monitoring customer perception (Clause 9.1.3).
- ✅ The QMS must include **outsourced processes** (Clause 8.4) within the process map.
- ✅ For medical devices, ISO 13485 takes precedence over ISO 9001 in regulatory contexts.
- ✅ **Continual improvement** is now mandatory (Clause 10.3) — reactive-only improvement is insufficient.
- ✅ **Management Review** (Clause 9.3) inputs include audit results, customer feedback, process performance, and risks.
- ✅ **Audit focus** has shifted from procedural compliance to **process performance and effectiveness**.
- ✅ **Knowledge management** (Clause 7.1.6) is a new explicit requirement.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISO 13485 (deeper understanding)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ISO 13485 (deeper understanding)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## ISO 13485 – The Global Benchmark for Medical Device Quality

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **ISO 13485:2016** is an internationally recognized standard titled *"Medical devices — Quality management systems — Requirements for regulatory purposes."* It specifies the requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide **medical devices and related services** that consistently meet **customer requirements** and applicable **regulatory requirements**.

The standard is published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and is specifically tailored to the **regulated medical device environment**. Unlike generic QMS standards, ISO 13485 mandates the integration of **risk management** throughout the product lifecycle, as outlined in **ISO 14971**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ISO 13485 is the *de facto* requirement for manufacturers seeking regulatory approval in the EU (under MDR 2017/745), Canada (under MDSAP), Japan (under PMD Act), Australia (TGA), and most global markets. The current version, **ISO 13485:2016**, replaced the 2003 version and aligns with the **GHTF (Global Harmonization Task Force)** principles.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ISO 13485 as the **"Driver's License + Vehicle Fitness Certificate"** for a medical device manufacturer.

| Real-World Analogy | ISO 13485 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Traffic rules (universal) | ISO 9001 (generic QMS) |
| Specialized driving rules for ambulances | ISO 13485 (medical-specific QMS) |
| Vehicle fitness certificate | Regulatory clearance (CE/FDA/COFEPRIS) |
| Driver training logbook | Documented QMS procedures |
| Accident reporting system | CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Actions) |
| Pre-trip vehicle inspection | Design controls and risk management |

**In simple words:** If you are building a medical device (say, a pacemaker or an MRI machine), ISO 13485 is the **rulebook** that ensures every step — from design, manufacturing, sterilization, packaging, distribution, to post-market surveillance — is **traceable, documented, and risk-controlled**. Without this rulebook, no regulatory authority in the world will allow your device to touch a patient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Regulatory Purposes" matters:** The phrase *"for regulatory purposes"* in the title means the standard is a **regulatory tool**, not just a business excellence tool. It exists so that regulators (FDA, EU Notified Bodies, CDSCO India) can audit a manufacturer's QMS with a globally harmonized yardstick.

---

## Scope and Applicability

**Who must implement ISO 13485?**

The standard applies to organizations regardless of their type or size, involved in one or more stages of the **medical device life-cycle**, including:

1. **Design and Development** of medical devices
2. **Production** of medical devices
3. **Storage and Distribution** of medical devices
4. **Installation** of medical devices
5. **Servicing** of medical devices
6. **Design, development, and provision of associated activities** (e.g., technical support)
7. **Sterilization, calibration, and contract manufacturing**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Does ISO 13485 apply only to manufacturers?"* The correct answer is **NO** — it applies across the entire medical device **value chain**, including suppliers, distributors, and service providers.

---

## Key Terminology (Foundation for KTU Answers)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **QMS** | Quality Management System — the set of policies, processes, and procedures required for planning and executing core business activities. |
| **Medical Device** | Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, implant, software, or material used for diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, or treatment of disease. |
| **Risk Management** | Systematic application of management policies, procedures, and practices to the analysis, evaluation, control, and monitoring of risk (per ISO 14971). |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Action — process to eliminate causes of non-conformities. |
| **MDR** | Medical Device Reporting (FDA) or Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745). |
| **Notified Body** | Third-party certification body authorized by the EU to assess medical device QMS conformity. |
| **Traceability** | Ability to trace the history, application, or location of a medical device through recorded identification. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Clause-by-Clause Architecture of ISO 13485:2016

ISO 13485 follows the **10-clause Annex SL structure** (high-level structure used by all modern ISO management standards), but with extensive **medical device-specific requirements** woven throughout.

### Clause-Wise Breakdown

---

### **Clause 1: Scope**
- Defines applicability to all medical device life-cycle stages.
- Permits exclusions only for **Clause 6 (Resource Management)**, **Clause 7 (Product Realization)**, and **Clause 8 (Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement)** — but **ONLY** if exclusions do not affect the organization's ability to ensure product safety and regulatory compliance.
- All Clause 5 (Management Responsibility) and Clause 8.2 (Monitoring & Measurement) requirements are **non-excludable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common question is *"Which clauses can a small medical device company exclude?"* The strict answer is — **virtually none in practice**, because regulatory authorities will reject such exclusions.

---

### **Clause 4: Quality Management System**

This clause mandates the foundational QMS infrastructure.

**4.1 General Requirements**
The organization must:
- **Identify** the QMS processes needed (Process Map).
- **Determine** the sequence and interaction of these processes (Process Interaction Chart).
- **Determine** criteria and methods for monitoring each process.
- **Ensure** availability of resources and information.
- **Monitor, measure, and analyse** these processes.
- **Implement** actions to achieve planned results and continual improvement.

**4.2 Documentation Requirements** — *Crucial for KTU exams*
Six levels of documentation are mandated:

$$\text{Documentation Hierarchy} = \{\text{Quality Manual},\ \text{Medical Device File},\ \text{Documented Procedures},\ \text{Records},\ \text{Forms},\ \text{Work Instructions}\}$$

| Document Level | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Quality Manual | Defines QMS scope, exclusions, and interactions | **High-yield topic** |
| Medical Device File (MDF) | Set of documents describing a specific medical device | **Frequently asked** |
| Documented Procedures (6 mandatory) | Control of documents, records, internal audit, non-conforming product, CAPA, design | **Frequently asked** |
| Records | Evidence of activities performed | Audit trail |

**The 6 Mandatory Documented Procedures (Clause 4.2):**
1. Control of Documents (4.2.4)
2. Control of Records (4.2.5)
3. Internal Audit (8.2.4)
4. Control of Non-Conforming Product (8.3)
5. Corrective Action (8.5.2)
6. Preventive Action (8.5.3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plus** one **mandatory** requirement (not a procedure, but a record): a **Medical Device File** must be maintained for each medical device type or family (4.2.3).

---

### **Clause 5: Management Responsibility**

Defines the **top management's obligations** — the leadership accountability.

**Key Requirements:**
1. **Management Commitment (5.1):** Top management must communicate the importance of meeting customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements.
2. **Customer Focus (5.2):** Identify customer requirements, including those of **end-users, patients, clinicians, and regulators**.
3. **Quality Policy (5.3):** Must be appropriate to the organization, include a commitment to comply with requirements and maintain QMS effectiveness.
4. **Quality Objectives (5.4):** Must be **measurable**, consistent with the quality policy, and include compliance objectives.
5. **Responsibility and Authority (5.5.1):** Must be defined and communicated.
6. **Management Representative (5.5.2):** A member of top management must be appointed to ensure QMS establishment, implementation, and reporting.
7. **Management Review (5.6):** At planned intervals, top management must review the QMS. Inputs and outputs are **prescribed by the standard**.

**Management Review Inputs (5.6.2):**
- Results of audits
- Customer feedback (including complaints)
- Process performance and product conformity
- Status of preventive and corrective actions
- Follow-up actions from previous management reviews
- Changes that could affect the QMS
- Recommendations for improvement
- **New or revised regulatory requirements**

**Management Review Outputs (5.6.3):**
- Improvement of the QMS and its processes
- Improvement of product-related to customer requirements
- Resource needs

---

### **Clause 6: Resource Management**

**6.1 Provision of Resources:** Determine and provide resources to implement and maintain the QMS.
**6.2 Human Resources:** Personnel performing work affecting product quality must be **competent** (education, training, skills, experience).
**6.3 Infrastructure:** Buildings, workspace, process equipment, and supporting services (IT, communication).
**6.4 Work Environment:** Must be suitable to achieve product conformity — includes environmental conditions for product or process (e.g., **cleanroom ISO 14644** classifications for sterile devices).
**6.5 Contamination Control:** *Specific to medical devices* — the organization must document requirements for contamination control if the product is required to be sterile.
**6.6 Infrastructure and Work Environment for Sterile Medical Devices:** Validated sterilization processes.

---

### **Clause 7: Product Realization — The Heart of ISO 13485**

This is the **most heavily tested clause** in KTU exams because it covers the entire product life-cycle from design to delivery.

**7.1 Planning of Product Realization:**
- Determine quality objectives and requirements for the product.
- Define processes, documents, and resources specific to the product.
- Define verification, validation, monitoring, inspection, and test activities.
- Define records needed to provide evidence of conformity.

**7.2 Customer-Related Processes:**
- **Identification of Requirements:** Product, customer, regulatory, training, and post-market requirements.
- **Review of Requirements:** Must be conducted **before commitment to supply** the product.
- **Customer Communication:** Includes **advisory notices** (a medical device-specific addition).

**7.3 Design and Development:**

This is the **crown jewel** of ISO 13485. The D&D process is broken into stages with strict control:

$$\text{D\&D Sub-clauses} = \{7.3.1 \text{ to } 7.3.10\}$$

| Sub-clause | Activity | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| 7.3.1 | Design and Development Planning | High |
| 7.3.2 | Design and Development Inputs | High |
| 7.3.3 | Design and Development Outputs | High |
| 7.3.4 | Design and Development Review | Medium |
| 7.3.5 | Design and Development Verification | High |
| 7.3.6 | Design and Development Validation | High |
| 7.3.7 | Control of Design and Development Changes | Medium |
| 7.3.8 | Design and Development Transfer | Medium |
| 7.3.9 | Control of Design and Development Files | Low |
| 7.3.10 | Design and Development Records | Medium |

**7.4 Purchasing:**
- **Supplier Selection & Evaluation:** Based on supplier's ability to meet purchasing requirements (sub-clause 7.4.1).
- **Purchasing Information:** Must describe the product to be purchased, acceptance criteria, and QMS requirements.
- **Verification of Purchased Product:** May include on-site audits of suppliers.

**7.5 Production and Service Provision:**
- **7.5.1 Control of Production:** Includes controlled conditions, qualified infrastructure, defined work instructions, measuring devices, and **traceability**.
- **7.5.2 Cleanliness of Product:** If product is cleaned by the organization or supplied non-sterile with cleaning instructions.
- **7.5.3 Installation Activities:** Documented requirements for installation and verification.
- **7.5.4 Servicing Activities:** Procedures for servicing activities and verification.
- **7.5.5 Particular Requirements for Sterile Medical Devices:** Sterilization process validation, environmental controls, sterilization traceability.
- **7.5.6 Validation of Processes for Production and Service Provision:** Especially for processes where **output cannot be verified by subsequent monitoring** (e.g., welding, sterilization, injection molding).
- **7.5.7 Particular Requirements for Validation of Processes for Sterilization and Sterile Barrier Systems.**
- **7.5.8 Identification:** Product identification throughout the process.
- **7.5.9 Traceability:** Establish and maintain a traceability system — mandatory for **implantable medical devices** (per 7.5.9.2) to enable tracking and recall.
- **7.5.10 Customer Property:** Including intellectual property and confidential health information.
- **7.5.11 Preservation of Product:** Packaging, storage, handling, and protection.

**7.6 Control of Monitoring and Measuring Equipment:**
- Calibration and validation of measuring equipment (e.g., calipers, pressure gauges, multimeters) used to verify product conformity.
- Records of calibration must include traceability to **national or international standards**.

---

### **Clause 8: Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement**

This clause ensures the QMS is **continuously monitored and improved**.

**8.1 General:** Plan and document monitoring, measurement, analysis, and improvement activities.

**8.2 Monitoring and Measurement:**
- **8.2.1 Feedback:** A systematic process to gather and monitor customer feedback (including complaints) as a measure of QMS performance — early warning indicator.
- **8.2.2 Complaint Handling:** Documented procedure for timely complaint handling — *medical device-specific*.
- **8.2.3 Reporting to Regulatory Authorities:** Advisory notices (Field Safety Notices in EU) for adverse events or non-conformities that could affect public health.
- **8.2.4 Internal Audit:** Conducted at planned intervals to determine QMS conformity — auditors must be **independent** of the area being audited.
- **8.2.5 Monitoring and Measurement of Processes:** Appropriate methods to demonstrate process capability.
- **8.2.6 Monitoring and Measurement of Product:** At appropriate stages per the planned arrangements.

**8.3 Control of Non-Conforming Product:**
- Identification, segregation, and disposition of non-conforming product.
- **Concession:** Permission to use, release, or accept non-conforming product — **only for non-safety-critical deviations** and with proper justification.
- **Rework:** Must be performed under documented procedures and re-verified.
- **Notification of Regulatory Authorities:** Required for non-conformities that could reasonably be expected to affect safety or performance.

**8.4 Analysis of Data:**
- Includes data on feedback, complaints, non-conforming product, audits, supplier performance, and **regulatory trends**.

**8.5 Improvement:**
- **8.5.1 General:** Identify and implement changes needed to ensure QMS suitability and effectiveness.
- **8.5.2 Corrective Action:** Eliminate the cause of detected non-conformities to prevent recurrence. Must include effectiveness verification.
- **8.5.3 Preventive Action:** Eliminate the cause of **potential** non-conformities. *This is unique to ISO 13485* — ISO 9001:2015 removed preventive action and merged it into risk-based thinking.

---

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Requirement | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ISO 13485:2016 | Module 4 |
| Structure | 10 Clauses (Annex SL framework) | 4.1–8.5 |
| Mandatory Procedures | 6 documented procedures | 4.2.4, 4.2.5, 8.2.4, 8.3, 8.5.2, 8.5.3 |
| Medical Device File | 1 mandatory file per device/family | 4.2.3 |
| Risk Standard | ISO 14971 (referenced throughout) | All clauses |
| Sterilization Standard | ISO 11135, 11137, 17665 | 7.5.5–7.5.7 |
| Implantable Traceability | Records of components, materials, personnel | 7.5.9.2 |
| Management Review | Min. every 12 months (typically quarterly) | 5.6 |
| Internal Audit | Covers all QMS areas over 12 months | 8.2.4 |
| CAPA Effectiveness | Must be verified | 8.5.2 |
| Process Validation | When output cannot be verified later | 7.5.6 |
| Calibration Records | Traceability to national/international standards | 7.6 |

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In the medical device industry, ISO 13485 is the **entry ticket** to the global market:

1. **European Union:** EU MDR 2017/745 mandates ISO 13485 QMS as a presumption of conformity for Class I (measuring, sterile) and higher-risk devices.
2. **United States (FDA):** FDA's Quality System Regulation (21 CFR Part 820) is largely harmonized with ISO 13485. The **QSR & ISO 13485 Harmonization** initiative aims to align them fully.
3. **India (CDSCO):** The Indian Medical Device Rules 2017 adopt a risk-based classification. ISO 13485 certification is widely accepted as evidence of QMS compliance.
4. **Canada, Japan, Australia, Brazil:** All recognize ISO 13485 certification through MDSAP (Medical Device Single Audit Program).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Insight:** For students pursuing careers in medical device companies (e.g., Medtronic, Stryker, Philips, BPL, Trivitron, Perfint Healthcare, or startups in Kerala's MedTech Park), ISO 13485 internal auditor and lead auditor certifications are **highly valued** credentials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Process Approach – The Logical Derivation

The core philosophy of ISO 13485 is the **"Process Approach"** combined with **"Risk-Based Thinking."** Let us derive this logically.

---

### Step 1: Identifying the Process Map

Every medical device manufacturer must identify its QMS processes. A typical process map looks like this:

$$\text{Process Map} = \big\{ P_{\text{Customer}}, P_{\text{Design}}, P_{\text{Purchase}}, P_{\text{Production}}, P_{\text{Verification}}, P_{\text{Distribution}}, P_{\text{Post-Market}} \big\}$$

**Step 1.1:** Identify each process and its owner (Process Owner concept).
**Step 1.2:** Define the **inputs** and **outputs** of each process.
**Step 1.3:** Define the **sequence and interaction** between processes.

---

### Step 2: The Process Interaction Equation

For any process $P_i$, the following relationship must be defined:

$$P_i = f(\text{Inputs}_i, \text{Resources}_i, \text{Methods}_i, \text{Outputs}_i, \text{Acceptance Criteria}_i)$$

Where:
- $\text{Inputs}_i$ = What the process receives (raw materials, design specs, customer orders)
- $\text{Resources}_i$ = People, equipment, infrastructure
- $\text{Methods}_i$ = Procedures, work instructions, software
- $\text{Outputs}_i$ = The deliverable (e.g., a finished device, a test report)
- $\text{Acceptance Criteria}_i$ = The standards the output must meet

> [!NOTE]
> The interaction is bidirectional: the **output of $P_i$** becomes the **input of $P_{i+1}$** in the process chain.

---

### Step 3: Derivation of the CAPA Logic

CAPA is the most-tested sub-clause in KTU. Let us derive the CAPA flow:

$$\text{Non-conformity Detected} \xrightarrow{\text{Containment}} \text{Investigation} \xrightarrow{} \text{Root Cause Analysis} \xrightarrow{} \text{Action Plan}$$

Mathematically, the CAPA effectiveness can be modeled as:

$$\text{Effectiveness Ratio} = \frac{N_{\text{recurrences prevented}}}{N_{\text{CAPAs implemented}}}$$

The standard requires that the **effectiveness of corrective actions** be **verified** and **documented** (8.5.2).

---

### Step 4: Design Control Stage-Gate Derivation

ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3 mandates a structured design and development process. This can be modeled as a series of **stage-gate reviews**:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{input} &\rightarrow D_{review_1} \rightarrow D_{verification} \rightarrow D_{validation} \rightarrow D_{transfer} \\
&\rightarrow D_{review_2} \rightarrow D_{change control} \rightarrow D_{file closure}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- $D_{input}$ = Design inputs (user needs, regulatory requirements, risk outputs from ISO 14971)
- $D_{verification}$ = "Did we design the product right?" (Did the design outputs meet the design inputs?)
- $D_{validation}$ = "Did we design the right product?" (Does the device meet user needs and intended use?)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Veritas of V&V (KTU Classic Question):**
> - **Verification** = Internal consistency check (Design Output ≡ Design Input). Method: Inspection, analysis, simulation.
> - **Validation** = External reality check (Device ≡ User Need & Intended Use). Method: Clinical evaluation, usability testing, in-vivo/in-vitro testing.

---

### Step 5: Risk Management Integration (ISO 13485 ↔ ISO 14971)

The relationship is governed by:

$$\text{Risk Control Measures} \xrightarrow{\text{Implemented in}} \text{Design Outputs, Manufacturing Process, Labeling, Training}$$

Verification of risk control effectiveness must occur in **design verification, design validation, and process validation**. The residual risk must be **acceptable** per the manufacturer's risk acceptability policy.

$$\text{Risk Acceptability} = f(\text{Probability of Harm}, \text{Severity of Harm}, \text{Detectability})$$

This is typically expressed as a **Risk Acceptability Matrix**:

| Severity \ Probability | Improbable (1) | Remote (2) | Occasional (3) | Probable (4) | Frequent (5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Catastrophic (5)** | Medium | High | High | Very High | Unacceptable |
| **Major (4)** | Low | Medium | High | High | Very High |
| **Moderate (3)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Minor (2)** | Negligible | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Negligible (1)** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** While a full FMEA table is rarely asked, expect 2-mark questions asking the student to **classify risk** or **propose a risk control measure** for a given scenario.

---

### Step 6: Process Validation Equation (7.5.6)

For processes where output cannot be verified by subsequent monitoring (e.g., sterilization, welding, software build), the process must be **validated**. The standard 4-step approach is:

$$\text{PV} = \{\text{PQ}, \text{OQ}, \text{VP}\}$$

Where:
- $\text{PQ}$ = **Process Design** (defining the process specifications)
- $\text{OQ}$ = **Process Qualification** (IQ — Installation Qualification, OQ — Operational Qualification, PQ — Performance Qualification)
- $\text{VP}$ = **Validation Protocol** (documented pre-defined acceptance criteria)
- **Re-validation** required when changes occur

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard **explicitly requires** validation for sterilization processes and sterile barrier systems (7.5.7). This is a **mandatory** requirement — not optional.

---

### Step 7: Management Review Output Equation

A common KTU question asks: *"List the inputs and outputs of Management Review."* Let us tabulate:

**Inputs (Clause 5.6.2):**

$$\text{MR Inputs} = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I\}$$

- $A$ = Results of audits
- $B$ = Customer feedback (including complaints)
- $C$ = Process performance and product conformity
- $D$ = Status of CAPA
- $E$ = Follow-up from previous management reviews
- $F$ = Changes affecting the QMS
- $G$ = Recommendations for improvement
- $H$ = New or revised regulatory requirements
- $I$ = Other relevant factors (training effectiveness, supplier performance)

**Outputs (Clause 5.6.3):**

$$\text{MR Outputs} = \{O_1, O_2, O_3\}$$

- $O_1$ = Improvement of the QMS and its processes
- $O_2$ = Improvement of product related to customer requirements
- $O_3$ = Resource needs

---

### Analytical Matrix: ISO 13485 vs ISO 9001

| Aspect | ISO 13485:2016 | ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|
| Primary focus | Regulatory compliance & safety | Customer satisfaction & continual improvement |
| Preventive action | Explicit (Clause 8.5.3) | Replaced by risk-based thinking |
| Risk management | Mandatory integration (ISO 14971) | Implicit (organizational context) |
| Documentation | Heavy (prescriptive) | Flexible (less prescriptive) |
| Management review | Includes regulatory requirements | Includes context of organization |
| Number of mandatory procedures | 6 documented procedures | No mandatory procedures |
| Medical Device File | Required | Not required |
| Cleanliness/Sterilization | Specific clauses (7.5.2, 7.5.5) | Not specifically addressed |
| Customer communication | Includes advisory notices | Generic |
| Continual improvement | Required but secondary | Central pillar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked *"How is ISO 13485 different from ISO 9001?"* — emphasize **regulatory orientation, prescriptive documentation, risk management integration, and medical device-specific clauses** (sterilization, traceability, complaints, advisory notices).

---

### Derivation: The "Why" Behind Process Validation

**Why must sterilization be validated when other processes may not be?**

The answer lies in Clause 7.5.6. Sterilization is one of the few processes where:
- The output (sterility) **cannot be verified by inspection** of every unit.
- Defects are **latent** (microbial contamination is invisible).
- A single failure can **kill a patient**.

Therefore, validation is the **only** way to ensure consistent sterility assurance level (SAL) of $10^{-6}$ (probability of a non-sterile unit being ≤ 1 in a million).

$$\text{SAL} \leq 10^{-6}$$

This is the mathematical benchmark used in sterilization validation per ISO 11137 (radiation) and ISO 11135 (EtO gas).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"List the six mandatory documented procedures required by ISO 13485:2016."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The six mandatory documented procedures mandated by ISO 13485:2016 are:

1. **Control of Documents** (Clause 4.2.4) — Defines how QMS documents are created, reviewed, approved, distributed, and archived.
2. **Control of Records** (Clause 4.2.5) — Defines the retention, protection, retrieval, and disposition of records.
3. **Internal Audit** (Clause 8.2.4) — Defines audit planning, frequency, criteria, scope, and reporting.
4. **Control of Non-Conforming Product** (Clause 8.3) — Defines identification, segregation, and disposition of non-conforming products.
5. **Corrective Action** (Clause 8.5.2) — Defines the process to eliminate causes of detected non-conformities.
6. **Preventive Action** (Clause 8.5.3) — Defines the process to eliminate potential non-conformities.

In addition, a **Medical Device File (MDF)** is mandatory for each device or device family (Clause 4.2.3).

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"Explain the difference between Design Verification and Design Validation as per ISO 13485."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Design Verification (7.3.5) | Design Validation (7.3.6) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Confirmation that design outputs meet design inputs | Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use |
| **Question Answered** | "Did we design the product right?" | "Did we design the right product?" |
| **Methods** | Inspection, calculation, analysis, simulation | Clinical evaluation, usability testing, in-vivo testing |
| **Timing** | Throughout the design process | Towards the end of design, on production-equivalent units |
| **Sample Size** | Can use design models or prototypes | Uses production-equivalent units under defined conditions |

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **clause-wise structure of ISO 13485:2016**. Discuss the role of **risk management (ISO 14971)** in each phase of the medical device life-cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **Design and Development process as per Clause 7.3 of ISO 13485**. Highlight the role of **Design Review, Verification, and Validation**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**1. Clause-wise Structure (3.5 marks):**
ISO 13485:2016 is organized into **8 main clauses** (0–8) following the Annex SL high-level structure:

- **Clause 0–3: Introduction, Scope, Normative References, Terms & Definitions.**
- **Clause 4: Quality Management System** — Process requirements, documentation (6 procedures + MDF).
- **Clause 5: Management Responsibility** — Commitment, customer focus, quality policy, objectives, responsibility, MR, management review.
- **Clause 6: Resource Management** — HR, infrastructure, work environment, contamination control.
- **Clause 7: Product Realization** — Planning, customer-related, design & development, purchasing, production & service, control of monitoring equipment.
- **Clause 8: Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement** — Feedback, complaints, internal audit, NC product, data analysis, CAPA, improvement.

**2. Risk Management Integration (3.5 marks):**
ISO 14971 is referenced throughout ISO 13485. The integration is:

- **Product Realization Planning (7.1):** Risk management activities must be planned and documented.
- **Design Inputs (7.3.2):** Include risk control measures from ISO 14971.
- **Design Outputs (7.3.3):** Must include residual risk information.
- **Design Verification & Validation (7.3.5, 7.3.6):** Verify risk control effectiveness.
- **Process Validation (7.5.6):** Validate that manufacturing processes maintain risk controls.
- **Production Traceability (7.5.9):** Enables recall if post-market risk emerges.
- **Customer Feedback (8.2.1):** Production information feeds back into ISO 14971 risk management review.
- **Improvement (8.5):** Risk data drives CAPA and risk file updates.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Design and Development Flow (7 marks):**

The design and development process per ISO 13485 Clause 7.3 is a **structured, iterative, stage-gated process**:

1. **Design and Development Planning (7.3.1):** Define stages, reviews, verification, validation, and responsibilities. Each project must have a **Design and Development Plan**.

2. **Design and Development Inputs (7.3.2):** Include:
   - Intended use and user requirements
   - Performance and safety requirements
   - Applicable regulatory requirements
   - Output of risk management
   - Information from previous similar designs

3. **Design and Development Outputs (7.3.3):** Must be in a form suitable for verification against inputs and must include:
   - Drawings, specifications, manufacturing instructions
   - Acceptance criteria
   - Labeling and packaging specifications
   - Risk management output

4. **Design and Development Review (7.3.4):** Systematic review at planned stages with participants including representatives of functions concerned. Records must be maintained.

5. **Design and Development Verification (7.3.5):** Confirms that outputs meet inputs. Methods include calculations, tests, inspections, and design reviews.

6. **Design and Development Validation (7.3.6):** Confirms the device meets user needs and intended use. Performed on production-equivalent units under simulated or actual use conditions. Includes **clinical evaluation**.

7. **Design and Development Transfer (7.3.8):** Ensures that design outputs are correctly translated into production specifications. Includes training of production personnel and validation of manufacturing processes.

8. **Control of Design Changes (7.3.7):** Changes must be reviewed, verified, validated, and approved before implementation.

9. **Records and Files (7.3.9, 7.3.10):** Maintained in the **Medical Device File**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **Verification** with **Validation**. Memorize: **"Verification = Right Design; Validation = Right Product."**
> - Many students **omit Clinical Evaluation** as part of design validation — this is mandatory for medical devices.
> - Failing to mention **Design Transfer** as a separate sub-clause costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **CAPA**? Explain the **Corrective Action process** as per ISO 13485:2016 with a suitable flow diagram. How is the **effectiveness of corrective action verified**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Management Review requirements** of ISO 13485:2016. What are the **mandatory inputs and outputs** of a management review meeting?

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**1. Definition of CAPA (1 mark):**
**CAPA** stands for **Corrective and Preventive Action**. It is a systematic QMS process to **eliminate the causes of detected non-conformities (Corrective Action)** and **potential non-conformities (Preventive Action)** to prevent recurrence or occurrence.

**2. Corrective Action Process (5 marks):**

The Corrective Action Procedure per ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 8.5.2) involves the following steps:

1. **Identify the Non-Conformity:** From complaints, audits, inspections, or process monitoring.
2. **Containment:** Quarantine the affected product to prevent unintended use or delivery.
3. **Document the NC:** Record the problem, scope, and impact.
4. **Investigate the Cause:** Use tools like **5-Why analysis**, **Fishbone diagram (Ishikawa)**, or **FMEA** to determine the **root cause**.
5. **Determine Action Plan:** Define actions to eliminate the root cause and prevent recurrence.
6. **Implement Action:** Execute the action plan (e.g., change process, retrain staff, modify design).
7. **Verify Effectiveness:** After implementation, verify whether the action has eliminated the root cause and prevented recurrence. **Records of this verification must be kept**.
8. **Update Risk Management File:** If the NC relates to product risk, update the risk file per ISO 14971.
9. **Close the CAPA:** Document the closure and lessons learned.

**3. Effectiveness Verification (1 mark):**
The organization must **review the effectiveness** of any corrective action taken. This may involve:
- Re-auditing the affected process.
- Monitoring complaint trends.
- Verifying that the same NC has not recurred within a defined period.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write a **generic CAPA** without specifying the **medical device-specific elements** (e.g., update of risk file, regulatory reporting, complaint linkage).
> - Skipping the **"verify effectiveness"** step is the most common error — it loses 2 marks.
> - Preventive Action (8.5.3) is **distinct from** Corrective Action (8.5.2) and addresses **potential** non-conformities.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Management Review (Clause 5.6) (7 marks):**

**1. Purpose (1 mark):**
Management Review is a **periodic, formal review** of the QMS conducted by **top management** to ensure its continuing suitability, adequacy, effectiveness, and alignment with regulatory requirements.

**2. Frequency (1 mark):**
Management reviews must be conducted at **planned intervals** (typically every 6 to 12 months). Any need for **additional reviews** (e.g., major non-conformity, regulatory change) must be addressed promptly.

**3. Inputs to Management Review (Clause 5.6.2) (3 marks):**

The review must include:
- **(a) Results of audits** (internal and external)
- **(b) Customer feedback** (including complaints)
- **(c) Process performance and product conformity**
- **(d) Status of preventive and corrective actions**
- **(e) Follow-up actions from previous management reviews**
- **(f) Changes that could affect the QMS**
- **(g) Recommendations for improvement**
- **(h) New or revised regulatory requirements**
- **(i) Other relevant factors** (e.g., training effectiveness, supplier performance, post-market surveillance data)

**4. Outputs of Management Review (Clause 5.6.3) (2 marks):**

The output must include:
- **Improvement of the QMS and its processes**
- **Improvement of product** related to customer requirements
- **Resource needs**

**5. Records (1 mark):**
Records of management review must be **maintained** (typically meeting minutes, attendance list, action items with owners and due dates).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Most students **omit the regulatory changes input** — this is unique to ISO 13485 and worth 1 mark.
> - The Management Review is **not the same as** a quality meeting or department meeting — it is a **top-management-led, formal, documented QMS review**.
> - The **output must include resource needs** — students commonly miss this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet — ISO 13485 Deeper Understanding**

- ✅ **ISO 13485:2016** is the medical device QMS standard published by ISO, titled *"for regulatory purposes."*
- ✅ **8 main clauses** following **Annex SL** structure: 0–3 (Introduction), 4 (QMS), 5 (Management), 6 (Resources), 7 (Product Realization), 8 (Improvement).
- ✅ **6 mandatory procedures** + **1 mandatory Medical Device File** = 7 mandatory document sets.
- ✅ **Process approach + Risk-based thinking** is the dual foundation.
- ✅ **ISO 14971** is the risk management standard referenced throughout.
- ✅ **Clause 7.3** (Design & Development) has **10 sub-clauses** — most heavily tested area.
- ✅ **Verification** = "Right Design?" (Output vs. Input); **Validation** = "Right Product?" (Device vs. User Need).
- ✅ **Design Transfer** is a distinct sub-clause (7.3.8) — not a vague step.
- ✅ **Implantable devices** require full **traceability** (Clause 7.5.9.2).
- ✅ **Sterilization processes** must be **validated** (Clauses 7.5.5, 7.5.6, 7.5.7).
- ✅ **CAPA** = Corrective (8.5.2) + Preventive (8.5.3); **Effectiveness must be verified**.
- ✅ **Internal Audit** (8.2.4) must be **independent** of the area audited.
- ✅ **Complaint handling** (8.2.2) is a **documented procedure** for timely processing.
- ✅ **Advisory Notices** (8.2.3) must be issued to regulatory authorities for safety-impacting non-conformities.
- ✅ **Management Review Inputs (9 items)** — memorize the 9 listed in Clause 5.6.2.
- ✅ **Management Review Outputs (3 items)** — QMS improvement, product improvement, resource needs.
- ✅ **Concession** (8.3) is allowed only for non-safety deviations with proper justification.
- ✅ **Process validation** (7.5.6) is required where output cannot be verified later (e.g., welding, sterilization).
- ✅ **Calibration records** must be traceable to **national/international standards** (7.6).
- ✅ **Preventive Action** is a **distinctive feature** of ISO 13485 — ISO 9001:2015 removed it.
- ✅ **Comparison with ISO 9001:** Focus on regulatory compliance, prescriptive documentation, risk integration, MD-specific clauses.
- ✅ **Real-world compliance** required for EU MDR, FDA QSR, CDSCO India, MDSAP (Canada/Japan/Australia/Brazil).
- ✅ **Auditor independence** is mandatory — auditors cannot audit their own work.
- ✅ **Risk Acceptability Matrix** uses Probability × Severity dimensions.
- ✅ **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) ≤ 10⁻⁶** is the universal benchmark for sterilization.
- ✅ **Customer Focus** in ISO 13485 includes end-users, patients, clinicians, and regulators.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hall Pro Tip:** For any 14-mark question on ISO 13485, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition / Introduction** (2 marks)
> 2. **Clause-wise breakdown** (3–4 marks)
> 3. **Process flow / diagram explanation** (3–4 marks)
> 4. **Real-world example / industry relevance** (2–3 marks)
> 5. **Conclusion / KTU-specific relevance** (1 mark)
>
> This template covers the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels expected in KTU 2024 Scheme exams: **Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate**.
