# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the "Risk Control Hierarchy" order.** Most students list IFU as the first option — it is the **last** (third) option. Marks are routinely deducted for this mistake.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the feedback loop.** Risk Management does not stop at market launch. Clause 11 (Production and Post-Production) is a separate, mark-bearing clause.
> 3. **Do NOT use generic safety engineering text** in answers. KTU examiners expect **clause-numbered, ISO 14971-specific** language (e.g., "Clause 5.1 — Risk Analysis").
> 4. **Do NOT forget secondary risks.** Every risk control measure must be reviewed for new hazards introduced (Clause 7.4 c).
> 5. **Do NOT skip the Residual Risk Evaluation.** Even after risk control, the residual risk must be re-evaluated against the same acceptability criteria (Clause 7.5).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "Risk Management File" as per ISO 14971:2019. List any four documents it should contain.**

**Model Answer:**

> As per **Clause 3.23 of ISO 14971:2019**, a Risk Management File is the **set of records and other documents that are produced by risk management**.

It should contain (any four):
1. Risk Management Policy
2. Risk Management Plan
3. Risk Analysis records
4. Risk Evaluation records
5. Risk Control measures and their verification records
6. Residual Risk Evaluation
7. Benefit-Risk Analysis records
8. Risk Management Review Report
9. Production and Post-Production Information

**Key Point:** It is a **living document set**, not a single file. The manufacturer is responsible for maintaining it throughout the device life cycle.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between "Hazard", "Hazardous Situation", and "Harm" with one example each, as defined in ISO 14971:2019.**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Example (Insulin Pump) |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** (Clause 3.4) | A potential source of harm. | Software algorithm bug in the dose calculator. |
| **Hazardous Situation** (Clause 3.5) | A circumstance in which people, property, or the environment are exposed to one or more hazards. | The patient receives the erroneously calculated dose. |
| **Harm** (Clause 3.3) | Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment. | Hypoglycemic coma resulting from overdose. |

**Key Point:** The standard requires the manufacturer to identify the **causal chain** Hazard → Hazardous Situation → Harm, not just the hazard in isolation. Marks are awarded for the **directionality** of the chain.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Answer both sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: ISO 14971 is a horizontal standard that specifies a risk management process for medical device manufacturers.**

**(a)** Describe the **Risk Management Process** as specified in **Clause 4 of ISO 14971:2019**, explaining the role of the Risk Management Policy, the Risk Management Plan, and the Risk Management File. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A manufacturer is developing a new **infusion pump** for intravenous drug delivery. Identify **three hazards**, the corresponding **hazardous situations**, and **harm** for each. Apply the **Risk Control Hierarchy of Clause 7.3** to one of these hazards, showing at least one risk control at each of the three levels. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

**(a) Risk Management Process per Clause 4 [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
As per Clause 4.1, the manufacturer must establish, document, and maintain a **continuous, iterative risk management process** throughout the life cycle of the medical device.

**Step 2 — Risk Management Policy (2 marks):**
Defined in Clause 4.2, the **Risk Management Policy** is a top-level document that:
- Defines the manufacturer's **risk acceptability criteria** (the threshold above which risk is deemed unacceptable).
- Allocates **management responsibility** for risk management.
- Is **approved by top management**.

**Step 3 — Risk Management Plan (2 marks):**
Defined in Clause 4.4, the **Risk Management Plan** is a per-device document that specifies:
- The **scope** of the risk management activities.
- The **risk acceptability criteria** to be applied.
- **Verification activities** for risk control measures.
- **Collection and review** of production and post-production information.
- **Assignment of responsibilities** for each risk management task.

**Step 4 — Risk Management File (2 marks):**
Defined in Clause 4.5, the **Risk Management File** is the **complete set of records** generated by the risk management process. It includes:
- Policy, Plan, Analysis, Evaluation, Control measures, Verification, Residual Risk Evaluation, Benefit-Risk Analysis, Risk Management Review Report, and Production/Post-Production information.

**Closing statement:** The process is **iterative** — outputs of Clause 11 (Production and Post-Production) feed back into Clause 5 (Risk Analysis), as required by the PDCA model.

---

**(b) Risk Analysis and Risk Control of an Infusion Pump [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Three Hazards, Hazardous Situations, and Harms (3 marks):**

| Hazard ID | Hazard | Hazardous Situation | Harm |
|---|---|---|---|
| HP1 | Software algorithm error | Incorrect flow rate delivered to patient | Drug overdose / underdose |
| HP2 | Air bubble in IV line | Bubble enters patient's bloodstream | Air embolism, stroke, death |
| HP3 | Battery failure | Pump stops mid-infusion | Interruption of critical medication |

**Step 2 — Application of Risk Control Hierarchy to HP1 (4 marks):**

| Level | Control Option (Clause 7.3) | Specific Measure for HP1 | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 — Inherent Safe Design** | Remove the hazard or reduce its severity by design | Build a maximum-dose software interlock that cannot be bypassed | Unit testing, code review, FMEA |
| **2 — Protective Measures** | Add safety guards or alarms independent of the failed system | Add an independent air-in-line sensor that triggers an audible alarm and halts infusion | Bench testing, sensor calibration records |
| **3 — Information for Safety** | Add IFU, training, and labelling | Label the pump "For trained clinical staff only" and add a user manual warning | User comprehension testing |

**Closing statement (per Clause 7.4 c):** Each of the above controls must be reviewed to ensure it does **not introduce new hazards** (e.g., the alarm's battery itself must not fail). The residual risk after all three controls is re-evaluated per Clause 7.5.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Modern risk-based regulatory frameworks rely heavily on the structured approach of ISO 14971.**

**(a)** Explain the **Risk Analysis process (Clause 5)** with emphasis on the causal chain **Hazard → Hazardous Situation → Harm**. Describe how the manufacturer estimates **severity** and **probability**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Benefit-Risk Analysis of Clause 8** in ISO 14971:2019. Why was this clause given greater emphasis in the 2019 edition compared to 2007? Provide a hypothetical example where the benefit outweighs the residual risk. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

**(a) Risk Analysis per Clause 5 [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Clause 5.1 of ISO 14971:2019 defines Risk Analysis as the **systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk** associated with them.

**Step 2 — Causal Chain (2 marks):**
The process follows a **causal chain**: **Hazard → Hazardous Situation → Harm**.
- **Hazard** (Clause 3.4): Potential source of harm (e.g., a sharp needle).
- **Hazardous Situation** (Clause 3.5): The circumstance in which a person is exposed to the hazard (e.g., the patient is pricked by the needle).
- **Harm** (Clause 3.3): The resulting physical injury or damage to health (e.g., transmission of blood-borne infection).

**Step 3 — Severity Estimation (1.5 marks):**
Severity is estimated on a **semantic scale** (e.g., Negligible, Minor, Serious, Critical, Catastrophic) and represents the **worst credible consequence** to the patient or user.

**Step 4 — Probability Estimation (1.5 marks):**
Probability is estimated for **each hazardous situation** and for **each harm** given the situation. The standard allows **qualitative scales** (e.g., Frequent, Occasional, Remote, Improbable) or **quantitative rates** (e.g., failures per million uses) — the choice depends on data availability and the manufacturer's established methods.

**Step 5 — Risk Combination (1 mark):**
The two are combined as: **R = P × S**, yielding a semiquantitative risk level that can be plotted on a **5×5 risk acceptability matrix** for evaluation (Clause 6).

---

**(b) Benefit-Risk Analysis per Clause 8 [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1.5 marks):**
Clause 8 of ISO 14971:2019 requires that for each individual residual risk that is **not acceptable** and for which **further risk control is not practicable**, the manufacturer must perform a **Benefit-Risk Analysis**. The output is a documented conclusion on whether the **medical benefit** outweighs the **residual risk**.

**Step 2 — Why Clause 8 was Strengthened in 2019 (2.5 marks):**
- The 2007 edition focused on **risk reduction** as the primary goal — if risk could be reduced, it must be.
- The 2019 edition recognizes that **eliminating all residual risk is sometimes impossible** (e.g., life-saving implantable devices inherently carry surgical and long-term risks).
- The 2019 edition aligns ISO 14971 with **EU MDR 2017/745** and **FDA guidance**, which explicitly require a benefit-risk determination.
- It incorporates the **state-of-the-art** principle: a device is acceptable if its residual risk is consistent with the state of the art for similar devices, **and** if its benefits outweigh that risk.

**Step 3 — Hypothetical Example (3 marks):**

> **Example:** A new **implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)** can deliver a 30-joule shock to restart the heart during ventricular fibrillation.
>
> - **Residual Risk:** The ICD battery can rarely (1 in 10,000 patient-years) fail without warning, leading to sudden cardiac death of the patient.
> - **Risk Control:** Further miniaturization of the battery to eliminate the failure mode is **not practicable** with current technology (state-of-the-art limit).
> - **Medical Benefit:** The ICD reduces sudden cardiac death by **50%** in high-risk patients (per clinical trial data).
> - **Benefit-Risk Conclusion:** The benefit (50% reduction in mortality) **substantially outweighs** the residual risk (1 in 10,000 patient-year battery failure).
> - **Decision:** The device **may be released** to the market, with documented post-market surveillance (Clause 11) to detect any change in this benefit-risk balance.

**Closing statement (per Clause 9):** The **overall residual risk** of the device, combined with all other residual risks, must also be acceptable. If not, the device cannot be placed on the market.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — ISO 14971**

- **Standard:** ISO 14971:2019 (third edition); horizontal; applies to all medical devices and IVDs.
- **Core formula:** $R = P \times S$ (qualitative) and $RPN = S \times P \times D$ (FMEA-based).
- **Causal Chain:** Hazard → Hazardous Situation → Harm. Always in this order.
- **Risk Control Hierarchy (Clause 7.3):**
  1. **Inherent Safe Design** (highest priority)
  2. **Protective Measures** (in the device or process)
  3. **Information for Safety** (IFU, labels, training) — lowest priority, **NOT first**.
- **Benefit-Risk Analysis (Clause 8)** is invoked only when:
  - Residual risk is **not acceptable**, AND
  - Further risk reduction is **not practicable**.
- **Risk Management File (Clause 4.5)** is a **collection of records**, not a single document.
- **Risk Management Review (Clause 10)** is a **formal sign-off** by management before market release.
- **Production and Post-Production (Clause 11)** is **mandatory feedback** to the risk analysis loop.
- **Risk Acceptability Matrix** is **5×5** in most KTU exam answers; **red zone (≥13) = unacceptable**.
- **ALARP** = "As Low As Reasonably Practicable" — used in the yellow zone.
- **State of the Art** is an explicit consideration in Clause 9 (Overall Residual Risk Evaluation).
- **New hazards from risk controls** must be analyzed (Clause 7.4 c) — a common exam trap.
- **EU MDR, FDA 21 CFR 820, and Indian MDR 2017** all reference or require ISO 14971.
- **IEC 62366-1 (Usability), IEC 62304 (Software), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility)** integrate with ISO 14971.
- **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** is the underlying process model of Clause 4.
- **Reasonably Foreseeable Misuse (Clause 5.2)** — must be considered; cannot be ignored.
- **Verification (Clause 7.2)** — every risk control measure must be verified to have actually reduced the risk.
- **Documentation** is the **key auditable deliverable** — no risk management file, no regulatory clearance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unique Device Identification (UDI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Unique Device Identification (UDI) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unique Device Identification (UDI)** is a system mandated by global medical device regulatory authorities (FDA, EU MDR, IMDRF) that requires every medical device and its packaging to be marked with a unique numeric or alphanumeric code, enabling unambiguous identification of the device throughout its lifecycle—from manufacturing and distribution to patient use and post-market surveillance. The UDI system consists of two primary components as defined by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)**: | Component | Full Form | Function | |-----------|-----------|----------| | **UDI-DI** | Device Identifier | Mandatory, static identifier specific to a **device model/version** (the "license plate of the device type") | | **UDI-PI** | Production Identifier | Conditional, dynamic identifier carrying **variable production data** (lot, serial, expiry, manufacture date) | The complete UDI is the concatenation: **UDI = UDI-DI + UDI-PI**, applied to the device label, packaging, and in some cases, the device itself (e.g., reusable surgical instruments). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of UDI like the Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) of a car, but applied to medical devices.** Every car has a **17-character VIN** that uniquely identifies the make, model, year, and manufacturing plant. If a car is recalled for a faulty airbag, the manufacturer does NOT need to recall every car of that model; instead, they can identify exactly **which production batch** is affected. The UDI works the same way for medical devices: - The **UDI-DI** is like the car model (e.g., "Toyota Camry 2024") — a unique identifier for that design/version. - The **UDI-PI** is like the production sequence number — it tells you *which specific unit* was made on *which day*, in *which batch*. When a faulty pacemaker leads or a contaminated infusion pump is reported, regulators and manufacturers can use the UDI to: 1. **Trace** every affected unit in seconds. 2. **Recal**l only the affected batch (not the entire product line). 3. **Notify** hospitals and patients precisely. 4. **Analyze** post-market adverse events with surgical precision. Without UDI, the industry relied on free-text descriptions, proprietary catalog numbers, and manual reconciliation — leading to errors, delayed recalls, and patient harm. ## 1.3 Why UDI Exists — The Regulatory Imperative > [!IMPORTANT] > The UDI system was born out of real-world patient safety crises and supply chain inefficiencies. It is the **foundational layer of modern medical device traceability**. **Key drivers for UDI adoption:** - **Patient Safety:** Faster, more accurate recalls and adverse event reporting. - **Supply Chain Integrity:** Combat counterfeiting and grey-market diversion. - **Post-Market Surveillance:** Link real-world clinical data to specific device versions. - **Electronic Health Records (EHR) Integration:** Enable automated capture of device data in patient records. - **Global Harmonization:** Allow regulators worldwide to share device identification data seamlessly. ## 1.4 UDI Carriers — How UDI is Physically Represented A UDI must be presented in two formats simultaneously on the label: | Carrier Type | Full Form | Purpose | Example | |--------------|-----------|---------|---------| | **AIDC** | Automatic Identification and Data Capture | Machine-readable code for scanners (barcode/2D/RFID) | `(01)00382903920123(17)250131(10)ABC123` | | **HRI** | Human Readable Interpretation | Plain-text alphanumeric readable by humans | `UDI-DI: 00382903920123, EXP: 2025-01-31, LOT: ABC123` | > [!NOTE] > **AIDC formats include:** > - **GS1:** DataMatrix, GS1-128, EAN-13 > - **HIBCC:** HIBCC DataMatrix, Code 128 > - **ICCBBA:** ISBT 128 (primarily for cellular/tissue products) > > These three entities are **FDA-accredited Issuing Agencies** for UDI assignment in the US. The EU MDR adds additional agencies (e.g., GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA, Zollverein). ## 1.5 Regulatory Framework — Who Mandates UDI | Regulator | Region | UDI Mandate | Key Database | |-----------|--------|-------------|--------------| | **US FDA** | United States | 21 CFR Part 801, 21 CFR Part 830 | **GUDID** (Global Unique Device Identification Database) | | **European Commission** | EU + EEA | EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27 | **EUDAMED** (European Database on Medical Devices) | | **Health Canada** | Canada | Medical Device Regulations amendments | **UDID** (Canada's UDI Database) | | **NMPA (China)** | China | Order No. 104 | **CUDID** (China UDI Database) | | **IMDRF** | Global Harmonization | UDI Guidance (2013) | Global guidance, not a regulator | | **TGA (Australia)** | Australia | Australian Regulatory Guidelines | TGA UDI Hub | | **PMDA (Japan)** | Japan | PMD Act | J-MUD | ## 1.6 UDI Assignment Workflow — The Big Picture The UDI assignment follows a strict hierarchical workflow: 1. **Manufacturer** determines the appropriate UDI-DI based on regulatory rules (e.g., model, size, sterilization method, packaging configuration). 2. **Manufacturer** acquires the UDI-DI from an **Accredited Issuing Agency** (e.g., GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA). 3. **Manufacturer** assigns the **UDI-PI** (lot number, serial number, expiry, manufacture date). 4. **Manufacturer** submits UDI-DI and core device attributes to the regulator's **UDI Database** (GUDID, EUDAMED). 5. **Manufacturer** affixes UDI in both AIDC and HRI formats on the **label, package, and/or device**. 6. **Distributors, hospitals, and clinicians** scan/use the UDI for inventory, EHR, recall, and adverse event reporting. 7. **Regulators** use the database for surveillance, recall management, and market oversight. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a UDI — The Complete Structure A complete UDI, when fully expanded, is not just one number but a structured concatenation of identifiers following **ISO/IEC 15459** and **ISO/IEC 15418** standards. The structure follows Application Identifiers (AI) prefixed in the machine-readable form: $$ \text{UDI}_{\text{full}} = \underbrace{(01)\text{GS1-DI}}_{\text{Device Identifier}} + \underbrace{(17)\text{EXP}}_{\text{Expiry}} + \underbrace{(10)\text{LOT}}_{\text{Lot Number}} + \underbrace{(11)\text{PROD}}_{\text{Production Date}} + \underbrace{(21)\text{SERIAL}}_{\text{Serial Number}} $$ > [!NOTE] > Not all UDI-PI elements are required for every device. The **UDI-PI is conditional** based on device characteristics: > - **Devices requiring sterilization:** UDI-PI must include sterilization expiry date. > - **Devices with lot/batch traceability:** UDI-PI must include lot number. > - **Devices intended for single use or unique implants:** UDI-PI must include serial number. > - **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD):** UDI-PI is typically a software version number (not a physical production code). ## 2.2 UDI-DI Assignment Rules — The "Versioning" Logic The UDI-DI is the **static identifier** of a device model. It is assigned once and remains constant for all units of that specific version. However, a new UDI-DI is required when **any change** affects: - **Device model number or part number** - **Packaging configuration** (e.g., single-pack vs. 10-pack) - **Sterilization method** (e.g., EO vs. gamma) - **Critical warnings, contraindications, or indications for use** that appear on the label - **MRI compatibility status** (MR-Safe, MR-Conditional, MR-Unsafe) - **Software version** (for SaMD) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assume that a UDI-DI changes with every production run. **This is FALSE.** The UDI-DI is version-stable and changes only when the device *design* or *label* changes. The UDI-PI is what changes per production run. ## 2.3 UDI-PI Variability — What's "Inside" the Production Identifier The UDI-PI elements are dictated by device type and labeling requirements. The exhaustive set of UDI-PI components includes: | AI Code | Element | Mandatory When | Example | |---------|---------|----------------|---------| | `(10)` | Batch/Lot Number | Lot-controlled devices | `LOT12345` | | `(11)` | Production Date | Date-of-manufacture required | `2024-03-15` | | `(15)` | Best Before Date | Shelf-life critical products | `2026-03-15` | | `(17)` | Expiration Date | Sterile devices | `2025-09-30` | | `(21)` | Serial Number | Single-use, implants, reusable surgical tools | `SN-9876543` | ## 2.4 The UDI Assignment Decision Logic To determine what constitutes a "different" device model requiring a new UDI-DI, the manufacturer follows this decision matrix: $$ \text{New UDI-DI Required} = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i $$ where each $\Delta_i$ represents a change in one of the following attributes: - $\Delta_1$ = Change in catalog/model number - $\Delta_2$ = Change in package quantity or configuration - $\Delta_3$ = Change in sterilization method - $\Delta_4$ = Change in label (warnings, indications, MR status) - $\Delta_5$ = Change in material (if affecting biocompatibility classification) - $\Delta_6$ = Change in software major version (for SaMD) If **any** $\Delta_i$ is true, a new UDI-DI must be assigned. The logical OR ($\vee$) means a single change triggers re-assignment. ## 2.5 UDI Hierarchy in Healthcare Delivery The UDI is not used in isolation. It integrates into a multi-tiered identification hierarchy: $$ \text{GTIN (Trade Item)} \rightarrow \text{UDI-DI (Device Version)} \rightarrow \text{UDI-PI (Specific Unit)} \rightarrow \text{Serialized UDI (sUDI)} $$ The **sUDI** is the highest specificity level — a single identifiable physical device unit. This is critical for: - **Implants** (pacemakers, hip prostheses) where post-implantation tracking is mandatory. - **Pharmacy compounding** where the device is "consumed" in a procedure. - **Single-use devices** requiring recall precision. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | **Parameter** | **Specification** | |---------------|-------------------| | **Originating Body (US)** | US FDA, 21 CFR Part 830 | | **Originating Body (EU)** | European Commission, EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27 | | **Global Harmonization Body** | IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) | | **US UDI Database** | **GUDID** — Global Unique Device Identification Database | | **EU UDI Database** | **EUDAMED** — European Database on Medical Devices | | **Issuing Agencies (US)** | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA | | **Issuing Agencies (EU)** | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA GmbH, Informationsstelle für Arzneimittelspezialitäten | | **UDI Components** | UDI-DI (static) + UDI-PI (variable) | | **Carrier Formats** | AIDC (machine) + HRI (human) | | **AIDC Tech Standards** | GS1 DataMatrix, GS1-128, HIBCC DataMatrix, ISBT 128, RFID | | **UDI-DI Trigger for New Assignment** | Model, packaging, sterilization, label, software version change | | **Mandatory UDI-PI for Implants** | Serial Number (`(21)`) | | **Mandatory UDI-PI for Sterile Devices** | Expiration Date (`(17)`) | | **Direct Marking Required For** | Reusable devices (e.g., surgical instruments) intended for multiple uses | | **U.S. Compliance Phase** | Class III → Class II → Class I (rolled out 2014–2022) | | **EU MDR Compliance Date** | May 26, 2021 (with transition extensions) | | **Software SaMD UDI** | UDI-DI is at the **system level**; UDI-PI is the software version | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The UDI system is not just a regulatory checkbox; it has direct engineering and clinical utility: 1. **Automated Adverse Event Reporting:** Hospitals can configure EHR systems to auto-populate the UDI of implanted devices during revision surgeries, linking outcome data to specific device versions. 2. **Predictive Recall Modeling:** Manufacturers can use UDI-tagged field data to identify failure clusters by production lot, geographic region, or hospital site. 3. **Counterfeit Detection:** Scanning UDI at customs and hospital receiving can instantly flag devices that do not match the regulator's database entry. 4. **Clinical Decision Support:** EHR-integrated UDI lookup can alert clinicians to known device issues (e.g., "This specific pacemaker firmware is under recall — consult patient"). 5. **Supply Chain Analytics:** Real-time inventory tracking using UDI reduces stockouts and expired-product waste. # 3. Analytical Matrix & Step-by-Step Decision Walkthroughs ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — UDI Component Selection for Common Device Classes | Device Class | UDI-DI Required? | UDI-PI — Lot | UDI-PI — Serial | UDI-PI — Expiry | UDI-PI — Mfg Date | Direct Marking | Example | |--------------|:----------------:|:------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:|---------| | **Class III Implant (Pacemaker)** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Serial-numbered sterile implant | | **Class II Sterile Device (Syringe)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Lot-controlled sterile consumable | | **Class I Non-Sterile (Stethoscope)** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Simple labeling device | | **Class II Reusable Surgical Instrument** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | Forceps, retractors (direct mark mandatory) | | **Software as Medical Device (SaMD)** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | N/A (no physical label) | UDI-PI = software version number | | **Cellular/Tissue Product** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ISBT 128 carrier format required | | **In-Vitro Diagnostic (IVD)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | Reagent kits, control materials | ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — UDI-DI Change Decision **Scenario:** A manufacturer currently markets an orthopedic hip stem (Catalog Number HS-100) in the following configurations: - Configuration A: Titanium alloy, 12mm diameter, sterile, single-pack - Configuration B: Titanium alloy, 14mm diameter, sterile, single-pack - Configuration C: Cobalt-Chromium alloy, 12mm diameter, sterile, single-pack **Question:** How many UDI-DIs are required? Justify using the decision logic. **Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify distinguishing attributes.** $$ \text{Attributes} = \{ \text{Alloy}, \text{Diameter}, \text{Packaging}, \text{Sterility} \} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the OR-logic decision rule for each configuration.** For Configuration A vs. B: $$ \Delta_{\text{diameter}} = 12 \rightarrow 14 = \text{TRUE} $$ $$ \therefore \text{New UDI-DI required for B} $$ For Configuration A vs. C: $$ \Delta_{\text{alloy}} = \text{Ti} \rightarrow \text{CoCr} = \text{TRUE} $$ $$ \therefore \text{New UDI-DI required for C} $$ **Step 3 — Final UDI-DI count.** $$ N_{\text{UDI-DI}} = 3 \text{ (one for each unique configuration)} $$ Each configuration is a distinct device model under FDA/EU MDR rules. The UDI-PI (e.g., serial number, expiry) will vary unit-by-unit within each UDI-DI. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Decoding a GS1-Compliant UDI **Given machine-readable UDI string:** ``` (01)00382903920123(17)250131(10)LOT-2024-A(21)SN-9876543 ``` **Step 1 — Parse the Application Identifiers (AIs).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AI } (01) &\rightarrow \text{GTIN (UDI-DI)} = 00382903920123 \\ \text{AI } (17) &\rightarrow \text{Expiry Date} = 250131 \text{ (2025-01-31, YYMMDD)} \\ \text{AI } (10) &\rightarrow \text{Lot Number} = \text{LOT-2024-A} \\ \text{AI } (21) &\rightarrow \text{Serial Number} = \text{SN-9876543} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Classify each element as UDI-DI or UDI-PI.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{UDI-DI} &= 00382903920123 \\ \text{UDI-PI} &= \{ 250131, \text{LOT-2024-A}, \text{SN-9876543 \}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Identify the issuing agency and device characteristics.** - The prefix `003` in the GTIN identifies **GS1** as the issuing agency. - Presence of `(17)` + `(10)` + `(21)` indicates a **sterile, lot-controlled, serialized implant** (Class III). - The expiry date is **2025-01-31**, meaning this device must not be used after that date. **Step 4 — Regulatory implications.** This UDI structure complies with both **FDA (21 CFR 830)** and **EU MDR (Article 27)** requirements for a sterile, single-use, implantable device. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — UDI in Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) **Scenario:** A radiology AI software application is classified as Class II SaMD. The manufacturer releases versions 2.1, 2.2, 3.0, and 3.1. **Step 1 — Determine UDI-DI assignment.** Under IMDRF guidance, the UDI-DI for SaMD is assigned at the **software system level** (not per version). Therefore: $$ \text{UDI-DI}_{\text{SaMD}} = 1 \text{ (assigned to the entire SaMD product)} $$ **Step 2 — Determine UDI-PI assignment.** The UDI-PI for SaMD is the **software version identifier**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{UDI-PI for v2.1} &= \text{SW-v2.1} \\ \text{UDI-PI for v2.2} &= \text{SW-v2.2} \\ \text{UDI-PI for v3.0} &= \text{SW-v3.0} \\ \text{UDI-PI for v3.1} &= \text{SW-v3.1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Special case: Major version change.** If a major version change alters the **intended use** or **indications**, a new UDI-DI may be required. For minor patches (e.g., 3.1.1), only the UDI-PI changes. ## 3.5 UDI Database Submission Lifecycle (GUDID Example) The submission of a UDI-DI to the **FDA GUDID** follows a defined sequence: $$ \text{Step 1: Obtain Company DUNS} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Obtain Issuing Agency ID} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Assign UDI-DI} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Submit GUDID Record} \rightarrow \text{Step 5: FDA Review} \rightarrow \text{Step 6: Publication in GUDID} $$ **Key GUDID attributes submitted (non-exhaustive list of 65+ data fields):** - **Primary:** Brand Name, Model/Version, Catalog Number, UDI-DI - **Regulatory:** Device Class, Premarket Submission Number (510(k), PMA), GMDN Code - **Physical:** Size, Sterilization Method, MRI Compatibility Status - **Lifecycle:** Market Status, Distribution End Date ## 3.6 The UDI Workflow as a Closed-Loop System $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Manufacturer} &\xrightarrow{\text{assigns UDI}} \text{Device + Package} \\ \text{Manufacturer} &\xrightarrow{\text{submits to}} \text{GUDID/EUDAMED} \\ \text{Distributor/Hospital} &\xrightarrow{\text{scans UDI}} \text{Inventory + EHR} \\ \text{Clinician} &\xrightarrow{\text{uses UDI}} \text{Patient Record + Adverse Event Report} \\ \text{Regulator} &\xrightarrow{\text{analyzes UDI data}} \text{Recall + Surveillance} \end{aligned} $$ This closed-loop traceability is the **strategic value proposition of UDI** — it converts a regulatory requirement into a public-health intelligence infrastructure. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UDI System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) The following Mermaid diagram maps the full UDI ecosystem from regulatory mandate to clinical utilization. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Regulatory Authority
FDA EU MDR Health Canada] --> B[Define UDI Rules
21 CFR 830 EU MDR Art 27] B --> C[Accredited Issuing Agencies
GS1 HIBCC ICCBBA] C --> D[Manufacturer Assigns UDI
DI plus PI] D --> E[Affix UDI to Device
AIDC plus HRI] D --> F[Submit to UDI Database
GUDID EUDAMED] E --> G[Distributor Scans UDI
Receiving Inventory] E --> H[Hospital Clinical Use
Implant Surgery] H --> I[EHR Captures UDI
Patient Record] I --> J[Adverse Event Report
MDR MAUDE] F --> K[Regulator Surveillance
Pattern Analysis] J --> K K --> L[Recall Notification
Precise Lot Targeting] L --> M[Hospital and Patient Alert
Targeted Cohort] G --> N[Supply Chain Visibility
Anti Counterfeit] F --> N ``` ## 4.2 UDI Component Decomposition Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR UDI[Complete UDI String] --> UDI_DI[UDI Device Identifier
Static Model Level] UDI --> UDI_PI[UDI Production Identifier
Dynamic Unit Level] UDI_DI --> A1[GTIN 00382903920123
Model HS 100 12mm] UDI_DI --> A2[Assigning Agency Code 003
GS1] UDI_PI --> B1[Lot Number AI 10
LOT 2024 A] UDI_PI --> B2[Serial Number AI 21
SN 9876543] UDI_PI --> B3[Expiry Date AI 17
2025 01 31] UDI_PI --> B4[Manufacture Date AI 11
2024 03 15] UDI --> CarrierA[AIDC Format
DataMatrix Barcode] UDI --> CarrierB[HRI Format
Plain Text] ``` ## 4.3 UDI Assignment Decision Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New Device or Change to Existing] --> Q1{Is it a new device model or version change?} Q1 -- Yes --> NewDI[Assign New UDI DI] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is there a packaging or label change?} Q2 -- Yes --> NewDI Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is there a sterilization or material change?} Q3 -- Yes --> NewDI Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is there a software major version change?} Q4 -- Yes --> NewDI Q4 -- No --> ReuseDI[Reuse Existing UDI DI] NewDI --> AssignPI[Assign New UDI PI per production run] ReuseDI --> AssignPI AssignPI --> Affix[Affix to Device Package Label] Affix --> Submit[Submit to GUDID EUDAMED] Submit --> Done[UDI Compliant] ``` ## 4.4 UDI Database Integration Map (GUDID ↔ EUDAMED ↔ Hospital EHR) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Regulator_Side FDA[FDA GUDID] EU[EU EUDAMED] end subgraph Manufacturer_Side GS1[GS1 Cloud] MFG[Manufacturer ERP MES] end subgraph Hospital_Side EHR[Hospital EHR] INV[Inventory System] SCAN[UDI Scanner] end MFG -- Submit UDI DI --> FDA MFG -- Submit UDI DI --> EU MFG -- Acquire GTIN --> GS1 SCAN -- Read UDI --> INV SCAN -- Read UDI --> EHR FDA -- API Query --> EHR EU -- API Query --> EHR INV -- Stock Recon --> SCAN EHR -- Adverse Event --> MFG ``` ## 4.5 UDI Carrier Format Comparison (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR AIDC[AIDC Machine Readable] --> DM[GS1 DataMatrix 2D] AIDC --> G128[GS1 128 Linear Barcode] AIDC --> HIBCC[HIBCC Code 128] AIDC --> ISBT[ISBT 128 for Tissue] AIDC --> RFID[RFID Tag HF UHF] HRI[HRI Human Readable] --> TXT1[Plain Text: UDI DI: 00382903920123] HRI --> TXT2[Plain Text: EXP 2025 01 31] HRI --> TXT3[Plain Text: LOT ABC123] AIDC --> BOTH[Required Simultaneously on Label] HRI --> BOTH ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Unique Device Identification (UDI). State its two main components with their roles. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Unique Device Identification (UDI) is a system that requires every medical device and its packaging to be marked with a unique numeric or alphanumeric code, enabling unambiguous identification throughout the device lifecycle for traceability, recall, and post-market surveillance. > > **Two Components (1.5 Marks):** > 1. **UDI-DI (Device Identifier):** A static, mandatory identifier that identifies a specific device model or version. It is the "device license plate." > 2. **UDI-PI (Production Identifier):** A conditional, dynamic identifier containing variable production data such as lot number, serial number, expiry date, or manufacture date. --- ### Question 2: List any three accredited UDI Issuing Agencies recognized by the US FDA. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]` **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):** The three US FDA-accredited UDI Issuing Agencies are: 1. **GS1** (e.g., assigns GTIN-based UDIs) 2. **HIBCC** (Health Industry Business Communications Council) 3. **ICCBBA** (primarily for cellular and tissue-based products using ISBT 128) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ CAREFULLY:** > - **Do NOT confuse UDI-DI with UDI-PI** in your answer. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this common mix-up. > - **Do NOT say "UDI is a barcode"** — UDI is a *system*; the barcode is merely the *carrier format*. This distinction is worth **1 mark**. > - **Always mention both AIDC and HRI** when describing UDI carriers. Omitting HRI costs **1 mark**. > - **For software/SaMD questions,** state explicitly that the UDI-PI is the software version number. Generic answers about lot numbers are marked down. > - **Always cite the specific regulation** (e.g., "21 CFR Part 830" for FDA, "EU MDR Article 27" for EU). Vague regulatory references lose **1 mark**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the UDI system in detail. Describe its components, carrier formats, and the role of accredited issuing agencies. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The UDI system was established by the US FDA under **21 CFR Part 830** and adopted globally under **EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27**. It mandates that every medical device carry a unique identifier to enable traceability from manufacturer to patient. **Components of UDI (3 Marks):** $$ \text{UDI} = \text{UDI-DI} + \text{UDI-PI} $$ 1. **UDI-DI (Device Identifier):** - Static, mandatory identifier. - Identifies the device model/version, labeler, and specific configuration. - Same UDI-DI is assigned to all units of the same model. - A new UDI-DI is required when the model, packaging, sterilization method, label warnings, or software major version changes. 2. **UDI-PI (Production Identifier):** - Conditional, variable identifier. - Includes lot/batch number, serial number, expiry date, and/or manufacture date. - Changes for every production run or unit. **Carrier Formats (2 Marks):** The UDI must be presented in two simultaneous formats: - **AIDC (Automatic Identification and Data Capture):** Machine-readable using GS1 DataMatrix, GS1-128, HIBCC DataMatrix, ISBT 128, or RFID. - **HRI (Human Readable Interpretation):** Plain-text alphanumeric readable by humans, displayed in a structured format. **Accredited Issuing Agencies (1 Mark):** - **GS1** — Global Standards body; assigns GTIN-based UDIs. - **HIBCC** — Health Industry Business Communications Council. - **ICCBBA** — For cellular and tissue products using ISBT 128. **Conclusion (0 Marks — optional wrap-up):** The UDI system is the foundation of modern medical device traceability, linking regulators, manufacturers, distributors, hospitals, and patients in a closed-loop surveillance network. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Describe the GUDID database, its purpose, and the lifecycle of UDI submission to GUDID. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]` **Model Solution:** **GUDID Overview (2 Marks):** The **Global Unique Device Identification Database (GUDID)** is the FDA-administered public database that stores key device identification information submitted by manufacturers. It serves as the central registry of all UDI-DIs marketed in the United States. **Purpose of GUDID (2 Marks):** 1. **Public Access:** Patients, clinicians, and researchers can search GUDID to identify devices and their attributes. 2. **Recall Management:** Enables precise, lot/serial-level recalls. 3. **Adverse Event Correlation:** Links MAUDE (Manufacturer and User Facility Device Experience) adverse event reports to specific device versions. 4. **EHR Integration:** Hospitals can auto-populate device information in patient records using GUDID lookups. 5. **Supply Chain Transparency:** Reduces counterfeit entry and supports post-market surveillance. **GUDID Submission Lifecycle (3 Marks):** $$ \text{Step 1: Obtain DUNS} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: Get Issuing Agency ID} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Assign UDI-DI} \rightarrow \text{Step 4: Prepare GUDID Record} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Step 5: Submit via GUDID Web Interface or HL7 SPL XML} $$ $$ \rightarrow \text{Step 6: FDA Validation} \rightarrow \text{Step 7: Publication in Public GUDID} $$ - The GUDID record contains 65+ data elements, including brand name, model, catalog number, device class, GMDN code, sterilization method, MRI compatibility, and premarket submission number (e.g., 510(k), PMA). - Submission is done in **HL7 Structured Product Labeling (SPL)** format using the FDA's Electronic Submissions Gateway. - Once published, the UDI-DI becomes searchable on the **AccessGUDID** public portal. **Key Regulatory Note:** GUDID submission is **mandatory** before marketing a device in the US. Non-compliance can result in FDA warning letters, import alerts, and civil penalties. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compare and contrast the UDI requirements under US FDA and EU MDR frameworks. Highlight at least four key differences. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]` **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** Both the US FDA (under 21 CFR Part 830) and the European Commission (under EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27) mandate UDI, but with nuanced differences in scope, timeline, database, and issuing agency recognition. **Comparative Analysis (5 Marks — distributed across differences):** | **Parameter** | **US FDA** | **EU MDR** | |---------------|------------|------------| | **Legal Basis** | 21 CFR Part 801, 830 | EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27 | | **UDI Database** | GUDID (operational) | EUDAMED (rollout in progress) | | **Issuing Agencies** | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA | GS1, HIBCC, ICCBBA, IFA GmbH, Zollverein | | **Direct Marking** | Required for devices intended to be **reprocessed and reused** | Required for **reusable devices** and certain **Class III implants** | | **Class III Implants** | Serial number required in UDI-PI | Serial number required + **implantation card** mandatory for the patient | | **SaMD (Software)** | UDI-DI at system level; UDI-PI = version | Same logic, with **explicit Annex VI** requirements for UDI assignment | | **Compliance Phasing** | Class III (2014) → Class II (2016) → Class I (2022) | Full application from **May 26, 2021** with transition extensions | | **Implant Card** | Not mandatory | **Mandatory** for all implantable devices (Article 18) | | **EUDAMED UDI Module** | N/A | Mandatory for all devices except custom-made and investigational | **Key Four Differences (1.25 Marks Each):** 1. **Implant Card Requirement:** EU mandates a patient implant card; US does not. 2. **Database:** GUDID is fully operational; EUDAMED rollout is phased. 3. **Issuing Agencies:** EU recognizes additional bodies (IFA, Zollverein). 4. **Reprocessed Devices:** US requires direct marking on devices intended for reprocessing; EU requires it for all reusable devices and specific Class III implants. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** While both systems share the UDI-DI + UDI-PI structure, the EU MDR places a stronger emphasis on patient-facing transparency (implant cards) and on traceability within the EUDAMED ecosystem, whereas the FDA focuses on GUDID-centric post-market surveillance. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain UDI for Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and direct marking requirements for reusable surgical instruments. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]` **Model Solution:** **UDI for SaMD (3.5 Marks):** Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) is regulated under the **IMDRF SaMD Working Group N12** guidance and integrated into UDI frameworks by both FDA and EU MDR. **Key Principles:** 1. **UDI-DI Assignment:** Assigned at the **SaMD system level** (the entire software product, not per version). 2. **UDI-PI Assignment:** The UDI-PI is the **software version identifier** (e.g., "v2.3.1" or build number). 3. **Carrier Format:** Since SaMD has no physical label, the UDI is displayed in the **"About" menu, splash screen, or settings page** of the software, in both AIDC (e.g., QR code) and HRI formats. 4. **Major Version Changes:** If a major version change alters the **intended use, indications, or contraindications**, a new UDI-DI is required. 5. **Modular SaMD:** If SaMD is sold as a **module** (e.g., a cardiac analysis module within a broader platform), each module may require its own UDI-DI, depending on regulatory determination. **Direct Marking of Reusable Devices (3.5 Marks):** **Why Direct Marking is Required:** - Reusable surgical instruments (forceps, retractors, scalpels) undergo **repeated cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization cycles** over their lifetime. - Paper labels and packaging are destroyed after the first use; therefore, the UDI must be **permanently affixed on the device itself** for traceability across all reuse cycles. **Regulatory Requirements:** $$ \text{Direct Marking} = \text{UDI on Device Body} + \text{Readable After Reprocessing} $$ 1. **US FDA (21 CFR 801.45):** Devices intended to be **reprocessed and reused** (e.g., surgical instruments) must bear a **permanent UDI on the device itself**, readable after each cleaning/sterilization cycle. 2. **EU MDR (Article 27, Annex VI):** Class III implantable devices and all reusable devices must carry direct marking. 3. **Marking Technology:** Often **laser-etched** or **etched-on stainless steel** to withstand autoclaving (typically 134°C for 18 minutes). 4. **AIDC vs. HRI in Direct Marking:** Direct marking is often **HRI only** (small text) due to space constraints, but AIDC (DataMatrix) is increasingly used for advanced instruments. 5. **Reprocessing Record Linkage:** Each reprocessing cycle is logged with the UDI, creating a usage history that can be cross-referenced with adverse event reports. **Conclusion:** The UDI system adapts to the unique characteristics of software and reusable devices, ensuring that the same traceability principles apply across all medical device categories, regardless of physical form. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — UDI Module 4** - **UDI Definition:** A unique numeric/alphanumeric code for unambiguous medical device identification, mandated by FDA (21 CFR 830) and EU MDR (Article 27). - **Two Components:** **UDI-DI** (static, device model) + **UDI-PI** (dynamic, production data: lot, serial, expiry, mfg date). - **UDI-DI is version-stable:** New UDI-DI only on model/packaging/sterilization/label/software change. - **UDI-PI is conditional:** Mandatory elements depend on device class (e.g., serial for implants, expiry for sterile devices). - **Two Carrier Formats Required:** **AIDC** (machine-readable barcode/DataMatrix/RFID) AND **HRI** (plain text) — must appear simultaneously. - **Three FDA-Accredited Issuing Agencies:** **GS1**, **HIBCC**, **ICCBBA**. - **EU Additional Agencies:** IFA GmbH, Zollverein. - **GUDID:** US FDA's public UDI database; submission via HL7 SPL format; 65+ data elements. - **EUDAMED:** EU's central database; rollout is phased; includes UDI module, manufacturer module, vigilance module. - **SaMD UDI:** UDI-DI = system level; UDI-PI = software version. Displayed in software UI, not on physical label. - **Direct Marking:** Required for reusable devices and Class III implants (EU); FDA requires for devices intended to be reprocessed and reused. Marking is typically laser-etched to survive sterilization. - **Implant Card (EU MDR Article 18):** Mandatory for all implantable devices in the EU; not required in the US. - **Compliance Phasing (US):** Class III (2014) → Class II (2016) → Class I (2022). - **Compliance Date (EU MDR):** May 26, 2021, with transition extensions for legacy devices. - **Global Harmonization Body:** **IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) — issued the foundational UDI guidance in 2013. - **Key Benefit of UDI:** Converts post-market surveillance from a reactive, manual process into a proactive, data-driven, real-time public health intelligence system.

topics.title: Labelling requirements and post market surveillance; quality: reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT863 - MEDICAL DEVICE REGULATIONS & STANDARDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Labelling requirements and post market surveillance; quality: reliability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Medical Device Labelling — Formal Definition

**Labelling** in the context of medical devices refers to all written, printed, or graphic matter (a) affixed to a device or any of its containers or wrappers, or (b) accompanying a device, and is governed by region-specific regulatory frameworks. Per **21 CFR Part 801 (US FDA)**, labelling includes both the **device label** (affixed to the device/its packaging) and the **Instructions for Use (IFU)** or user manual.

Under **EU MDR 2017/745, Article 2(11) and Article 10(7)**, the term "labelling" explicitly means the information appearing on the device, on the packaging of each unit, or on the packaging of multiple devices, including instructions for use (IFU), intended purpose identification, warnings, precautions, and the **Unique Device Identifier (UDI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners expect students to know that "labelling" is **NOT just the physical sticker on a device** — it is a **comprehensive information package** covering the device, its packaging, the IFU, and electronic labelling (eIFU per EU Regulation 207/2012).

### 1.2 Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) — Formal Definition

**Post-Market Surveillance** is a *systematic and continuous process* carried out by medical device manufacturers to proactively collect, review, and act upon information about their devices that have been placed on the market, with the objective of identifying any need to apply corrective or preventive actions, updating the benefit-risk determination, and ensuring continuous compliance with regulatory requirements.

Per **EU MDR 2017/745, Article 2(60)**, PMS is defined as *"all activities carried out by manufacturers in cooperation with other economic operators to institute and keep up to date a systematic and regular procedure to proactively collect and review experience gained from devices they place on the market, make available on the market, or put into service, for the purpose of identifying any need to immediately apply any necessary corrective or preventive actions."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** PMS is **proactive** (continuous data collection), while **Vigilance** is **reactive** (response to specific incidents). This is a frequent **2-mark question** in KTU exams.

### 1.3 Quality & Reliability — Formal Definitions

- **Quality (per ISO 13485:2016):** The *degree to which a set of inherent characteristics* of a device, process, or system fulfills requirements (defined in Clause 3.6 of ISO 9000:2015 adapted for medical devices).
- **Reliability (per IEEE Std 1413 / IEC 60300):** The *probability* that a device performs its intended function without failure under stated conditions for a specified period of time.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Manufacturer" Mental Model

Imagine an aircraft manufacturer (think Boeing or Airbus):
- **Labelling** = Every switch, lever, and warning sticker inside the cockpit plus the entire 800-page flight manual. The pilot *must* know it before takeoff.
- **PMS** = The continuous monitoring of every flight via black-box data, pilot reports, and in-flight sensors **after** the plane is delivered. Boeing tracks every 737 flying globally, even decades later.
- **Quality** = The build standard (ISO 9001 / AS9100) that every component meets before assembly.
- **Reliability** = The probability that the plane's engine will function for 20,000 flight hours without failure. Measured statistically across the fleet.

Just as an aircraft is monitored for its **entire 30-year service life**, a medical device is monitored from the moment of CE-marking or FDA clearance until **withdrawal from the market**.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Weightage:** Module 4 typically carries 15–20% of the total marks in PEBRT863. KTU frequently tests (a) the **structure of an IFU**, (b) the **PMS Plan content** (Annex III of MDR), (c) **difference between PMSR and PSUR**, and (d) **MTTF/MTBF calculations** for reliability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Labelling Requirements — Deconstructed Framework

#### A. Per US FDA — 21 CFR Part 801

The label of a medical device must contain, at minimum:
1. **Name and place of business** of the manufacturer, packer, or distributor (Section 801.1)
2. **Intended use / Indications for Use** statement
3. **Adequate directions for use** (Section 801.5) — except where exempt per Section 801.109
4. **Adequate warnings and precautions** against any hazardous uses
5. **Sterilization status** if applicable (e.g., "Sterile", "Non-Sterile", "Single Use Only")
6. **Prescription device statement**: *"Caution: Federal law restricts this device to sale by or on the order of a [physician/dentist/etc.]"* (per Section 801.109)
7. **Unique Device Identifier (UDI)** per **21 CFR Part 830** — comprising Device Identifier (DI) + Production Identifier (PI)
8. **Symbol-based labelling** per **ISO 15223-1:2021** (sterile symbol, expiry date, lot number, manufacturer, etc.)

#### B. Per EU MDR 2017/745 — Article 10(7) and Annex I, Chapter III

The information on the label (Section 23 of Annex I) must include:
1. The **name or trade name** of the device
2. All **information strictly necessary** to identify the device, its intended purpose, and the user
3. The **UDI carrier** (in both human-readable and machine-readable, e.g., GS1/HIBC formats)
4. A **warning or precaution** that must be brought to the immediate attention of the user
5. The **manufacturing date** or expiry date (year-month-day)
6. The **sterility state** and sterilization method
7. The **batch code or lot number** or serial number
8. **Single-use indication** if applicable
9. **Storage/transport conditions** (temperature, humidity symbols per ISO 15223-1)
10. Information on the **intended purpose** and any residual risks, contraindications, and foreseeable adverse events

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The MDR requires labelling to be provided in the **official language(s) of the Member State** where the device is made available — unlike the US, which accepts English for export. This is a frequent short-answer question.

#### C. Instructions for Use (IFU) — Detailed Content (per EU MDR Annex I, Section 23.4)

The IFU must contain:
- Device description and intended purpose
- Indications and target patient population
- Contraindications
- Residual risks, warnings, and precautions
- Summary of clinical evaluation and safety/performance (per Article 32)
- Summary of PMS data and PMCF
- Materials in contact with the body (per Section 23.4(u))
- Installation, preparation, use instructions
- Verification before use
- Cleaning, disinfection, sterilization instructions
- Maintenance, calibration, disposal instructions
- Electronic IFU (eIFU) eligibility per **EU Regulation 207/2012** (for software, implants, and certain Class IIa/IIb devices)

### 2.2 Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) — Five-Pillar Architecture

PMS operates through five integrated pillars:

| # | Pillar | Regulatory Anchor | Output Document |
|---|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **PMS Plan** | MDR Art. 84 / Annex III | PMS Plan (mandatory for all classes) |
| 2 | **PMS Report (PMSR)** | MDR Art. 85 | PMSR (Class I) |
| 3 | **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** | MDR Art. 86 | PSUR (Class IIa, IIb, III) |
| 4 | **Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF)** | MDR Art. 10(3), 61, 74, Annex XIV Part B | PMCF Plan + PMCF Evaluation Report |
| 5 | **Vigilance System** | MDR Art. 87-92 / 21 CFR 803 | Vigilance reports (MIR per FDA, FSCA/FSN per EU) |

#### Detailed PMS Documents

**(a) PMS Plan (Annex III):** A documented plan that describes:
- Methods for collecting post-market data (complaints, registries, literature, surveys)
- Indicators and thresholds for re-evaluation of benefit-risk
- Methodology for trend analysis
- Methods for handling complaints and field safety corrective actions
- Protocols for PMCF studies
- Reference to the Risk Management File (ISO 14971)

**(b) PMSR — Post-Market Surveillance Report (Class I only):**
- Annual summary of PMS data
- Conclusion of benefit-risk determination
- Volume of sales, population characteristics
- Submitted to the Competent Authority (CA) **only upon request**

**(c) PSUR — Periodic Safety Update Report (Class IIa, IIb, III):**
- For Class IIa: updated **every 2 years**
- For Class IIb: updated **annually**
- For Class III: updated **annually**
- Contains: summary of data, findings of vigilance, volume of sales, estimate of exposed population, benefit-risk re-evaluation, conclusions of PMCF, and CAPA actions
- For Class III and implantable devices, PSUR must be submitted to the Notified Body for assessment

**(d) PMCF — Post-Market Clinical Follow-up:**
- A continuous process to update the clinical evaluation
- Uses clinical data from real-world use
- Documented in PMCF Plan → PMCF Evaluation Report
- Updates Clinical Evaluation Report (CER) and Risk Management File

**(e) Vigilance Reporting:**
- **Serious Incident** (per MDR Art. 87): any incident that directly or indirectly led, or may lead, to death, serious deterioration in health, or serious public health threat
- **Reportable Serious Incident Timeline:**
  - **EU MDR:** Notification to Competent Authority within **15 days** (or **10 days** for serious public health threat) per Art. 87(3)
  - **US FDA (MDR per 21 CFR 803):** Within **30 calendar days** for most events; **5 work days** for events requiring remedial action or unexpected serious injury; **7 work days** for events that necessitate remedial action
- **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA):** Action taken by manufacturer to reduce risk of death or serious deterioration — includes recall, modification, exchange, destruction, retrofit
- **Field Safety Notice (FSN):** Communication sent to users/caregivers about the FSCA

### 2.3 Quality Management — ISO 13485:2016 Key Clauses

| Clause | Title | Relevance |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| 4.1.6 | Validation of software (used in QMS) | Applies to SaMD (Software as a Medical Device) |
| 5.6 | Management Review | Periodic review of QMS effectiveness |
| 6.3 | Infrastructure & Work Environment | Includes cleanroom design |
| 7.3 | Design and Development | Design controls, DHF, DMR, DHR |
| 7.4 | Purchasing | Supplier controls |
| 7.5 | Production and Service Provision | Includes traceability per UDI |
| 8.2.1 | Feedback | Customer complaints → input to PMS |
| 8.2.2 | Internal Audit | Required annually |
| 8.3 | Control of Nonconforming Product | Includes CAPA |
| 8.5.2 | **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** | Core quality improvement loop |
| 8.5.3 | **Preventive Action** | Eliminating causes of *potential* nonconformities |

### 2.4 Reliability Engineering — Key Concepts

#### Fundamental Definitions

- **Reliability $R(t)$:** Probability that a device performs its intended function from time 0 to time $t$ without failure.
- **Unreliability / Cumulative Distribution Function $F(t)$:** Probability that the device has failed by time $t$.
- **Probability Density of Failure $f(t)$:** Rate of failure density across time.
- **Hazard Rate / Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$:** Instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, given survival to that time.
- **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF):** Expected time to first failure for **non-repairable** devices (e.g., a single-use syringe, a disposable electrode).
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Expected time between consecutive failures for **repairable** systems (e.g., an MRI machine, ventilator).
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Average time to restore a repairable system to operational state.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | Dimensionless | Probability of survival |
| 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = \int_0^t f(u) \, du$ | Dimensionless | Probability of failure by $t$ |
| 3 | Hazard Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | per hour / per cycle | Instantaneous failure rate |
| 4 | MTTF | $\mathrm{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt$ | hours | Non-repairable |
| 5 | MTBF | $\mathrm{MTBF} = \mathrm{MTTF} + \mathrm{MTTR}$ | hours | Repairable |
| 6 | MTBF (alternative) | $\mathrm{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (for constant $\lambda$) | hours | Exponential distribution |
| 7 | MTTF (constant hazard) | $\mathrm{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Exponential distribution |
| 8 | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Memoryless / constant $\lambda$ |
| 9 | Weibull Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | Dimensionless | Wear-out / infant mortality |
| 10 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | Dimensionless | All components must function |
| 11 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | Dimensionless | Redundancy-based design |
| 12 | Effective Failure Rate (series) | $\lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$ | per hour | For independent series components |
| 13 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\mathrm{MTBF}}{\mathrm{MTBF} + \mathrm{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Operational uptime fraction |
| 14 | Design Margin | $\mathrm{DM} = \dfrac{\text{Specification Limit}}{\text{Mean Capability}}$ | Ratio | Process capability indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** The constant $\lambda$ assumption (Exponential model) is **valid only during the useful-life phase** of the **bathtub curve** — NOT during infant mortality or wear-out. Examiners may test this distinction.

### 2.6 The "Bathtub Curve" — Reliability Lifecycle

The reliability of a typical medical device follows a **bathtub-shaped** hazard rate $\lambda(t)$:

1. **Infant Mortality / Burn-in phase (decreasing $\lambda$):** Early failures due to manufacturing defects. Reduced via burn-in screening (e.g., 48-hour burn-in of electronic circuits).
2. **Useful Life phase (constant $\lambda$):** Random failures. Validates the exponential model.
3. **Wear-out phase (increasing $\lambda$):** Failures from aging, fatigue, material degradation. Mitigated via preventive maintenance and design margins.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **MTBF in Hospital Procurement:** Hospital tender documents (e.g., NHS, GPO) specify minimum MTBF (often $\geq$ 10,000 hours) for capital equipment like ventilators and infusion pumps.
- **Reliability Allocation in Design:** A Class III device with 50 components and target $\lambda_{\mathrm{system}} = 10^{-6}$/hr requires $\lambda_{\mathrm{avg}} = 2 \times 10^{-8}$/hr per component — a major driver of design margin and component screening.
- **PMS Linking to Reliability:** Field complaint data feeds back into reliability predictions. If observed $\lambda_{\mathrm{field}} > 2 \times$ predicted $\lambda$, a CAPA is triggered (per ISO 13485 Clause 8.5.2).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reliability Function from the Hazard Rate

**Starting Premise:** By definition, the probability that a device fails in the small interval $(t, t+dt)$, **given** it has survived to time $t$, is $\lambda(t) \, dt$.

**Step 1 — Formulate the Conditional Failure Probability**

The probability of failure in $(t, t+dt)$ AND survival up to $t$ is the product of the survival probability and the conditional failure:

$$
P(\text{fail in } (t, t+dt)) = R(t) \cdot \lambda(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Express Total Unreliability by Integration**

Equivalently, the density $f(t)$ of failure at time $t$ is the survival probability times the hazard:

$$
f(t) = R(t) \cdot \lambda(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Boundary Condition**

At $t=0$, the device is presumably functional (assuming screening removes early failures):

$$
R(0) = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Differential Equation**

Rearranging the density equation:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)}
$$

The negative sign arises because $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ (failure is the *loss* of reliability).

**Step 5 — Solve the Differential Equation**

$$
\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du = -\int_0^t \frac{1}{R(u)} \frac{dR(u)}{du} \, du
$$

$$
\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du = -\ln[R(t)] + \ln[R(0)]
$$

Since $R(0) = 1$ and $\ln[1] = 0$:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du}
$$

**Step 6 — Special Case: Constant Failure Rate**

If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (a constant — useful-life phase assumption):

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute MTTF for Constant Failure Rate**

$$
\mathrm{MTTF} = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

Using integration by parts with $u=t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$:

$$
\mathrm{MTTF} = \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

The boundary term evaluates to $0 - 0 = 0$, and the remaining integral:

$$
\mathrm{MTTF} = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** For a constant hazard rate, $\mathrm{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ and $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. This is the most-tested result in KTU reliability problems.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Series System Reliability

**Problem:** A ventilator consists of 3 critical subsystems with the following constant failure rates:
- Air blower motor: $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Microcontroller PCB: $\lambda_2 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$/hr
- Oxygen sensor: $\lambda_3 = 3 \times 10^{-5}$/hr

Compute (a) the effective system failure rate, (b) the MTTF of the system, (c) the reliability at $t = 5{,}000$ hours.

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Series Failure Rate Summation (assuming independent components)**

$$
\lambda_{\mathrm{system}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3
$$

$$
\lambda_{\mathrm{system}} = 5 \times 10^{-5} + 2 \times 10^{-5} + 3 \times 10^{-5} = 10 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\lambda_{\mathrm{system}} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per hour}
$$

**Step 2 — System MTTF**

$$
\mathrm{MTTF}_{\mathrm{system}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{system}}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 10{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Reliability at $t = 5{,}000$ hours**

$$
R(5000) = e^{-\lambda_{\mathrm{system}} \cdot t} = e^{-(1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 5000}
$$

$$
R(5000) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

**Interpretation:** There is approximately a **60.65%** probability that the ventilator will operate without failure for 5,000 hours.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Series Reliability via Individual $R_i$ (Verification)

Using the same component data at $t=5{,}000$ hours:
- $R_1(5000) = e^{-0.25} \approx 0.7788$
- $R_2(5000) = e^{-0.10} \approx 0.9048$
- $R_3(5000) = e^{-0.15} \approx 0.8607$

**Series Reliability:**

$$
R_s(5000) = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 = 0.7788 \times 0.9048 \times 0.8607
$$

$$
R_s(5000) \approx 0.6065
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-verification:** Both methods yield $R_s \approx 0.6065$, confirming the series failure-rate summation is consistent with the product of reliabilities. This is a powerful KTU-style self-check.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Parallel Redundancy for a Critical Sensor

**Problem:** A Class III implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) uses two identical oxygen sensors in parallel, each with $R_1 = R_2 = 0.95$ at 1 year. Find system reliability.

**Step 1 — Probability of a Single Sensor Failing**

$$
P(\text{fail}_1) = 1 - R_1 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of BOTH Sensors Failing (parallel = system failure)**

$$
P(\text{both fail}) = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel System Reliability**

$$
R_p = 1 - P(\text{both fail}) = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

**Interpretation:** Redundancy increases reliability from 95% to 99.75% — a **20x improvement** in survival probability. This is precisely why safety-critical medical subsystems use redundancy.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — PMSR vs. PSUR vs. PMCF

| Parameter | PMSR | PSUR | PMCF |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Device Class** | Class I | Class IIa, IIb, III | Class IIa, IIb, III (especially III & implantables) |
| **Frequency** | Annual | IIa: 2 yrs / IIb & III: annual | Continuous |
| **Submitted To** | Competent Authority (on request) | Notified Body (for III, implantable) | Notified Body (included in CER update) |
| **Scope** | Data summary + benefit-risk | Includes vigilance findings, CAPA, sales volume | Clinical data only (real-world) |
| **Triggering Regulation** | MDR Art. 85 | MDR Art. 86 | MDR Art. 10(3), 61, 74 |
| **Output Linked To** | Technical File | Risk Management + CER | CER |

### 3.6 CAPA Cycle (Corrective and Preventive Action)

The 8-step loop per **21 CFR 820.100 / ISO 13485:2016 Clause 8.5.2**:

1. **Identify** nonconformity (data sources: complaint, audit, PMS, trend analysis)
2. **Contain** the issue (quarantine, recall if needed)
3. **Investigate** root cause (5-Whys, Ishikawa / Fishbone)
4. **Determine** corrective action(s)
5. **Implement** action
6. **Verify** effectiveness
7. **Document** in CAPA file
8. **Preventive** action for similar potential nonconformities in other products/processes

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PMS Information Flow Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Pre-Market Phase] --> planA[PMS Plan per Annex III]
    planA --> devA1[PMSR for Class I]
    planA --> devA2[PSUR for Class IIa, IIb, III]
    planA --> devA3[PMCF Plan]
    devA3 --> pmcfEval[PMCF Evaluation Report]
    devA2 --> cerUpdate[CER Update per Art 61]
    devA1 --> cerUpdate
    pmcfEval --> cerUpdate
    cerUpdate --> riskFile[Update Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    riskFile --> capaTrig{Triggers CAPA?}
    capaTrig --> yes1[Yes - ISO 13485 Clause 8.5.2]
    capaTrig --> no1[No - Continue Monitoring]
    yes1 --> fscaAction[Field Safety Corrective Action]
    fscaAction --> fsnComm[Field Safety Notice to Users]
    fsnComm --> caReport[Report to Competent Authority]
    fsnComm --> fdaReport[Report to FDA per 21 CFR 803]
    cerUpdate --> annualReview[Annual Management Review Clause 5.6]
    annualReview --> continuePMS[Update PMS Plan]
    continuePMS --> planA
    no1 --> annualReview
```

**Interpretation:** The PMS data flows in a continuous loop — pre-market data inform the initial PMS Plan, which then drives PMSR/PSUR/PMCF activities, which update the Clinical Evaluation Report and Risk Management File, which can trigger CAPA, FSCA, and regulatory reports, which feed back into the next iteration of the PMS Plan.

### 4.2 Labelling Information Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Device Level]
        devLbl[Device Label - Immediate Container]
    end
    subgraph L2[Package Level]
        pkgLbl[Package Label - Outer Carton]
    end
    subgraph L3[Companion Documents]
        ifu[Instructions for Use IFU]
        smp[Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance SSCP]
    end
    subgraph L4[Digital / Electronic]
        eifu[Electronic IFU per EU Reg 207/2012]
        udiDB[UDI Database EUDAMED FDA GUDID]
    end
    devLbl --> udi[UDI Carrier DI + PI]
    pkgLbl --> udi
    ifu --> udi
    udi --> eifu
    udi --> udiDB
    pkgLbl --> ifu
    ifu --> smp
    smp --> eifu
```

**Interpretation:** Labelling is a four-tier hierarchy. The lowest tier is the **device itself** (stickers/markings), tier 2 is the **outer packaging**, tier 3 is the **IFU/SSCP**, and tier 4 is the **electronic / database** layer. UDI serves as the *connective tissue* linking all four layers.

### 4.3 Bathtub Curve — Reliability Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Infant Mortality - Decreasing Lambda]
        p1desc[Early failures from manufacturing defects, component defects, contamination]
        p1action[Action: Burn-in screening, 100 percent inspection]
    end
    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Useful Life - Constant Lambda]
        p2desc[Random failures, exponential distribution valid, R = e to power minus lambda t]
        p2action[Action: Preventive maintenance, redundancy]
    end
    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3: Wear-out - Increasing Lambda]
        p3desc[Aging, fatigue, material degradation, calibration drift]
        p3action[Action: Replace consumables, schedule replacement, end of life signaling]
    end
    Phase1 --> Phase2
    Phase2 --> Phase3
    Phase3 --> retire[End of Service Life Withdrawal from Market]
```

**Interpretation:** The bathtub curve frames the three reliability regimes. Manufacturers design burn-in processes to *eliminate Phase 1 failures* before shipment, engineer redundancy for *Phase 2 random failures*, and plan preventive maintenance or scheduled replacement for *Phase 3 wear-out*.

### 4.4 Vigilance Reporting Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    event{Incident or Near Incident Occurred}
    event --> seriousIs{Serious Incident per MDR Art 87?}
    seriousIs --> noOK[No - Document in Complaint File]
    seriousIs --> yesCheck1[Yes - Classify further]
    yesCheck1 --> spht{Serious Public Health Threat}
    spht --> rep10[Report to CA within 10 days]
    yesCheck1 --> deathCheck{Patient Death or Severe Deterioration}
    deathCheck --> rep15[Report to CA within 15 days]
    deathCheck --> seriousWors{Serious Worsening State of Health}
    seriousWors --> rep15
    noOK --> trendAnal{Trend Detected Increase in Events}
    trendAnal --> trendCA[Trend Report to CA per Art 88]
    trendAnal --> trendDoc[Documented but not reportable individually]
    rep10 --> fscaChk{FSCA Required}
    rep15 --> fscaChk
    trendCA --> fscaChk
    fscaChk --> fscaYes[Yes - Issue FSN + FSCA Notification]
    fscaChk --> fscaNo[No - Continue Monitoring]
    fscaYes --> logComplete[Update Risk Management File, CER, PMS Plan]
    logComplete --> review
    fscaNo --> review[Periodic Management Review]
    trendDoc --> review
```

**Interpretation:** Every event traces a path. Even non-serious complaints must be trend-analyzed. Serious incidents have **hard deadlines** (10 or 15 days in EU; 5/7/30 days in US), and FSCA decisions trigger a chain of FSN issuance and regulatory notifications.

### 4.5 Quality Management System — Clause Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mgmtResp[Management Responsibility Clause 5] --> mgmtRev[Management Review 5.6]
    mgmtRev --> resource[Resource Management Clause 6]
    resource --> productReal[Product Realization Clause 7]
    productReal --> design[Design and Development 7.3]
    design --> purchase[Purchasing 7.4]
    purchase --> production[Production 7.5]
    production --> measure[Measurement Analysis Improvement Clause 8]
    measure --> monitor[Monitoring and Measurement 8.2]
    monitor --> feedback[Feedback 8.2.1]
    feedback --> ncr[Control of Nonconforming Product 8.3]
    ncr --> capa[CAPA 8.5.2]
    capa --> dataAnalysis[Analysis of Data 8.4]
    dataAnalysis --> preventiveAct[Preventive Action 8.5.3]
    preventiveAct --> improve[Improvement 8.5]
    improve --> mgmtRev
    feedback --> pmsPMS[Feeds into PMS System]
    pmsPMS --> mgmtRev
```

**Interpretation:** QMS is a **closed feedback loop** — the Feedback clause (8.2.1) ingests customer complaints, which feed both the QMS CAPA process and the regulatory PMS system. Management Review (5.6) is the apex decision point that uses both streams.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define "labelling" as per the EU MDR 2017/745. List any six essential elements that must appear on a medical device label.**

**Model Answer:**

> Per **Article 2(11) of EU MDR 2017/745**, labelling means the *information appearing on the device itself, on the packaging of each unit or the packaging of multiple devices, and in the accompanying instructions for use (IFU)*.
>
> Six essential elements that must appear on the label (per Annex I, Section 23):
> 1. Name or trade name of the device and the manufacturer
> 2. **Unique Device Identifier (UDI)** carrier — in human-readable + machine-readable form
> 3. Batch/lot/serial number for traceability
> 4. Manufacturing date or expiry date (year-month-day format)
> 5. Sterility state and sterilization method (if applicable)
> 6. Warnings, precautions, and storage/transport conditions

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between PMSR and PSUR. Mention their applicable device classes and submission frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PMSR | PSUR |
|-----------|------|------|
| **Full Form** | Post-Market Surveillance Report | Periodic Safety Update Report |
| **Device Class** | Class I | Class IIa, IIb, III |
| **Frequency** | Annually | IIa: biennially; IIb & III: annually |
| **Submit To** | Competent Authority **on request only** | Notified Body (for Class III, implantables) |
| **Key Content** | PMS data summary + benefit-risk conclusion | Includes vigilance findings, sales volume, PMCF results, CAPA actions |
| **Regulatory Anchor** | MDR Art. 85 | MDR Art. 86 |

> PMSR is a **lighter-weight** report for low-risk Class I devices, while PSUR is a **comprehensive** report mandatory for higher-risk classes that feeds directly into the Notified Body's certification review.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a) Explain the complete structure of a Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Plan as per EU MDR Annex III. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The PMS Plan is a **mandatory documented procedure** that describes how the manufacturer will systematically collect, review, and act upon post-market data for a specific device. It is required for **all device classes** under **Article 84** of EU MDR and is reviewed annually.

**Components of the PMS Plan (per Annex III):**

1. **Title and Scope** — Names of devices covered, including UDI-DI references
2. **Objectives** — Goals such as re-evaluating benefit-risk, detecting previously unknown risks, etc.
3. **Reference Documents** — Links to relevant parts of the technical documentation, especially the Risk Management File (per ISO 14971) and Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)
4. **Post-Market Data Collection Methods:**
   - **Proactive methods:** Patient registries, PMCF studies, physician surveys, post-market observational studies
   - **Reactive methods:** Customer complaints, service reports, vigilance reports
   - **Public sources:** Scientific literature, regulatory authority databases, social media monitoring
